GN.statement: Guinea can partially comply with a Legal and Other Requirements Procedure but faces material gaps because there is no dedicated ESG legislation or consolidated regulatory register to draw from, enforcement is selectively applied and unpredictable, and legal system opacity means that maintaining an accurate, current legal register — a core requirement of this procedure type — is structurally difficult without specialist external legal support.
GN.specific_barrier: Absence of a dedicated ESG legislative framework and selective, discretionary enforcement mean that no stable, comprehensive legal register can be constructed from publicly accessible, reliable official sources; the 2025 mass licence revocations demonstrate that formal legal compliance does not map reliably onto enforcement reality, undermining the foundational assumption of a legal requirements register.
GN.workaround: Engage a Conakry-based mining law firm with government relations capability to maintain a bespoke legal register updated on a rolling basis; supplement with international ESG frameworks (IFC Performance Standards, ICMM) as the de facto compliance baseline where domestic law is silent or unreliable; build a formal review cadence (minimum quarterly) to capture sudden regulatory shifts; document all regulatory interactions and approvals in writing to create an evidence trail given enforcement unpredictability.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading Legal and Other Requirements Procedure standards, supported by a mature, well-documented legislative framework (Mines Safety and Inspection Act, Mining Act 1978, EP Act, WHS Regulations), readily available legal resources, and established regulatory registers, though operators must actively monitor the July 2025 DEMIRS restructure for successor agency changes and EPA referral outcomes specific to Rhodes Ridge.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading Legal and Other Requirements Procedure standards given its mature multi-agency legislative framework (MQSHA, EP Act, RSHQ oversight), though the density and complexity of overlapping agency requirements (RSHQ, DETSI, NRMMRRD) means the legal register must be carefully structured to capture all applicable instruments and concurrent obligations arising from East Weipa cessation, Norman Creek development, and potential Kangwinan expansion simultaneously.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with core Legal and Other Requirements Procedure elements but faces material gaps arising from provincial primacy creating jurisdictional inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, legally unresolved interactions between the Glacier Law amendment (April 2026), Wetlands Law, and lithium brine operations, and a documented history of implementation friction between enacted legislation and on-the-ground compliance — meaning a single consolidated legal register cannot be maintained without province-specific parallel registers.
AR.specific_barrier: The April 2026 Glacier Law amendment transferring protected-area designation authority to provinces — combined with the unresolved Wetlands Law interaction with Puna salares — means that key regulatory boundaries directly affecting operational scope are legally unsettled and subject to conflicting interpretations, making it impossible to produce a complete and legally certain register of applicable requirements for lithium brine operations at this time.
AR.workaround: Maintain separate legal registers for each operative province (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca) updated by locally retained legal counsel; flag Glacier Law and Wetlands Law applicability as 'contested/pending resolution' line items with defined review triggers; adopt IFC Performance Standards as the minimum compliance floor where domestic requirements are ambiguous; establish a formal legal review process triggered by any provincial regulatory action or court ruling on contested instruments; engage with IANIGLA and provincial authorities proactively to document regulatory positions in writing.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most Legal and Other Requirements Procedure requirements given a well-developed legislative infrastructure, but faces a specific gap in that Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026 and the reformed Mining Act regime is not yet fully operationalised, meaning the legal register cannot be finalised for future project phases until those regulations are gazetted; the Supreme Court's October 2023 Impact Assessment Act ruling also leaves federal-provincial assessment interaction partially unresolved.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations are still in draft consultation as of March 2026, meaning the regulatory requirements for mandatory provincial environmental assessment (including BAPE review) for new mining project phases are not yet legally certain and cannot be fully incorporated into a compliant legal register; this is a temporary but material gap for projects in permitting or assessment phases.
CA.workaround: Maintain the legal register with Bill 63 draft regulation requirements flagged as 'anticipated/pending gazette' with a defined update trigger upon finalisation; assign a Quebec regulatory monitoring function (internal counsel or retained firm) to track the 45-day consultation outcome and gazette date; document the ATI requirement (effective May 2024) as an active obligation now; monitor the federal IAA constitutional uncertainty via counsel and flag affected assessment pathways; build schedule contingency for BAPE review timelines into project compliance planning.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with the core structure of a Legal and Other Requirements Procedure but faces significant practical barriers to maintaining an accurate and current legal register, given that over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law have created overlapping and confusing provisions, EIA procedures lack clear national criteria per UNDP assessment, regulatory changes are abrupt and unpredictable particularly around election cycles, and the 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law introduced major ownership and participation obligations that are still being interpreted.
MN.specific_barrier: Over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law 2006 have produced a legislative text of acknowledged internal inconsistency with no consolidated, authoritative version readily accessible, and the EIA framework explicitly lacks operational procedures and clear agency criteria (per UNDP assessment) — meaning the foundational inputs required to build a complete and reliable legal register are not reliably available from official public sources.
MN.workaround: Retain a Ulaanbaatar-based mining law specialist to maintain a custom consolidated legal register updated on a minimum quarterly basis and immediately following any election or parliamentary session; cross-reference all Minerals Law provisions against the amendment history to identify conflicts and document where official interpretation has been sought or obtained; adopt ICMM and IFC Performance Standards as the baseline compliance framework where domestic requirements are ambiguous or absent; build election-cycle review triggers into the legal register update schedule; track Sovereign Wealth Fund Law implementing regulations and strategic deposit designation criteria as a standing priority item.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most Legal and Other Requirements Procedure standards given a well-defined federal and state legislative framework (MSHA, NEPA, EPA, OSHA, state mining laws), but faces procedure-specific gaps arising from unresolved FPIC obligations and Indigenous rights considerations that are not yet fully reflected in the formal legal register, the pre-development status of the Oak Flat project meaning site-specific compliance obligations under MSHA and EPA are not yet fully established, and the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule requiring proactive register updates and gap analysis against current operational controls.
US.specific_barrier: The unresolved Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) obligations and sustained San Carlos Apache Tribe opposition represent a category of 'other requirement' (per international standards including IFC PS7 and ICMM Position Statement on Indigenous Peoples) that is not captured by the formal US federal/state legal register alone but must be incorporated into a complete Legal and Other Requirements Procedure — and the current status of those obligations is legally and socially contested, making compliant register management complex; additionally, the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires explicit mapping to avoid compliance gaps.
US.workaround: Structure the legal register to include a dedicated 'other requirements' section explicitly incorporating IFC Performance Standard 7, ICMM Indigenous Peoples Position Statement, and UN DRIP as applicable voluntary/contractual obligations alongside formal US law; engage tribal liaison and FPIC process documentation as a standing legal register input; map the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities in writing with sign-off from both regulatory specialists; update the register immediately to reflect the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule 50 µg/m³ PEL and conduct a gap analysis against current dust controls; establish a regulatory monitoring function to track Pattern-of-Violations enforcement triggers under MSHA.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a risk management procedure at the operational level, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG legislation and selectively enforced, discretionary regulatory environment means that external regulatory alignment components of the procedure cannot be reliably mapped to a stable legal framework, and sudden licence revocations or policy shifts may invalidate risk registers without warning.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated ESG legislation or guidelines exist against which to benchmark risk controls; selective and discretionary enforcement means regulatory risk itself cannot be quantified with confidence; 2025 mass licence revocations demonstrate that formal compliance status does not translate to predictable protection, making regulatory risk registers inherently unreliable.
GN.workaround: Develop a dual-layer risk register: one layer aligned to company internal standards and ICMM/ISO 31000 as a surrogate framework, and a separate dynamic political and regulatory risk register updated at minimum quarterly with in-country legal counsel input; embed a force majeure and licence continuity scenario into the risk management process; do not rely on regulatory compliance status alone as a control for legal risk.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading risk management procedure requirements, supported by mature WHS and environmental legislation, readily available equipment and trained personnel, though operators must self-manage risk more actively under the 2022 WHS Regulations' performance-based model and should monitor DEMIRS restructuring for any transitional process disruptions.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading risk management procedure requirements under the MQSHA and EP Act framework, though the multi-agency compliance landscape (RSHQ, DETSI, NRMMRRD) requires disciplined cross-regulatory risk mapping, and concurrent obligations across active operations, new development at Norman Creek, and East Weipa rehabilitation demand a well-resourced risk management function.
QLD.specific_barrier: Multi-agency regulatory complexity and identified gaps in RSHQ oversight consistency create risk that the procedure's regulatory compliance components require more intensive coordination than a single-agency framework would demand; limited equipment availability in Queensland may constrain rapid deployment of risk control hardware.
QLD.workaround: Develop a jurisdiction-specific regulatory obligations register that maps each agency's requirements to the risk management procedure's control categories; assign a dedicated compliance coordinator for the RSHQ/DETSI/NRMMRRD interface; build rehabilitation and closure risks into the active risk register rather than treating East Weipa cessation as a standalone workstream.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement risk management procedure requirements at the operational level, but material regulatory uncertainty — particularly the unresolved Wetlands Law interaction with Puna salares, the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment creating provincial conflict of interest, and the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL — means that the regulatory risk component of the procedure cannot be resolved to a stable baseline without ongoing legal monitoring.
AR.specific_barrier: Unresolved legal status of the Wetlands Law interaction with lithium brine operations in salt flat environments means that a key environmental risk control cannot be mapped to a definitive regulatory requirement; provincial primacy with variable capacity and independence prevents consistent risk control benchmarking across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca; extreme altitude and remoteness limit availability of emergency response resources needed to support residual risk controls.
AR.workaround: Engage specialist Argentine environmental and mining legal counsel in each operative province to maintain a live regulatory uncertainty log as an annex to the risk register; treat Glacier Law and Wetlands Law exposure as unresolved regulatory risk items with conservative controls until legal clarity is achieved; build altitude and remoteness-specific emergency response scenarios (including medical evacuation at 4,000m ASL) as named risk scenarios with dedicated control plans; do not assume national minimum standards reflect the actual compliance floor in any given province.

CA.statement: Quebec can implement risk management procedure requirements within a well-established legislative framework, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations, the new ATI requirement for exploration-stage work, the unresolved federal-provincial interface following the Supreme Court's October 2023 Impact Assessment Act ruling, and the complexity of James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations mean that the regulatory compliance mapping component of the procedure contains material uncertainties that cannot yet be resolved.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft and subject to 45-day public consultation as of March 2026, meaning the reformed Mining Act compliance regime is not yet fully operationalized and risk controls cannot be mapped to finalised requirements; the Supreme Court's ruling on the federal Impact Assessment Act leaves the federal-provincial assessment interface legally uncertain, creating an unresolvable gap in regulatory risk mapping for projects subject to both regimes.
CA.workaround: Maintain a regulatory change log as a live annex to the risk register, reviewed at minimum monthly until Bill 63 regulations are finalised; engage Quebec mining law specialists to track federal-provincial assessment interface developments; explicitly identify Bill 63 implementation and IAA constitutional uncertainty as named regulatory risks with defined trigger points for escalation; ensure JBNQA obligations are captured as a discrete risk category with dedicated engagement and consultation controls.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement core operational risk management procedure requirements, but legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law, an EIA framework assessed by UNDP as lacking operational procedures and national criteria, weak enforcement, and election-cycle permitting stalls mean that the regulatory and environmental risk components of the procedure cannot be anchored to a stable or reliably enforced legal baseline.
MN.specific_barrier: UNDP has assessed Mongolia's EIA framework as lacking operational procedures and national criteria, meaning environmental risk controls cannot be mapped to a functioning national standard; over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law create overlapping and contradictory provisions that prevent stable regulatory risk mapping; the 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law's 34% private ownership cap and non-compensated state participation mandate introduce unquantifiable sovereign risk that falls outside standard risk register categories.
MN.workaround: Apply ISO 31000 and ICMM Risk Management Framework as the primary procedural standard in the absence of a reliable national equivalent; maintain a dedicated legislative amendment tracker updated after every parliamentary session; treat sovereign participation risk and election-cycle permitting delays as named risk scenarios with specific contingency controls; engage in-country legal and government relations advisors with standing instructions to provide 48-hour notification of any Minerals Law amendment or regulatory agency directive; consider IFC Performance Standards as the environmental risk control baseline given the weakness of the national EIA framework.

US.statement: The United States can implement industry-leading risk management procedure requirements under a well-established MSHA, OSHA, and EPA regulatory framework, but the pre-development status of the Oak Flat project, unresolved Indigenous rights and FPIC obligations to the San Carlos Apache Tribe, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule requiring proactive compliance investment, and complex federal-state regulatory overlap mean that the procedure's social, environmental, and occupational health risk components require more intensive development than would be typical for an operational site.
US.specific_barrier: Sustained and legally unresolved opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe regarding the sacred significance of Oak Flat constitutes an ongoing human rights and social licence risk that standard risk management frameworks do not typically accommodate; FPIC obligations are not assessed as fully satisfied despite judicial outcomes, creating reputational and legal risk that must be explicitly named in the risk register; the project has no operational compliance track record under MSHA standards, meaning residual risk calibration cannot draw on site-specific incident data.
US.workaround: Explicitly incorporate Free, Prior and Informed Consent risk as a named risk category with a dedicated Indigenous engagement and grievance management control plan developed in consultation with affected communities; engage MSHA compliance specialists to develop a pre-operational compliance readiness assessment against the 2024 Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) before first operational activity; develop a regulatory interface map for the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities as a standalone risk control document; treat social licence risk as a board-level risk item with quarterly review cadence.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement the organisational structure, role definition, and resource allocation requirements of this procedure, but the absence of dedicated ESG legislation means there is no statutory framework against which organisational responsibilities for environmental or social compliance can be anchored, and selective enforcement means that even well-documented responsibility structures offer limited regulatory protection.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated ESG legislation or guidelines exist in Guinea, meaning organisational responsibilities for HSESC cannot be mapped to a coherent statutory compliance framework; the Mining Code and Environmental Code provide only generic anchors, leaving role definitions and accountability structures without enforceable regulatory backing.
GN.workaround: Adapt the procedure to reference international standards (IFC Performance Standards, ICMM Principles) as the primary compliance framework in lieu of absent domestic ESG legislation; document organisational responsibilities against these voluntary standards and build internal accountability structures that do not rely on external regulatory enforcement to function.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading requirements for this procedure, with a mature regulatory framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act, Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations, and established guidance on organisational accountability, supported by ready availability of qualified personnel and training providers.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading requirements for this procedure under the well-established MQSHA and associated legislative framework, which prescribes clear requirements for organisational structure, site senior executive appointments, statutory role holders, and resource obligations at mining operations.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement the core organisational responsibilities framework, but provincial primacy over mining and environmental regulation across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca means that statutory role obligations and resource requirements vary by province, preventing a single unified organisational structure from satisfying all jurisdictional requirements without province-specific adaptation.
AR.specific_barrier: Provincial fragmentation of mining and environmental oversight means there is no single authority to which organisational responsibilities can be uniformly mapped; the role of a nationally-recognised HSESC officer or competent person may carry different statutory standing or recognition across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca.
AR.workaround: Develop province-specific annexes to the procedure that map each organisational role and its responsibilities to the applicable provincial regulatory authority and requirements; engage provincial mining secretariats early to confirm acceptance of nominated responsible officers and ensure accreditation of key role-holders under each province's professional registration system.

CA.statement: Quebec can substantially comply with this procedure's requirements under the Mining Act, Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), and associated frameworks, but incomplete implementation of Bill 63's regulations and evolving ATI obligations for Indigenous consultation mean that the full scope of organisational responsibilities — particularly for environmental and community engagement roles — cannot yet be definitively defined or stabilised.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the reformed organisational compliance obligations for new mining projects under the revised Mining Act are not yet fully operationalised, leaving residual uncertainty about the complete set of statutory roles and responsibilities that must be resourced.
CA.workaround: Establish a regulatory monitoring mechanism within the procedure's governance structure to track Bill 63 regulation finalisation and update responsible officer assignments accordingly; include ATI compliance responsibilities explicitly within designated community and Indigenous relations roles, and resource those roles adequately ahead of regulation finalisation to avoid reactive restructuring.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement the structural elements of this procedure, but over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law, the 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law mandating non-compensated state participation up to 66%, and persistently weak enforcement capacity mean that organisational accountability structures — particularly those involving joint venture governance, state-appointed representatives, and environmental compliance roles — face material legal and operational instability.
MN.specific_barrier: The 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law requires state participation of up to 66% in strategic deposits with no compensation obligation, fundamentally altering the organisational control and accountability structures the procedure relies upon; it is unclear how HSESC responsibilities are allocated when state entities hold majority ownership without having negotiated or accepted corresponding compliance obligations.
MN.workaround: Adapt the procedure to explicitly address governance arrangements under state majority participation scenarios, including defined HSESC accountability assignments regardless of ownership split; engage with the Mongolian Mineral Resources and Petroleum Authority to clarify which organisational roles the state co-owner is expected to fulfil, and document agreed responsibility boundaries in the joint venture or investment agreement.

US.statement: The United States can largely comply with this procedure's requirements given the well-developed MSHA, OSHA, and EPA regulatory frameworks that prescribe specific organisational roles, responsible person designations, and resource obligations, but the pre-development status of the Resolution Copper project, unresolved FPIC obligations toward the San Carlos Apache Tribe, and the complex MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities introduce compliance definition gaps that prevent the procedure from being fully operationalised at this stage.
US.specific_barrier: The project remains in pre-development phase with no established operational compliance track record under MSHA, meaning organisational resource requirements — including the number, qualifications, and placement of statutory safety officers, competent persons, and environmental compliance roles — cannot yet be fully scoped against actual site conditions; additionally, the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities requires explicit delineation in role assignments to avoid accountability gaps.
US.workaround: Develop a phased organisational responsibility matrix that identifies roles required at each project stage (pre-development, construction, operations) and explicitly maps MSHA versus OSHA jurisdictional boundaries to specific position responsibilities; establish a dedicated Indigenous relations and FPIC accountability role within the organisational structure with direct executive reporting lines to manage the ongoing San Carlos Apache engagement risk.

GN.statement: Guinea can comply with core training and competency requirements but faces significant practical barriers including very limited availability of accredited training providers, absence of ESG-specific regulatory frameworks defining competency standards, and weak institutional capacity to verify or recognise third-party qualifications.
GN.specific_barrier: No established network of accredited mining safety or ESG training providers in-country; no national competency framework for mining roles; absence of dedicated ESG legislation means there is no regulatory definition of what constitutes 'adequate' training or competency against which compliance can be measured.
GN.workaround: Deploy expatriate or regionally mobile trainers (e.g., from Senegal, Côte d'Ivoire, or South Africa); develop an operator-defined internal competency framework aligned to ICMM or equivalent international standards to substitute for absent national standards; use e-learning platforms for foundational awareness content; maintain detailed training records to demonstrate good faith compliance given selective enforcement environment.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading training and competency requirements, supported by a mature regulatory framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act and new WHS Regulations, readily available accredited training providers, and a well-established competency verification infrastructure.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading training and competency requirements under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999 and associated competency standards enforced by RSHQ, with a mature training provider market and established statutory certificate of competency regime for key roles.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with most training and competency requirements but faces material barriers including limited availability of specialised mining training providers in remote Puna provinces, inconsistent provincial application of competency standards, and extreme operational conditions at altitude (approx. 4,000m ASL) that require specialised high-altitude safety training not routinely available in-country.
AR.specific_barrier: No nationally standardised mining competency framework consistently applied across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca; limited in-province availability of accredited trainers for specialised topics (e.g., high-altitude health and safety, lithium brine-specific hazards, emergency response at remoteness); provincial primacy means competency recognition may not transfer between jurisdictions.
AR.workaround: Contract specialist training providers from Buenos Aires or internationally (e.g., Chile-based providers familiar with Puna conditions); develop site-specific high-altitude induction and competency modules; establish mutual recognition agreements with provincial authorities; build internal trainer capacity on-site to reduce dependency on external providers in remote locations.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most training and competency requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST) and Mining Act obligations, but faces emerging complexity from Bill 63's incomplete implementing regulations and the need to integrate Indigenous community awareness and cultural competency training consistent with James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations and the new ATI requirement for exploration-stage work.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the full scope of training obligations for the reformed Mining Act regime is not yet legally defined; no finalised regulatory standard specifying competency requirements under the new framework, creating planning uncertainty; cultural competency and Indigenous engagement training requirements for James Bay territory operations are evolving and not yet codified.
CA.workaround: Apply existing LSST and current Mining Act competency standards as the interim baseline; proactively engage MRNF and CNESST to clarify anticipated Bill 63 training obligations before regulations are finalised; integrate Indigenous cultural awareness training aligned to JBNQA obligations using community-developed content; monitor Bill 63 regulation consultations and update training matrices accordingly.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with foundational training and competency requirements under the Labour Law and Minerals Law but faces significant gaps including limited availability of internationally accredited mining training providers, an EIA and compliance framework lacking operational procedures that would otherwise define environmental competency requirements, and persistent enforcement weakness that reduces the practical impetus for maintaining industry-leading competency standards.
MN.specific_barrier: No established in-country network of internationally accredited mining safety or environmental training providers; absence of clear national operational criteria for environmental competency under the EIA framework (per UNDP assessment); legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law makes it difficult to define a stable regulatory competency baseline; limited pool of locally qualified trainers in specialised disciplines (e.g., radiation safety, tailings management, emergency response).
MN.workaround: Import specialist trainers from Australia, South Africa, or Canada for high-risk competency domains; develop a company-defined competency framework anchored to ICMM Practice Guides or equivalent international standards; use Ulaanbaatar-based institutions (e.g., Mongolian University of Science and Technology) for foundational modules; maintain comprehensive training records as evidence of good-faith compliance given weak enforcement environment; build internal trainer pipeline to reduce long-term reliance on imported expertise.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most training and competency requirements given readily available training infrastructure, but the project's pre-development status means MSHA Part 46/Part 48 training programme approvals have not yet been obtained, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) introduces new competency requirements for dust monitoring and control that require proactive investment, and culturally appropriate awareness training for San Carlos Apache Tribe engagement represents an unresolved gap with no established regulatory template.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA-approved site-specific training plan (required under 30 CFR Part 46 or Part 48 depending on mine classification) not yet established given pre-development status at Resolution Copper; no regulatory template or MSHA-approved framework for FPIC-aligned Indigenous cultural awareness training; new silica competency requirements under the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule require task-specific training and competency verification not universally embedded in existing industry training programmes.
US.workaround: Develop and submit MSHA Part 46/48 training plan for regulatory pre-approval during pre-development phase; engage a specialist industrial hygiene training provider to build silica-specific competency modules meeting the 2024 Final Rule requirements; co-develop Indigenous cultural awareness training content with San Carlos Apache Tribe representatives as part of broader FPIC and social licence efforts; establish OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary mapping to ensure no training coverage gaps at surface processing interface.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core supplier and contractor management controls but faces significant structural barriers including the complete absence of ESG-specific legislation against which to pre-qualify suppliers, a weak and unreliable legal system that undermines contractual enforceability, very limited availability of qualified local contractors meeting industry-leading HSE standards, and selective enforcement that creates unpredictability in setting and verifying compliance thresholds.
GN.specific_barrier: No ESG-specific legislative framework exists against which supplier and contractor prequalification criteria can be anchored; local contractor pool is extremely shallow with very limited availability of firms holding verifiable HSE management systems, insurance, or accredited competency records; contractual enforceability is unreliable given weak institutional legal capacity.
GN.workaround: Develop a standalone project-level contractor prequalification standard referencing ICMM, IFC Performance Standards, and Equator Principles as the compliance baseline in lieu of absent national ESG legislation; require all significant contractors to be prequalified against this internal standard regardless of Guinean registration status; mandate that higher-risk specialist contractors be sourced internationally or regionally where local capacity is absent; implement enhanced on-site HSE supervision and audit regimes to compensate for inability to rely on contractor self-certification; include force majeure and early termination provisions in all contracts to address enforcement unpredictability and licence revocation risk under Article 145.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading supplier and contractor management requirements, supported by a mature WHS and environmental regulatory framework, a deep and competitive contractor market with readily available qualified suppliers, and well-established prequalification and chain-of-responsibility obligations under the WHS Act 2020 and Resources Safety legislation, with the only near-term caveat being short-term process uncertainty during the July 2025 DEMIRS restructure.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading supplier and contractor management requirements under the mature MQSHA, Coal Mining Safety and Health Act, and Environmental Protection Act framework, with a well-developed contractor market and established principal contractor obligations, though the multi-agency regulatory environment (RSHQ, DETSI, NRMMRRD) requires careful coordination to ensure contractor obligations are correctly mapped across all applicable regulatory regimes.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement most supplier and contractor management requirements but faces material compliance gaps due to provincial primacy creating inconsistent prequalification and licensing standards across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, an extreme high-altitude operational environment (~4,000m ASL) that severely restricts the pool of contractors with relevant altitude and remote-logistics competency, and significant legal uncertainty around environmental obligations that complicates contractor scope-of-work and liability definition.
AR.specific_barrier: No nationally consistent contractor registration or HSE prequalification standard exists across the three relevant provinces; the pool of contractors with demonstrated competency and equipment for high-altitude (approx. 4,000m ASL), remote Puna operations — including medical emergency response contractors, specialised earthworks, and brine handling — is limited, and the unresolved Wetlands Law and amended Glacier Law create undefined environmental liability exposure that is difficult to pass down contractually.
AR.workaround: Develop a project-level contractor prequalification standard that meets or exceeds the most stringent of the three provincial requirements and apply it uniformly; require altitude acclimatisation and emergency medical protocols as mandatory contractor competency criteria; engage specialist Argentine mining contractors with documented Puna/Altiplano experience; include explicit contractual provisions defining liability boundaries relative to unresolved environmental legislation, and ensure all contractor environmental obligations reference IFC Performance Standards as a backstop where provincial requirements are ambiguous or subject to change.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most supplier and contractor management requirements under a well-developed provincial legal framework, but is currently in a period of regulatory transition as Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete, meaning that contractor environmental and permitting obligations for future project phases cannot be fully defined against a finalised regulatory baseline, and the new ATI requirement and potential Bill 5 intersections with James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations add procedural complexity for contractor scoping in affected territories.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations are not yet finalised as of March 2026, meaning contractor environmental compliance obligations for new project phases cannot be fully specified or contractually locked down against a complete regulatory baseline; contractor scopes involving impact-causing exploration work must now account for the ATI permitting layer effective May 2024, which may not yet be systematically embedded in existing contractor management frameworks.
CA.workaround: Build regulatory change management provisions into all contractor agreements to allow scope and compliance obligation updates as Bill 63 regulations are finalised; monitor the 45-day public consultation process for draft regulations and engage legal counsel to update contractor prequalification criteria promptly upon finalisation; explicitly include ATI obligations in contractor HSE plans and permitting responsibility matrices; for projects in James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement territory, require contractors to demonstrate familiarity with and compliance obligations under the Agreement and Title II of the EQA.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement core supplier and contractor management controls but faces notable barriers from legislative instability across over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law, an EIA framework lacking operational procedures and clear agency processes that complicates defining contractor environmental obligations, limited availability of local contractors with internationally certified HSE management systems, and approval and permitting delays around election periods that create unpredictable timelines affecting contractor mobilisation and scope continuity.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks operational procedures and national criteria per UNDP assessment, meaning contractor environmental compliance obligations cannot be reliably anchored to a clear regulatory standard; the local contractor market has limited firms holding internationally recognised HSE certifications (e.g. ISO 45001, ISO 14001); legislative instability — including the 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law restricting private ownership — creates uncertainty about contractor investment structures and liability that complicates long-term contracting.
MN.workaround: Apply ICMM and IFC Performance Standards as the primary compliance reference for contractor prequalification and environmental obligation definition, supplementing the incomplete Mongolian EIA framework; require key contractors to obtain or work towards ISO 45001 and ISO 14001 certification as a prequalification condition, with a phased timeline for local contractors; include contractual provisions addressing regulatory change risk arising from legislative instability, and build election-period permitting delay contingencies into contractor mobilisation schedules; consider retaining internationally experienced HSE contractors in supervisory or JV arrangements with local firms.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most supplier and contractor management requirements under a well-developed MSHA, OSHA, and EPA regulatory framework, but the pre-development status of the Oak Flat project means site-specific contractor compliance obligations under MSHA and the 2024 Silica Final Rule have not yet been operationally tested, and the project's complex Indigenous rights context — including unresolved FPIC obligations with the San Carlos Apache Tribe — requires contractors to operate within heightened social and human rights compliance requirements that go beyond standard US regulatory minimums.
US.specific_barrier: No operational MSHA compliance track record exists for the Oak Flat site, meaning contractor prequalification criteria cannot yet be calibrated against site-specific hazard profiles; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) requires proactive contractor competency and equipment investment that may not yet be standard in contractor supply chains; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities creates a compliance boundary that must be explicitly managed in contractor scoping; unresolved FPIC obligations with the San Carlos Apache Tribe require contractors to adhere to culturally sensitive engagement and operational protocols beyond standard regulatory requirements.
US.workaround: Develop a project-specific contractor HSE requirements document anchored to MSHA Part 46/Part 48 training standards and the 2024 Silica Final Rule as mandatory prequalification criteria; clearly delineate OSHA and MSHA jurisdictional boundaries in contractor scope documents and site-specific safety plans; include mandatory Indigenous cultural awareness training and compliance with project-level FPIC and community engagement protocols as non-negotiable contractor prequalification and ongoing compliance requirements; conduct pre-mobilisation MSHA compliance readiness assessments with all primary contractors prior to site entry.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core Fitness for Work controls but faces structural gaps: no dedicated occupational health legislation mandating pre-employment or periodic medical surveillance, no accredited occupational physicians or certified drug and alcohol testing laboratories in-country, and the selective enforcement environment means baseline regulatory requirements under the Mining Code are inconsistently applied.
GN.specific_barrier: No accredited occupational health physicians or certified toxicology/drug-and-alcohol testing laboratories commercially available in Guinea; no statutory framework specifically mandating fatigue management, drug and alcohol testing, or psychological fitness assessment in mining operations.
GN.workaround: Engage expatriate or regionally-based occupational health physician (e.g., contracted from Senegal or Côte d'Ivoire) for pre-employment medicals and periodic surveillance; procure UKAS/ISO 17025-accredited point-of-care drug and alcohol testing devices importable in-country; develop company-authored Fitness for Work standard that references ICMM and IFC Performance Standard 2 as the compliance baseline in absence of national regulation; embed fatigue risk management protocols internally rather than relying on regulatory mandate.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading Fitness for Work requirements: the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, Occupational Safety and Health Act 1984, and the 2022 WHS (Mining) Regulations provide a comprehensive statutory framework; accredited occupational physicians, certified drug and alcohol testing providers (AS/NZS 4308 compliant), and fatigue risk management specialists are all readily available in-state.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading Fitness for Work requirements under the Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999, Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, and associated regulations, which explicitly address health surveillance, fatigue, and impairment; accredited occupational health providers and AS/NZS 4308-compliant drug and alcohol testing services are available, though the remote Cape York location may require fly-in medical and testing service arrangements.
QLD.specific_barrier: Remote location of Weipa/Cape York Peninsula means on-site access to accredited occupational health physicians and certified laboratory confirmation testing is not immediate — nearest major laboratory confirmation services are in Cairns or Brisbane, introducing turnaround time delays for confirmatory drug testing.
QLD.workaround: Establish fly-in/fly-out occupational health nurse practitioner presence on-site supported by telehealth-linked occupational physician; use AS/NZS 4308-compliant on-site screening devices with chain-of-custody sample despatch to Cairns-based NATA-accredited laboratory for confirmation; pre-position stand-down and managed return-to-work protocols to accommodate testing turnaround delays.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with most Fitness for Work requirements but faces material gaps at high-altitude Puna operations (~4,000m ASL): no specific national mining regulation mandates altitude acclimatisation as a formal fitness-for-work criterion, accredited occupational physicians in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca with high-altitude medicine competency are scarce, and drug and alcohol testing laboratory infrastructure is concentrated in Buenos Aires and Mendoza rather than proximate to remote Puna project sites.
AR.specific_barrier: No accredited high-altitude occupational medicine specialists commercially available proximate to Puna project sites in Salta/Jujuy/Catamarca; no national regulatory standard specifically addressing altitude acclimatisation fitness criteria for mining workers; nearest ISO 17025-accredited drug and alcohol confirmation laboratories are in Salta city or Buenos Aires, not on-site.
AR.workaround: Develop company Fitness for Work standard incorporating UIAA/ISMM high-altitude medical guidelines and ILO Occupational Health standards as the baseline; contract Buenos Aires-based occupational physician with high-altitude medicine credentials for pre-employment and periodic medicals (with telehealth follow-up); implement validated point-of-care drug and alcohol screening devices on-site with courier chain-of-custody despatch to Salta or Buenos Aires laboratory for confirmation; include mandatory acclimatisation protocol and altitude fitness criteria as contractual conditions of site access.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most Fitness for Work requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines, and federal impairment-related obligations under Bill C-46, but implementation complexity arises from incomplete Bill 63 regulations, James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations requiring Indigenous community engagement on health programs, and the remote northern location of projects increasing logistical lead times for specialist occupational health services.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the full reformed obligations for health surveillance and sector-specific fitness requirements in new mining project phases are not yet legally crystallised; remote northern Quebec locations (e.g., James Bay territory) have limited proximate access to accredited occupational physicians and cannabis/drug impairment testing specialists.
CA.workaround: Apply existing LSST and Mines Regulation requirements as the current compliance baseline; engage Quebec CNESST-accredited occupational health clinic (e.g., Rouyn-Noranda or Val-d'Or based) for pre-employment and periodic medicals; establish fly-in occupational health nurse on-site; use CCSA-aligned drug and alcohol policy incorporating both impairment and safety-sensitive role criteria; monitor Bill 63 draft regulations and engage legal counsel to anticipate transitional obligations; address JBNQA community engagement on occupational health programs proactively.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement core Fitness for Work controls but faces gaps: the Labour Law and Minerals Law provide only generic occupational health obligations with no specific fitness-for-work, fatigue management, or drug and alcohol testing framework for mining; accredited occupational physicians exist in Ulaanbaatar but are scarce at remote mine sites; ISO 17025-accredited drug and alcohol testing laboratories are not confirmed available in-country; and legislative instability risks changing baseline requirements without notice.
MN.specific_barrier: No specific Mongolian mining regulation mandating drug and alcohol testing, fatigue risk management systems, or psychological fitness assessment; no confirmed ISO 17025 or equivalent-accredited drug and alcohol testing laboratory commercially available outside Ulaanbaatar; remote mine site distance from Ulaanbaatar specialist occupational health services (Oyu Tolgoi region is approximately 550km from Ulaanbaatar).
MN.workaround: Develop company Fitness for Work standard referencing IFC Performance Standard 2 and ICMM Health and Safety Framework as the operative compliance baseline; contract Ulaanbaatar-based occupational physician for pre-employment medicals and periodic site visits; deploy validated point-of-care drug and alcohol screening devices on-site; use international courier chain-of-custody for confirmation sample despatch to accredited laboratory in South Korea or Australia; embed fatigue risk management schedule controls contractually in shift rosters independent of regulatory mandate.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most Fitness for Work requirements under MSHA 30 CFR Part 56/57 and OSHA standards, and the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule adds a specific new medical surveillance obligation requiring proactive investment; however, the project's pre-operational status means site-specific medical surveillance programmes, substance abuse testing protocols compliant with DOT/MSHA expectations, and fatigue management systems have not yet been established or tested, and the complex OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities creates a compliance gap risk for which specific fitness-for-work obligations may differ.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA 30 CFR does not mandate a comprehensive drug and alcohol testing programme equivalent to DOT 49 CFR Part 40 for all mine workers — absence of a uniform federal mine-sector drug testing mandate creates policy design ambiguity; 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) medical surveillance obligations require establishment of a medical removal protection programme that has not yet been operationalised at the pre-development Oak Flat site; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities creates dual-standard fitness obligations that must be carefully mapped.
US.workaround: Adopt a company Fitness for Work standard that voluntarily applies DOT 49 CFR Part 40-equivalent drug and alcohol testing across all safety-sensitive roles regardless of MSHA mandate gap; establish MSHA-compliant silica medical surveillance programme with a NIOSH-approved B-reader physician prior to first ore; conduct formal OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional mapping exercise to assign fitness-for-work obligations correctly to each facility type; engage MSHA district office early for pre-operational compliance consultation.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a management improvement planning procedure internally, but the absence of any dedicated ESG legislative framework and the explicitly selective, discretionary enforcement environment mean that improvement plans cannot be anchored to stable regulatory benchmarks, and commitments made in plans may be invalidated by unpredictable political or administrative actions.
GN.specific_barrier: No ESG-specific regulatory instrument exists against which improvement plans can be benchmarked or validated; improvement milestones set under Article 145 of the Mining Code (50% work programme completion within two years) create an externally imposed timeline that may conflict with the operator's own improvement planning cadence, and which cannot be negotiated away through internal process quality.
GN.workaround: Anchor improvement plans to international voluntary standards (ICMM Performance Expectations, IFC Performance Standards) rather than local regulatory benchmarks; build in explicit contingency milestones to satisfy Article 145 work programme thresholds; maintain contemporaneous records of all improvement actions to demonstrate good-faith compliance in the event of discretionary regulatory review or licence revocation challenge.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully implement an industry-leading management improvement planning procedure, supported by a mature legislative framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act, the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations, and the Mining Rehabilitation Fund Act, all of which create structured obligations for corrective and improvement action that align directly with this procedure type.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully implement an industry-leading management improvement planning procedure under the Mineral Resources (Safety) and Health Act 1999, the Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999, and associated RSHQ enforcement frameworks, which explicitly require operators to maintain and action improvement plans, including in response to inspection findings and incident investigations.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a management improvement planning procedure operationally, but the provincial primacy structure means that improvement plan obligations, approval requirements, and enforcement expectations will vary materially across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and the legally unresolved status of the Glacier Law amendment and Wetlands Law interaction with Puna salares means that some improvement plan items may target regulatory targets that shift mid-cycle.
AR.specific_barrier: Provincial inconsistency in mining and environmental enforcement capacity means that improvement plan commitments accepted and monitored in one province may not meet the expectations of regulators in another; no single national authority can validate an improvement plan as meeting Argentina-wide compliance obligations for lithium brine operations at altitude.
AR.workaround: Develop jurisdiction-specific improvement plan annexes for each of the three provinces; engage provincial mining secretariats early to align improvement plan milestones with current provincial enforcement priorities; build regulatory change triggers into the plan to allow revision if the Glacier Law or Wetlands Law position is clarified; apply IFC Performance Standards as a floor standard where provincial requirements are ambiguous.

CA.statement: Quebec can implement a management improvement planning procedure with strong foundational support from the Environmental Quality Act, the Mining Act (as amended by Bill 63), and the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety, but Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning that specific improvement obligations under the reformed regime are not yet fully defined, creating a gap in regulatory anchoring for plans developed during this transition period.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations are in draft consultation phase as of March 2026, meaning the reformed Mining Act compliance obligations that improvement plans must address are not yet legally operative, and plans drafted now may require revision once final regulations are gazetted; the ATI requirement for impact-causing exploration work (effective May 2024) adds a permitting layer that may generate improvement actions without a fully settled compliance baseline.
CA.workaround: Proceed with improvement planning against the current operative legislative baseline while flagging specific sections as subject to revision upon Bill 63 regulation finalisation; build a regulatory monitoring trigger into the procedure so that plans are formally reviewed within 60 days of final regulations being gazetted; consult with legal counsel experienced in James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations where projects intersect with affected territories under Bill 5.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a management improvement planning procedure at the operational level, but over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law, a weak and procedurally incomplete EIA framework, and abrupt regulatory changes around election cycles mean that improvement plans anchored to specific regulatory obligations are at material risk of obsolescence or non-alignment with the current legal position at any given time.
MN.specific_barrier: The Mongolian EIA framework lacks national criteria and clear agency procedures per UNDP assessment, meaning that environmental improvement plan items cannot be referenced to stable, defined regulatory thresholds; the 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law mandating non-compensated state participation at up to 66% in strategic deposits may alter the governance authority and accountability structures within which management improvement plans are approved and actioned.
MN.workaround: Anchor improvement plans to ICMM Performance Expectations and IFC Performance Standards as stable international benchmarks rather than the unstable domestic regulatory baseline; designate a regulatory monitoring role to track Minerals Law amendments and EIA framework updates on a quarterly basis; build governance change triggers into the procedure so that improvement plan ownership and sign-off authority is reviewed if state participation structure changes; maintain a rolling issues log that maps each improvement action to both international standards and the best-available current domestic requirement.

US.statement: The United States can implement a management improvement planning procedure with strong structural support from MSHA enforcement mechanisms (including pattern-of-violations provisions that effectively mandate formal improvement planning), EPA compliance frameworks, and SEC ESG disclosure expectations, but the pre-development status of Resolution Copper at Oak Flat means that site-specific improvement planning cannot yet be fully calibrated to established operational compliance baselines, and the unresolved FPIC and Indigenous rights issues create a category of improvement actions — social licence and community engagement — that fall outside conventional regulatory improvement planning frameworks and require specialised treatment.
US.specific_barrier: The sustained and unresolved opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe means that improvement plans addressing social performance and community engagement at Oak Flat cannot be resolved through standard regulatory compliance actions alone; no federal regulatory framework currently mandates FPIC in a form that a management improvement plan can satisfy, creating a gap between international best practice (IFC PS7, UNDRIP) and the domestic regulatory minimum, which leaves the operator exposed to reputational and legal risk that improvement planning alone cannot close.
US.workaround: Develop a separate Social Performance Improvement Plan track aligned to IFC Performance Standard 7 and UNDRIP principles, distinct from but integrated with the core regulatory improvement plan; engage independent Indigenous rights specialists to advise on meaningful consultation pathways; ensure the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) compliance trajectory is explicitly captured as a time-bound improvement action with assigned ownership and measurement milestones; clarify OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundaries at surface processing facilities as a specific improvement plan workstream.

GN.statement: Guinea can partially comply with a Working at Heights procedure but faces significant gaps: no dedicated working at heights regulation exists, harnesses and lanyards meeting EN/ANSI standards are not commercially available in-country, and no accredited competency assessment body for heights work (e.g., IRATA, PASMA equivalent) operates locally, requiring all certified equipment and trained assessors to be imported.
GN.specific_barrier: No certified fall-protection equipment suppliers or accredited heights competency assessors (IRATA/PASMA equivalent) in Guinea; no dedicated working at heights regulation — reliance on generic Mining Code only; selective enforcement means compliance documentation may provide limited practical protection.
GN.workaround: Procure all certified fall-arrest systems (full-body harnesses, lanyards, anchor devices meeting EN 361/362/353 or ANSI Z359 series) via international supply chain through Conakry or Dakar freight routes; engage an internationally accredited training provider (e.g., IRATA affiliate or equivalent) to deliver in-country competency assessment on a fly-in basis; develop a site-specific working at heights standard that references ILO Safety and Health in Mines Convention (No. 176) and ICMM Good Practice as the compliance benchmark in the absence of specific national regulation; maintain rigorous internal documentation to demonstrate procedural compliance independent of state enforcement.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with an industry-leading Working at Heights procedure: the Work Health and Safety (General) Regulations 2022 (WA) impose explicit fall prevention hierarchy obligations, certified equipment (AS/NZS 1891 series) is readily available, and IRATA-accredited, RIIWHS204E-certified, and scaffold licensing providers are well-established across the Pilbara region.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with an industry-leading Working at Heights procedure under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (Qld) and WHS Regulations 2011, with the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999 providing additional mine-specific overlay; certified equipment (AS/NZS 1891 series) is available, and RIIWHS204E/scaffolding licensing providers operate in and around Weipa, though limited local availability of specialist rope-access contractors at the Cape York site means some contractor mobilisation lead time should be anticipated.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited availability of specialist rope-access (IRATA) and elevated work platform contractors permanently based at Weipa/Cape York — mobilisation from Cairns or further south adds lead time and cost.
QLD.workaround: Establish preferred supplier agreements with Cairns-based certified heights contractors for planned work; maintain a site inventory of certified fall-arrest equipment sufficient for emergency and unplanned tasks; include working-at-heights competency in site induction and verify currency of all contractor tickets prior to mobilisation.

AR.statement: Argentina has applicable working at heights regulation under Resolution SRT 299/11 (fall prevention) and Resolution SRT 905/15 (mandatory harness use), but compliance at high-altitude Puna operations (approx. 4,000m ASL) introduces compounded physiological risk that standard procedures do not address, certified EN/ANSI-compliant equipment availability in Salta/Jujuy is limited compared to Buenos Aires, and no IRATA-accredited rope access operators are based in the northwest Andean region.
AR.specific_barrier: No IRATA-accredited or equivalent rope-access contractors based in Salta, Jujuy, or Catamarca provinces; extreme altitude (4,000m ASL) requires altitude-specific medical fitness-to-work protocols not covered by standard heights procedures; certified fall-protection equipment supply chain concentrated in Buenos Aires, creating remote logistics dependency.
AR.workaround: Supplement Resolution SRT 299/11 compliance with an altitude-specific annex addressing reduced worker capacity, increased fatigue, and impaired judgment at elevation; pre-position certified fall-arrest equipment on-site to avoid supply chain delays; engage Buenos Aires or Chilean (Santiago)-based IRATA-certified contractors for specialist rope-access tasks with planned mobilisation schedules; include altitude medical clearance as a prerequisite for working at heights task assignments.

CA.statement: Quebec can largely comply with working at heights requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST) and the Safety Code for the Construction Industry (S-2.1, r.4), with certified equipment readily available; however, for mine construction and surface work in James Bay/northern Quebec territory, the intersection of Bill 5 obligations, JBNQA Title II requirements, and incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations creates procedural uncertainty for approval of certain elevated work structures (e.g., headframes, conveyors), and remote northern locations can present equipment logistics challenges.
CA.specific_barrier: Incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations (as of March 2026) create uncertainty around permitting for permanent elevated structures at new mine sites; remote James Bay territory locations present logistics challenges for specialised elevated work platform and scaffolding equipment mobilisation.
CA.workaround: Apply the more stringent of LSST/Construction Safety Code and federal Canada Labour Code Part II requirements as the baseline; engage CNESST proactively for pre-approval of elevated work plans at new northern sites given regulatory transition; establish equipment pre-positioning and certified inspector fly-in schedules for remote construction phases; monitor Bill 63 regulation finalisation to update site-specific fall protection plans accordingly.

MN.statement: Mongolia has working at heights provisions within its Labour Safety and Hygiene Law and associated ministerial standards, but enforcement is persistently weak, no IRATA-accredited rope-access operators are based in Mongolia, certified fall-protection equipment meeting EN or ANSI standards is not routinely stocked domestically and must be imported, and the absence of an independent nationally accredited competency assessment body for heights work means operator self-certification is the practical norm.
MN.specific_barrier: No IRATA-accredited or equivalent internationally certified rope-access contractors based in Mongolia; no domestic accredited competency assessment body for working at heights; certified fall-arrest equipment (EN 361/362 or ANSI Z359 compliant) not routinely commercially available in Ulaanbaatar — requires importation from China, South Korea, or Australia.
MN.workaround: Import all certified fall-arrest systems via Ulaanbaatar freight hub (Beijing or Seoul routing) and maintain on-site inventory with documented inspection and retirement schedules; develop and apply a site-specific working at heights standard referencing ICMM Good Practice and ILO Convention No. 176 as the substantive benchmark; engage an internationally accredited training provider (fly-in basis from Australia, South Korea, or Singapore) to deliver and certificate competency assessments; maintain internal rescue-at-height capability given absence of external emergency response resources at remote Oyu Tolgoi or similar sites.

US.statement: The United States has robust working at heights regulation under MSHA 30 CFR Parts 56/57 (surface and underground metal/nonmetal mines) and OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart R/M for construction phases, certified equipment is readily available, and trained contractors are accessible, but the Oak Flat project's pre-development status means site-specific MSHA compliance has not yet been operationally tested, and the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires explicit procedural demarcation to avoid compliance gaps.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing and construction facilities requires explicit documented demarcation — failure to correctly assign jurisdiction can result in overlapping or contradictory fall-protection standards (e.g., MSHA 30 CFR 56.15005 vs OSHA 1926.502 guardrail specifications differ); project pre-development status means no site-specific MSHA inspection history to validate procedural adequacy.
US.workaround: Develop a site-wide jurisdictional map clearly delineating MSHA-regulated areas from OSHA-regulated construction zones prior to first works; where MSHA and OSHA standards conflict, apply the more protective standard and document the rationale; engage MSHA's Metal/Nonmetal district office proactively for pre-operational consultation on fall protection plan adequacy; ensure all contractor JSAs for elevated work explicitly identify the applicable regulatory standard for the work location.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core incident reporting and action tracking internally, but the absence of any dedicated ESG or incident management legislation means there is no external regulatory framework to align the procedure with, and selective/discretionary enforcement creates unpredictability around what incidents must be formally notified to authorities and within what timeframes.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated ESG legislation or prescribed incident notification requirements in Guinea's Mining Code or Environmental Code — no statutory incident classification, notification timeframe, or mandatory investigation standard exists against which to benchmark compliance, and selective enforcement means regulatory expectations are inconsistent.
GN.workaround: Align procedure to international best practice (ICMM, IFC Performance Standards) as the de facto compliance standard in the absence of Guinea-specific requirements; establish internal incident classification, notification, and investigation protocols independently of regulatory minimums; document all incidents rigorously to demonstrate good-faith compliance in the event of discretionary enforcement action; engage proactively with the Ministry of Mines to confirm any informal notification expectations.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a mature, well-defined incident notification and investigation framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and DMIRS (transitioning to successor agencies from July 2025) guidance, and equipment and trained personnel for incident management are readily available.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a comprehensive incident reporting and investigation framework under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, the Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999, and RSHQ oversight, with well-established notification obligations, statutory investigation requirements, and trained workforce available, though the operator must manage coordination across multiple agencies (RSHQ, DETSI, EP Act) to ensure all notification obligations are met.
QLD.specific_barrier: Multi-agency notification obligations (RSHQ, DETSI, EPA under the EP Act) for incidents with environmental consequence create coordination complexity — an incident with both safety and environmental dimensions triggers parallel notification streams with different timeframes and recipients, and independent assessment has identified gaps in RSHQ's incident data collection consistency.
QLD.workaround: Develop a jurisdiction-specific incident notification matrix mapping incident type to each responsible agency, required timeframe, and designated contact; embed this matrix into the procedure as a QLD-specific annex; assign a compliance coordinator role responsible for cross-agency notification and tracking.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement incident and action management procedures operationally, but provincial primacy means incident notification obligations, investigation requirements, and regulatory reporting formats differ materially across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL adds practical complexity to timely incident notification, emergency response, and investigation logistics.
AR.specific_barrier: No single national incident reporting standard for mining — each province (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca) sets its own notification requirements under its own mining and environmental authority, meaning the procedure must be adapted for each provincial context; additionally, remote high-altitude locations in the Puna have limited telecommunications infrastructure, which can delay timely incident notification to provincial authorities.
AR.workaround: Develop province-specific notification annexes for each of Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca identifying the relevant authority, prescribed notification timeframe, required format, and any provincial investigation obligations; invest in satellite or redundant communications at remote Puna sites to ensure notification capability; align investigation standards to IFC Performance Standards as a consistent internal baseline across all provincial contexts.

CA.statement: Quebec has a functional incident reporting and investigation framework under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines, and CNESST oversight, and equipment and trained personnel are readily available, but incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations and unresolved federal-provincial jurisdictional interaction following the 2023 Supreme Court ruling on the Impact Assessment Act create residual uncertainty around environmental incident notification obligations.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the reformed environmental notification and assessment obligations under the updated Mining Act regime are not yet fully operationalized; it is therefore not possible to confirm with certainty that the procedure's environmental incident notification provisions will satisfy the final regulatory requirements once regulations are gazetted.
CA.workaround: Implement procedure against the currently operative LSST and CNESST mining regulations as the confirmed baseline; monitor Bill 63 regulation-making process and build in a scheduled review trigger to update environmental notification provisions once implementing regulations are finalised; engage Quebec's MRNF and MELCCFP proactively to clarify interim expectations for environmental incident notification during the regulatory transition period.

MN.statement: Mongolia has formal incident reporting obligations under the Law on Minerals and associated health and safety regulations administered by the Authority for Fair Competition and Consumer Protection and the Mine Safety Inspectorate, but the EIA framework lacks operational procedures and clear agency processes per UNDP assessment, enforcement is persistently weak and inconsistent, and legislative instability from over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law creates uncertainty about current notification obligations.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks national criteria and clear agency procedures for environmental incident notification (per UNDP assessment), meaning there is no reliable regulatory standard for environmental incident reporting against which to benchmark compliance; combined with weak enforcement and legislative instability, the operator cannot be certain that current notification obligations will remain stable or be consistently applied.
MN.workaround: Align procedure to ICMM and IFC Performance Standards as the internal baseline for incident classification and notification; engage the relevant Mongolian regulatory authority (MRPAM and Mine Safety Inspectorate) directly to confirm current notification timeframes and formats; document all notifications and regulatory interactions comprehensively to demonstrate compliance in the event of inconsistent enforcement; build a procedure review trigger linked to monitoring of Mongolian legislative amendments.

US.statement: The United States has highly prescriptive and well-enforced incident reporting and investigation requirements under MSHA (30 CFR Part 50) and OSHA (29 CFR 1904), and equipment and trained personnel are readily available, but the project is in pre-development phase at Oak Flat meaning operational compliance has not yet been tested, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities must be explicitly mapped to avoid notification gaps.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing and beneficiation facilities — incidents occurring in areas where jurisdictional boundary between MSHA (underground and surface mine workings) and OSHA (processing facilities) is ambiguous require careful classification to ensure the correct agency is notified within the correct timeframe (MSHA requires notification within 15 minutes for fatal/serious accidents under 30 CFR 50.10); misclassification creates pattern-of-violations risk.
US.workaround: Develop a US-specific jurisdictional boundary map for the Resolution Copper site identifying each operational area as MSHA or OSHA jurisdiction; embed this map in the procedure as a US annex with a decision tree for incident notification routing; engage MSHA district office proactively pre-operations to confirm jurisdictional boundaries and notification expectations; ensure incident management personnel are trained on both 30 CFR Part 50 and 29 CFR 1904 requirements.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a document control procedure but faces practical barriers including very limited digital infrastructure, unreliable internet connectivity affecting cloud-based document management systems, and an absence of ESG-specific regulatory requirements that would otherwise mandate particular documentation standards, meaning the procedure must be adapted to function with offline or hybrid systems and paper-based fallbacks.
