GN.specific_barrier: Very limited availability of reliable internet and digital infrastructure in Guinea makes cloud-based or networked electronic document management systems (EDMS) impractical as a primary control mechanism; no ESG-specific regulatory documentation requirements exist to anchor compliance obligations.
GN.workaround: Deploy a hybrid document control system combining offline-capable EDMS software (e.g., locally hosted server with periodic sync) with parallel paper-based master document registers; appoint a dedicated document controller at site; and align document retention and approval workflows to the generic Mining Code and Environmental Code requirements rather than ESG-specific instruments.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading document control standards, with reliable digital infrastructure, readily available document management software and services, a mature regulatory framework under the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations and Environmental Protection Act that defines documentation obligations, and a trained workforce capable of operating enterprise-level EDMS platforms.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading document control standards under a well-established multi-agency regulatory framework (RSHQ, DETSI, EP Act, MQSHA) that explicitly requires documented safety management systems, with trained personnel and readily accessible document management tools, though the complexity of multi-agency documentation obligations across concurrent development and rehabilitation phases at Weipa requires careful procedure scoping to ensure all regulatory document retention and submission requirements are captured.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a document control procedure but faces material complications from provincial primacy over environmental licensing, meaning document control requirements — particularly for EIA-related records, regulatory submissions, and approval correspondence — vary across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca with no single unified standard, and the extreme remote high-altitude operational environment (~4,000m ASL) presents practical challenges for reliable connectivity and document system access at site.
AR.specific_barrier: Provincial regulatory fragmentation means document retention, submission format, and approval documentation requirements differ across the three relevant provinces, with no harmonised national standard applicable; remote Puna location limits reliable connectivity for cloud-based EDMS access.
AR.workaround: Develop a jurisdiction-specific document control matrix mapping retention and submission requirements for each province (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca) separately; implement a locally hosted or satellite-connected EDMS at site with offline capability; designate provincial regulatory liaisons to track evolving documentation requirements as Glacier Law and Wetlands Law obligations are resolved.

CA.statement: Quebec can implement a document control procedure with full access to digital infrastructure and trained personnel, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations as of March 2026 means that the full scope of mandatory documentation and submission requirements under the reformed Mining Act regime is not yet settled, creating a compliance planning gap for future-state obligations including BAPE review documentation and ATI permitting records.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft/consultation phase as of March 2026, meaning the definitive documentary requirements for environmental assessment submissions, BAPE proceedings, and the new ATI permitting layer have not been finalised, preventing a complete procedure that covers all future regulatory document obligations.
CA.workaround: Implement the document control procedure against currently confirmed requirements (existing Mining Act, EQA Title II, JBNQA obligations) and build in a formal regulatory monitoring and procedure review trigger linked to Bill 63 regulation finalisation; maintain flexible document classification schema that can absorb new regulatory categories without wholesale procedure revision.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a document control procedure but faces significant challenges from an unstable legislative environment with over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law creating overlapping and sometimes contradictory documentation obligations, weak and inconsistent regulatory enforcement meaning document standards are unevenly applied, limited availability of sophisticated EDMS tools and trained document control personnel in-country, and approval and permitting processes that stall around elections potentially leaving document submission status unresolved for extended periods.
MN.specific_barrier: Over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law have created legally inconsistent documentation obligations with no consolidated or reliable single reference for required records; limited in-country availability of qualified document control professionals and enterprise EDMS platforms.
MN.workaround: Maintain a consolidated internal regulatory change log tracking all active Minerals Law and EIA-related documentation obligations with version control; use an internationally supported EDMS platform with local offline capability; contract a Ulaanbaatar-based legal and regulatory advisory firm to provide quarterly documentation requirement verification; and design the document control procedure with explicit change-management triggers for legislative amendments.

US.statement: The United States can implement a document control procedure against a well-developed federal and state regulatory framework (MSHA, EPA, NEPA, OSHA), but the project's pre-development status at Oak Flat means site-specific documentation obligations under the Final EIS and MSHA mine plan approval are not yet fully defined, the complex overlapping federal-state-tribal documentation and consultation record requirements — including FPIC-related records and Section 106 National Historic Preservation Act correspondence — require careful procedure design, and the evolving 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule introduces new exposure monitoring documentation obligations requiring proactive integration.
US.specific_barrier: Pre-development status means MSHA mine plan, EPA permit, and tribal consultation documentation requirements are not yet fully crystallised; FPIC and Section 106 consultation record management introduces non-standard document control obligations not typically covered by generic mining document control procedures.
US.workaround: Design the document control procedure with explicit placeholders and workflow pathways for MSHA mine plan documentation, NEPA record-of-decision compliance records, tribal consultation correspondence registers (managed with appropriate cultural sensitivity protocols), and MSHA Silica Rule exposure monitoring records; establish a regulatory development tracking register to incorporate new documentation obligations as pre-development approvals progress.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core communication and consultation activities but faces structural barriers: no ESG-specific legal framework mandates stakeholder engagement standards, selective enforcement creates unpredictable compliance benchmarks, and the combination of political instability and limited institutional transparency undermines meaningful consultation with communities and regulators.
GN.specific_barrier: Absence of any dedicated ESG or stakeholder engagement legislation means there is no defined legal standard for what constitutes compliant consultation; selective enforcement and weak legal institutions mean industry-leading practices (e.g., FPIC-aligned community consultation, grievance mechanisms) have no formal regulatory anchor and may be inconsistently recognised or enforced by authorities.
GN.workaround: Adopt IFC Performance Standard 1 (Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks) and PS 7 (Indigenous Peoples) as the internal compliance benchmark in the absence of Guinea-specific ESG instruments; document all consultation activities against these international standards; engage a locally embedded community liaison officer with French and local language capability; and maintain detailed consultation records to demonstrate good-faith compliance independent of the regulatory environment.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a mature, well-resourced regulatory framework for communication and consultation — including Native Title Act obligations, EPA engagement requirements, and the WA Mineral Resources (Sustainable Development) Act — and Rhodes Ridge's location within a Native Title Determination area means structured consultation with the Nyiyaparli and other Traditional Owners is legally mandated and well-precedented.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a comprehensive multi-agency framework with well-defined community and stakeholder consultation requirements under the Environmental Protection Act 1994, the Aboriginal Cultural Heritage Act, and RSHQ obligations, all of which are applicable to Rio Tinto's Weipa operations; the concurrent rehabilitation obligations at East Weipa and new development at Norman Creek add consultation complexity but do not represent a structural barrier.

AR.statement: Argentina can support communication and consultation activities but faces material gaps: provincial primacy means consultation obligations and standards vary across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca; the legally unresolved Wetlands Law interaction with Puna salares creates uncertainty about which communities and authorities must be consulted; and the Glacier Law amendment generating active legal challenges and protests creates a charged social environment that elevates the risk of consultation processes being contested.
AR.specific_barrier: No nationally uniform free, prior and informed consent (FPIC) or community consultation standard exists for mining in Argentina — each province sets its own requirements, and for Puna lithium brine operations the applicable consultation framework for Indigenous Puna communities (Atacameño, Kolla peoples) under ILO Convention 169 (ratified by Argentina) intersects ambiguously with provincial permitting processes that lack independent oversight.
AR.workaround: Develop jurisdiction-specific consultation plans for each province, anchored to ILO Convention 169 and IFC PS 7 as the minimum standard; engage specialist Argentine Indigenous rights legal counsel to map provincial consultation triggers; establish a tripartite stakeholder register covering provincial regulators, Indigenous community representatives, and civil society; and build in additional consultation timeline buffers given active litigation and protest activity around the Glacier Law amendment.

CA.statement: Quebec has strong foundational consultation requirements under the Environmental Quality Act, the James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement (JBNQA), and the newly expanded BAPE review process under Bill 63, but the incomplete implementing regulations for Bill 63 as of March 2026 mean the full scope of mandatory consultation obligations for new mining projects is not yet operationally defined, creating planning uncertainty.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63's implementing regulations remain in draft consultation as of March 2026, meaning the precise procedural consultation requirements for new mining projects subject to mandatory provincial environmental assessment — including timing, public notice, and Indigenous engagement obligations under Title II of the EQA and JBNQA — cannot yet be fully operationalised or tested, creating a gap between the legal intent and actionable compliance steps.
CA.workaround: Apply the existing EQA Title II and JBNQA consultation requirements as the current baseline; engage Quebec's Ministère de l'Environnement, de la Lutte contre les changements climatiques, de la Faune et des Parcs (MELCCFP) proactively to clarify evolving obligations under Bill 63; maintain continuous dialogue with Cree and Inuit rights-holders under the JBNQA; and design the consultation procedure with sufficient flexibility to incorporate updated regulatory requirements once implementing regulations are finalised.

MN.statement: Mongolia has formal consultation and EIA public participation requirements under the Law on Environmental Impact Assessment and the Minerals Law, but the EIA framework has been assessed by UNDP as lacking operational procedures and clear agency processes; over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law create confusion about current obligations; and election-cycle-driven regulatory stagnation creates unpredictable delays in consultation approvals and responses from government counterparts.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA public participation framework lacks standardised national criteria and operational procedures per UNDP assessment (UNDP Mongolia, 2020 EIA Review), meaning there is no reliable regulatory definition of what constitutes legally sufficient community consultation for mining operations — compliance with industry-leading standards (e.g., IFC PS 1 grievance mechanisms, structured community engagement plans) cannot be benchmarked against a clear domestic legal standard.
MN.workaround: Adopt IFC Performance Standards 1 and 7 as the internal consultation standard; engage a Mongolian-language community liaison team with local cultural competency given herder and nomadic community stakeholder groups; map all relevant soum (district) and aimag (provincial) government counterparts for formal engagement; maintain consultation records sufficient to demonstrate international best practice independently of regulatory confirmation; and build election-cycle awareness into consultation scheduling to avoid critical engagement windows coinciding with political transition periods.

US.statement: The United States has well-developed statutory and regulatory frameworks for communication and consultation — including NEPA public participation, NHPA Section 106 consultation, and MSHA worker communication requirements — but the Resolution Copper project faces a structurally exceptional consultation challenge: sustained, organised opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe regarding Oak Flat's sacred significance, combined with unresolved FPIC obligations, means that technically compliant consultation processes have not achieved social licence and remain subject to legal and reputational challenge.
US.specific_barrier: Free, Prior and Informed Consent (FPIC) obligations to the San Carlos Apache Tribe under UNDRIP (endorsed by the US in 2010) and the substantive requirements of NHPA Section 106 have not been satisfied to the satisfaction of the affected Indigenous community, despite federal judicial outcomes permitting the land exchange; this creates an ongoing structural gap between procedural legal compliance and industry-leading consultation standards that require meaningful consent, not merely notification.
US.workaround: Engage an independent Indigenous engagement specialist to facilitate renewed government-to-government and company-to-community dialogue with the San Carlos Apache Tribe and allied Nations; document all consultation activities against both NHPA Section 106 requirements and IFC PS 7 / FPIC principles; consider establishing an independently chaired Indigenous advisory body to provide ongoing input into operational decisions; and develop a transparent public communication strategy addressing environmental and cultural concerns raised by national conservation organisations to manage reputational risk alongside regulatory compliance.

GN.statement: Guinea has no dedicated chemical or hazardous substances regulatory framework — the Mining Code and Environmental Code provide only generic obligations — meaning industry-leading chemical management standards (SDS systems, chemical registers, storage classification, emergency response protocols) must be self-imposed by the operator without a legislative scaffold, and enforcement selectivity means non-compliant competitors face no consistent penalty pressure.
GN.specific_barrier: No Guinean equivalent to GHS-aligned hazardous substances legislation or dedicated chemical storage and handling regulations; no national SDS (Safety Data Sheet) registry or mandatory format; local availability of compliant chemical storage infrastructure (bunded containment, classified storage units, spill response consumables) is very limited in-country.
GN.workaround: Operator must adopt an international standard (e.g. GHS/UN Recommendations, IFC EHS Guidelines for Hazardous Materials) as the internal compliance baseline in the absence of national law; chemical storage infrastructure and specialist spill response equipment must be imported and maintained on-site; a self-maintained chemical register with SDS library (English/French) must be established independently; training must be delivered internally or via contracted specialists flown in, given absence of local hazmat training providers.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a comprehensive and well-established chemical and hazardous substances regulatory framework under the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, the Dangerous Goods Safety Act 2004 and subordinate regulations, and the national GHS-aligned Hazardous Chemicals Regulations, with readily available equipment, trained personnel, and licensed dangerous goods facilities; the procedural transition risk from DEMIRS restructuring is administrative rather than substantive.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a mature and fully articulated hazardous substances and chemicals framework under the Work Health and Safety Regulation 2011 (Hazardous Chemicals chapters), the Dangerous Goods Safety Management Act 2001, and the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, supported by readily available SDS systems, licensed storage and transport infrastructure, and a trained workforce; limited equipment availability noted in the jurisdiction context relates to operational remote-site logistics rather than regulatory or technical capability gaps.
QLD.specific_barrier: Remote location of Weipa/Cape York Peninsula may limit same-day replenishment of spill response consumables and specialist hazmat contractor response times, which is a logistics rather than compliance framework barrier.
QLD.workaround: Maintain minimum on-site strategic stockpile of spill response and chemical neutralisation consumables calibrated to remoteness; establish pre-contracted hazmat emergency response retainer with a Cairns-based provider; ensure chemical inventory and SDS library is accessible offline given potential connectivity limitations.

AR.statement: Argentina has national-level hazardous substances legislation (Law 24,051 on Hazardous Waste and Decree 831/93, plus the GHS-aligned SDS framework) but provincial primacy means the practical implementation requirements — permits, storage approvals, transport manifests, disposal routes — vary significantly across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and the extreme altitude and remoteness of Puna operations materially constrains access to compliant chemical storage infrastructure and licensed hazardous waste disposal facilities.
AR.specific_barrier: No licensed hazardous waste treatment or disposal facilities within practical operational distance of the Puna altiplano (~4,000m ASL); licensed chemical transport carriers capable of operating at altitude on unpaved access roads are scarce; provincial environmental inspectorates in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca have inconsistent capacity to approve chemical storage facilities or validate waste manifests on predictable timelines.
AR.workaround: Establish a chemical minimisation and substitution programme to reduce on-site hazardous substance inventory to the minimum operationally necessary; pre-approve hazardous waste transport routes and disposal facilities in advance with each relevant provincial authority; contract a certified hazardous waste logistics provider operating in the NOA (Northwest Argentina) region; maintain extended on-site secure storage capacity for hazardous waste pending transport given infrequent vehicle access windows.

CA.statement: Quebec has a robust chemicals and hazardous substances framework under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Hazardous Materials (RHM), the Environment Quality Act, and federal Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act alignment, with readily available equipment and trained workforce; however, incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations and evolving provincial environmental assessment obligations introduce procedural uncertainty for chemical storage facility approvals and spill contingency plan submissions at the project-development stage.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft consultation as of March 2026, meaning the precise provincial environmental approval pathway for new chemical storage or hazardous substance handling facilities at new project sites is not yet fully defined, creating approval timeline uncertainty for infrastructure commissioning.
CA.workaround: Engage MELCCFP (Ministère de l'Environnement, de la Lutte contre les changements climatiques, de la Faune et des Parcs) and CNESST early with pre-submission consultations to clarify applicable approval requirements under the transitional regulatory regime; design chemical storage and spill containment infrastructure to exceed current RHM standards to provide headroom against any regulatory uplift in final Bill 63 regulations; maintain contingency timeline buffer in project schedule for chemical facility approvals.

MN.statement: Mongolia has chemical and hazardous substances obligations spread across the Law on Toxic and Hazardous Chemicals (2006, amended), the Law on Environmental Protection, and sector-specific mining regulations, but the EIA framework lacks clear operational procedures for chemical risk assessment, enforcement is persistently weak and unpredictable, and in-country availability of compliant chemical storage systems, licensed hazardous waste contractors, and qualified industrial hygienists is limited.
MN.specific_barrier: No functioning national GHS-compliant SDS registry or mandatory SDS format enforcement in practice; licensed hazardous waste treatment and disposal facilities with verifiable chain-of-custody documentation are scarce outside Ulaanbaatar; qualified industrial hygienists and chemical safety officers with international certification are not readily available in-country for remote Gobi or steppe operations.
MN.workaround: Adopt IFC EHS Guidelines and GHS standards as the internal compliance baseline given the absence of enforced national equivalents; import a self-contained SDS management system (electronic, offline-capable); establish a direct contractual relationship with one of the limited number of Ulaanbaatar-based hazardous waste contractors with documented disposal chain; engage international chemical safety consultants on rotational basis for compliance auditing and staff training given absence of local certified professionals.

US.statement: The United States has a comprehensive and prescriptive chemical and hazardous substances regulatory framework — OSHA Hazard Communication Standard (29 CFR 1910.1200/1926.59), EPA RCRA hazardous waste requirements, EPCRA Tier II reporting, MSHA-specific chemical exposure standards, and the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) — with readily available equipment and trained personnel nationally, but the project's pre-development status means site-specific chemical inventories, EPCRA thresholds, and spill prevention control and countermeasure (SPCC) plans have not yet been established, and the overlapping OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful procedural delineation.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface chemical handling and processing facilities is not yet formally delineated for the Resolution Copper site, creating potential compliance gaps; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule at 50 µg/m³ PEL requires proactive engineering controls investment that has not yet been operationally tested at this site; EPCRA Section 312 Tier II chemical inventory reporting obligations will be triggered upon commencement of chemical storage above threshold quantities.
US.workaround: Conduct a formal OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional mapping exercise prior to any chemical storage or handling operations commencing on-site to assign clear regulatory ownership to each facility zone; develop SPCC Plan, EPCRA Tier II reporting protocol, and RCRA waste determination documentation during project development phase rather than at commissioning; implement engineering controls for silica and other chemical exposures to the new MSHA 50 µg/m³ standard from initial operations to avoid retrofit costs.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core noise exposure controls in practice, but lacks any dedicated occupational noise regulation — no specific permissible exposure limits (PELs), action levels, or audiometric testing requirements exist under the Mining Code or Environmental Code, requiring the operator to self-impose international standards (e.g. ILO, IFC Performance Standard 2) without a domestic regulatory baseline to anchor compliance.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated occupational noise exposure legislation or PELs in Guinea — no equivalent to 85 dB(A) action level or 8-hour TWA limits enforceable under domestic law; audiometric testing services and calibrated sound level meter/dosimetry equipment are very limited in-country, likely requiring importation from Conakry or overseas.
GN.workaround: Adopt IFC Performance Standard 2 and ILO Code of Practice on Ambient Factors in the Workplace as the compliance reference standard in lieu of absent domestic regulation; procure calibrated dosimetry equipment and establish audiometric baseline testing through contracted occupational health providers sourced from Conakry or regionally (e.g. Dakar); document self-imposed standards in the Environmental and Social Management Plan to satisfy lender and parent-company requirements.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a comprehensive and well-established occupational noise framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994 (and successor WHS-M Regulations), with the Code of Practice for Managing Noise at Mines providing specific guidance, calibrated equipment readily available, and trained occupational hygienists accessible across the Pilbara region.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a mature noise exposure control framework under the Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999, Mineral Resources Safety and Health Act 1999, and associated standards including the Managing Noise and Preventing Hearing Loss at Work Code of Practice, with occupational hygiene services and audiometric testing providers accessible in the Cairns/Weipa corridor, though remote location adds minor logistical burden.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited availability of specialist occupational hygiene contractors and audiometric testing services at Weipa/Cape York Peninsula, requiring periodic mobilisation from Cairns or Brisbane rather than on-demand local access.
QLD.workaround: Establish scheduled audiometric testing rotations using fly-in occupational health providers contracted from Cairns; maintain calibrated dosimetry equipment on-site with trained site personnel conducting routine monitoring between specialist visits.

AR.statement: Argentina has national occupational noise standards under Resolution SRT 295/2003 (setting an 85 dB(A) 8-hour TWA limit and audiometric requirements), but enforcement is primarily provincial and consistency cannot be assumed across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca; extreme altitude (approx. 4,000m ASL) affects noise propagation and worker physiological response, and calibrated dosimetry and audiometric testing services are limited in Puna locations.
AR.specific_barrier: Calibrated noise dosimetry equipment and certified audiometric testing services are not readily available in remote Puna operational areas at 4,000m ASL; the nearest certified occupational health laboratories are in Salta City or Jujuy City, requiring significant worker or equipment mobilisation; provincial enforcement of SRT 295/2003 varies materially across the three relevant provinces.
AR.workaround: Maintain on-site calibrated dosimetry equipment with trained site personnel; contract periodic audiometric testing through certified providers mobilised from Salta or Jujuy on a scheduled rotation; document compliance against SRT 295/2003 as the national minimum and supplement with IFC PS2 where provincial enforcement is demonstrably weaker than the national standard.

CA.statement: Quebec has occupational noise requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST) and the Regulation respecting occupational health and safety (RSST, s.131–140, 85 dB(A) limit), with equipment readily available and training accessible, but Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, creating some uncertainty about future-state obligations; James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations may introduce additional community noise consultation requirements for operations in affected territories.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations are not yet finalised as of March 2026, meaning that future noise-related obligations under the reformed Mining Act regime (including potential community noise standards for Indigenous territories) are not yet fully defined; JBNQA territory operations may require additional noise impact consultation steps not currently standardised.
CA.workaround: Comply with current RSST noise exposure requirements as the confirmed baseline; monitor Bill 63 implementing regulations through the 45-day public consultation process and update the procedure upon finalisation; engage proactively with Cree and Inuit community representatives under JBNQA obligations regarding community noise impacts as a precautionary measure.

MN.statement: Mongolia has occupational noise provisions under the Labour Law and associated Mongolian standards (MNS), and the Law on Occupational Safety and Health sets a general framework, but enforcement is persistently weak, calibrated dosimetry and audiometric testing services are limited outside Ulaanbaatar, and legislative instability creates uncertainty about the current operative standard.
MN.specific_barrier: Calibrated noise dosimetry equipment and certified audiometric (hearing conservation) testing services are not reliably available at remote mine sites in Mongolia — providers are concentrated in Ulaanbaatar; the operative Mongolian noise exposure standard (MNS 4990) has been subject to inconsistent enforcement, and no clear national equivalent to an 85 dB(A) action level with mandatory hearing conservation programme has been consistently applied in practice.
MN.workaround: Procure and maintain calibrated dosimetry equipment on-site; contract audiometric baseline and periodic testing through Ulaanbaatar-based occupational health providers on a scheduled rotation aligned with worker R&R cycles; adopt IFC Performance Standard 2 as the operative compliance reference to ensure consistent application above the uncertain domestic standard; document the self-imposed standard in the ESMP.

US.statement: The United States has a well-defined mining noise framework under 30 CFR Part 62 (MSHA Metal/Nonmetal Noise Standard, 90 dB(A) 8-hour TWA PEL with 85 dB(A) action level triggering a Hearing Conservation Programme), and equipment and audiometric services are readily available nationally, but the project remains pre-operational meaning site-specific noise monitoring baselines are not yet established, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires deliberate management to avoid applying the wrong standard.
US.specific_barrier: Pre-operational status means no site-specific noise exposure baseline data exists for Oak Flat/Resolution Copper, and the OSHA (29 CFR 1910.95, 90 dB(A) PEL) versus MSHA (30 CFR Part 62) jurisdictional boundary at surface processing and support facilities has not yet been formally mapped and tested, creating a compliance gap risk at the interface; note also that MSHA's PEL of 90 dB(A) is less protective than the IFC PS2 85 dB(A) standard, requiring a deliberate decision on which reference to apply.
US.workaround: Conduct pre-operational noise baseline survey during construction phase; formally map OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundaries across all work areas prior to first operations and document in the procedure; adopt 85 dB(A) as the action level across all work areas (aligning with MSHA HCP trigger and IFC PS2) to eliminate the need to manage different thresholds at the jurisdictional interface; establish audiometric testing programme through contracted CAOHC-certified providers available in the Arizona region.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a confined spaces procedure operationally — trained personnel can be sourced and basic atmospheric monitoring equipment imported — but there is no dedicated confined spaces regulation in Guinea's Mining Code or Environmental Code, meaning the procedure must self-justify its standards by reference to ILO Convention 176 or international best practice rather than local law, and selective enforcement creates unpredictability around any inspection-driven compliance demonstration.
GN.specific_barrier: No Guinea-specific confined spaces regulation exists; atmospheric gas detectors (multi-gas monitors for O2, CO, H2S, LEL), supplied-air respirators, and rescue tripods are not reliably available commercially in Conakry and must be imported, creating lead-time and spares supply risk.
GN.workaround: Procedure should be written explicitly to ILO Convention 176 and ICMM safe-systems-of-work standards as the normative reference in the absence of local regulation; all critical confined space equipment (gas monitors, calibration gas, retrieval systems, SCBAs) must be procured internationally and held as site inventory with redundancy; confined space entry competency training must be delivered on-site or in-country by a contracted international provider (e.g., via remote trainer deployment from Dakar or Abidjan); a buddy/standby attendant system must be mandatory given the absence of reliable external emergency rescue services.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading confined spaces requirements under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and the associated Code of Practice for Confined Spaces, with readily available equipment, a mature local training and competency certification market, and established rescue service infrastructure across the Pilbara.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading confined spaces requirements under the Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999, Mineral Resources Safety and Health Act 1999, and the Work Health and Safety Regulation 2011 (Chapter 8 Confined Spaces), supported by the Safe Work Australia Code of Practice for Confined Spaces, with trained personnel and equipment available in the region, noting that limited equipment availability in Weipa/Cape York compared to Brisbane requires forward-stocking of gas monitors and rescue equipment on site.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local commercial availability of confined space rescue equipment and specialist confined space rescue contractors in the Weipa/Cape York Peninsula region compared to south-east Queensland — equipment and specialist rescue contractors must be sourced from Cairns or further south and pre-positioned on site.
QLD.workaround: Pre-position a full confined space rescue kit (retrieval tripods, SCBAs, multi-gas monitors with calibration gas, communications) at site; train and maintain a site-based confined space rescue team to a standard equivalent to industry rescue response contractors; establish a written agreement with the nearest competent external rescue provider (e.g., Cairns-based industrial rescue) for backup; review equipment inventory at each operational phase transition (East Weipa closure activities, Norman Creek ramp-up).

AR.statement: Argentina has applicable confined spaces obligations under Resolution SRT 302/2013 (MTESS) and provincial occupational health and safety frameworks, and trained personnel can be sourced, but the extreme high-altitude operating environment (approx. 4,000m ASL in Puna salares) creates physiological complexity for confined space entry that is not addressed in standard Argentine regulatory guidance, and limited equipment service infrastructure at altitude creates maintenance and calibration risk for atmospheric monitoring devices.
AR.specific_barrier: At approximately 4,000m ASL, standard lower-explosive-limit (LEL) sensor calibration and oxygen sensor baseline readings are affected by reduced atmospheric pressure and partial pressure of oxygen, meaning gas monitor readings require altitude-correction factors not specified in SRT 302/2013 or standard manufacturer defaults; additionally, confined space rescue at altitude is physiologically demanding and specialist high-altitude rescue contractors are not readily available in Salta, Jujuy, or Catamarca provinces.
AR.workaround: Procedure must include an altitude-correction annex for atmospheric monitoring equipment specifying manufacturer-validated correction factors for operation above 3,500m ASL; gas monitors must be calibrated at altitude using certified calibration gas with documented altitude adjustment protocols; confined space entry teams must be altitude-acclimatised (minimum 72-hour acclimatisation protocol before entry work) and medically screened for altitude fitness; a site-based confined space rescue team must be trained and equipped given the absence of proximate specialist rescue contractors; equipment service and sensor replacement must be managed through pre-stocked site inventory given the distance from service centres in Buenos Aires or Mendoza.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with confined spaces requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (RSST, s.300-309 on confined spaces), and CNESST enforcement, with readily available equipment and training providers, but the remote James Bay territory location of the Baie James projects introduces practical barriers to specialist rescue contractor access and equipment logistics that require site-specific adaptation.
CA.specific_barrier: Remote northern Quebec / James Bay territory location means that specialist confined space rescue contractors and emergency medical services are not within standard emergency response distance; the nearest adequately equipped industrial rescue services are typically based in Val-d'Or, Rouyn-Noranda, or Chibougamau, which may be 4–8+ hours from remote site locations by road or require air evacuation.
CA.workaround: Establish and maintain a site-based confined space rescue team trained to CNESST-compliant standard with equipment pre-positioned on site; define the emergency response plan with explicit reference to air medevac capability and documented response time agreements with the nearest hospital with hyperbaric or trauma capability; equipment procurement is straightforward given Quebec-based supply chains; ensure procedure references RSST s.300-309 explicitly as the regulatory foundation and aligns with CNESST's 2023 Confined Spaces Guide as the interpretive reference.

MN.statement: Mongolia has general occupational safety obligations under the Labour Law 1999 and the Law on Safety and Hygiene at Work 2008, but there is no Mongolia-specific confined spaces technical standard or regulation equivalent to Australian, Canadian, or US requirements, meaning the procedure must be self-standardised to international best practice; equipment is available but must largely be sourced from China or through international supply chains with associated lead times, and local confined space rescue and competency training provision is very limited.
MN.specific_barrier: No Mongolia-specific confined spaces regulation or technical standard exists — the Law on Safety and Hygiene at Work 2008 provides only general duty-of-care obligations with no confined space entry permitting, atmospheric testing, or standby attendant requirements specified; certified confined space rescue training providers and industrial rescue contractors are not established in the Mongolian market, requiring international contractor mobilisation or in-country delivery by imported trainers.
MN.workaround: Procedure should reference ILO-OSH 2001 guidelines and ISO 45001 as the normative framework given the absence of local technical standards; all confined space entry equipment (multi-gas monitors, retrieval systems, supplied-air breathing apparatus) must be procured from China (Ulaanbaatar distributors can source) or internationally and held as site stock with documented calibration schedules; confined space entry competency training and rescue team formation must be delivered by an international provider contracted to attend site or by sending personnel to Australia or China for certified training; the procedure must establish its own permit-to-enter system as no regulatory template exists locally.

US.statement: The United States has comprehensive confined spaces regulation under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 (permit-required confined spaces, general industry) and MSHA 30 CFR Part 57 (underground/surface metal and nonmetal mines), with readily available equipment and training, but the Oak Flat (Resolution Copper) project's pre-development status means site-specific MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundaries at the surface processing / underground interface have not yet been operationally defined, and the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) adds an atmospheric monitoring integration requirement for any confined space work involving silica-bearing dust.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at the surface processing facility boundary is not yet operationally defined for this pre-development site, creating potential ambiguity about which confined space standard (29 CFR 1910.146 or 30 CFR 57.22003-22005) governs specific spaces; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule requires that atmospheric monitoring in confined spaces also account for silica exposure, which is not integrated into standard confined space atmospheric monitoring equipment (multi-gas monitors do not measure respirable crystalline silica — separate sampling is required).
US.workaround: Prior to construction and commissioning, conduct a formal jurisdictional boundary determination exercise with MSHA district office to map which confined spaces fall under MSHA 30 CFR Part 57 versus OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 and document this in the procedure scope; develop a dual-standard compliance matrix for spaces near the jurisdictional boundary; integrate silica exposure assessment into confined space pre-entry and re-entry atmospheric monitoring protocols by combining real-time gas monitoring with IOM sampler-based silica sampling for relevant spaces where silica dust generation is plausible; equipment and training providers are readily available across Arizona.

GN.statement: Guinea cannot realistically comply with industry-leading crane and lifting standards due to the near-total absence of certified lifting equipment inspectors, no national crane operator licensing regime, critical shortages of certified lifting gear (rated slings, shackles, load cells) in-country, and no dedicated regulatory framework governing lifting operations beyond generic labour and mining codes.
GN.specific_barrier: No national crane operator certification or third-party lifting equipment inspection body exists in Guinea; certified lifting equipment (rated rigging hardware, load-test facilities, calibrated load indicators) is not commercially available in-country and must be imported; no accredited rigger/dogman certification pathway exists locally.
GN.workaround: Import all certified lifting equipment and rigging hardware through established freight corridors (typically via Conakry port); contract expatriate or regionally certified crane operators and lifting supervisors (sourced from Senegal, Côte d'Ivoire, or internationally); establish a project-internal lifting equipment inspection and certification regime aligned with ISO 9927 / ASME B30 series as a compensating control; apply company-mandated lifting standards as the de facto compliance framework given absence of enforceable national regulation.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading crane and lifting standards, supported by a comprehensive and well-enforced regulatory framework under the Work Health and Safety (General) Regulations 2022, the Occupational Safety and Health Regulations 1996 (transitioning), WorkSafe WA licensing for crane operators and riggers, readily available certified equipment, and a mature pool of licensed crane operators, riggers, and dogmen.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with the substantive requirements of a crane and lifting procedure under the Work Health and Safety Regulation 2011 and associated codes of practice, but remote Weipa/Cape York Peninsula operations face practical constraints including limited local availability of specialised crane types, reduced access to third-party inspection services, and longer mobilisation times for certified lifting personnel and equipment.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited commercial availability of heavy-lift crane types (e.g. crawler cranes, large-capacity mobile cranes) and certified rigging inspection services in the Weipa/Cape York Peninsula region; mobilisation of specialist lifting equipment from Cairns or further south introduces lead times and cost premiums.
QLD.workaround: Pre-mobilise certified lifting equipment and licensed operators (rigger/dogman/crane operator tickets under the High Risk Work Licence framework) well in advance of lift campaigns; establish long-term service agreements with Cairns-based crane and rigging contractors; maintain an on-site inventory of rated rigging hardware to reduce dependency on just-in-time supply; build remote inspection capability through internal competency programs recognised under the WHS Regulation.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with core crane and lifting requirements under national Resolution SRT 319/99 and provincial occupational safety regulations, but high-altitude Puna operations (approx. 4,000m ASL) introduce specific crane performance derating requirements, limited local availability of certified heavy lifting equipment, and a shallow pool of locally certified crane operators and riggers in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca provinces.
AR.specific_barrier: Crane rated capacity derating at 4,000m ASL (reduced air density affects diesel engine output and hydraulic performance, typically requiring 10–20% capacity reduction); limited availability of certified heavy mobile cranes in northwestern Argentina provinces; no established local third-party lifting equipment inspection and certification market equivalent to AS/NZS or ASME standards in remote Puna locations.
AR.workaround: Apply manufacturer-specified altitude derating factors to all crane capacity charts and incorporate into lift plans as a mandatory step; mobilise certified cranes from Salta or Buenos Aires for major lift campaigns with advance planning; engage Chilean crane and rigging contractors via Paso de Jama border crossing as an alternative supply source; implement a project-internal lifting equipment inspection regime aligned with IRAM standards and documented under Decreto 351/79 requirements; train and certify local operators through ART-accredited programs.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with crane and lifting requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Safety Code for the Construction Industry (S-2.1, r.4), and CCQ collective agreements governing crane operator qualifications, but James Bay/northern Quebec project locations face seasonal accessibility constraints, a limited local pool of certified crane operators outside unionised construction frameworks, and evolving regulatory obligations under Bill 63's incomplete implementing regulations.
CA.specific_barrier: In remote James Bay and northern Quebec project locations, mobilisation of CCQ-certified crane operators and specialised lifting equipment is constrained by seasonal road (winter road) access; the incomplete implementing regulations under Bill 63 create some uncertainty about whether future site-specific approvals may impose additional lifting-related requirements not yet defined.
CA.workaround: Plan major lift campaigns to coincide with winter road access seasons or arrange fly-in/fly-out of certified crane operators and equipment via air freight where feasible; establish long-term contractor agreements with Quebec-based unionised crane operators (CCQ ticket holders) well in advance; monitor Bill 63 regulation development and engage with CNESST to confirm that existing S-2.1 framework obligations remain the operative compliance standard during the transitional period.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with basic crane and lifting requirements under the Labour Safety and Hygiene Law and associated Mongolian national standards (MNS series), but faces material gaps including a shallow domestic pool of internationally certified crane operators and riggers, limited in-country availability of modern certified lifting equipment meeting ASME B30 or equivalent standards, and weak third-party inspection and certification infrastructure for lifting gear.
MN.specific_barrier: No established in-country third-party lifting equipment inspection and certification body operating to ISO 9927 or ASME B30 standards; Mongolian crane operator licensing (issued by the Professional Inspection Agency, MASM) does not map directly to international competency frameworks; certified rigging hardware (load-rated slings, shackles, below-the-hook devices) with traceable certification is not reliably available commercially in Ulaanbaatar, and is largely unavailable at remote mine sites.
MN.workaround: Import all certified rigging hardware and below-the-hook lifting devices with full OEM certification documentation; contract internationally certified lifting supervisors and appoint them as the competent person of record for all critical lifts, supplementing locally licensed crane operators; implement a project-internal lifting equipment inspection regime with documented six-monthly and pre-use inspection records; establish a rigger and dogman competency assessment program against an international standard (e.g. LEEA, ASME B30.9) as an internal certification compensating control; pre-position critical spare rigging components at site to reduce dependency on Ulaanbaatar supply.

US.statement: The United States (Arizona/Oak Flat context) can comply with crane and lifting requirements under OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart CC (Cranes and Derricks in Construction) and MSHA 30 CFR Part 56 (surface metal/nonmetal), with certified equipment and licensed operators readily available in Arizona, but the pre-development project status means site-specific lift plans, ground bearing assessments, and MSHA-compliant operational protocols have not yet been established, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful management.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing versus underground/mine areas requires explicit documented demarcation to avoid compliance gaps in crane and lifting oversight; Arizona crane operator certification (under OSHA 1926.1427 requiring operator qualification/certification) must be confirmed as NCCCO or equivalent accredited body certified prior to operations commencing — not yet tested at this pre-development site.
US.workaround: Develop a site-specific jurisdictional boundary map clearly delineating MSHA and OSHA zones of authority for all lifting operations prior to first lift; require all crane operators to hold NCCCO certification (satisfying OSHA 1926.1427) and confirm MSHA applicability for in-pit lifting operations; engage a qualified rigger and signal person per 1926.1428/1926.1419 standards for all lifts; complete ground bearing pressure assessments and crane assembly/disassembly plans as part of site establishment documentation before operations commence.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core hot works controls (permits, fire watches, gas testing) but faces practical gaps in sourcing calibrated gas detection equipment, certified fire suppression equipment, and formally trained hot works supervisors locally, with no dedicated ESG or hot works regulatory framework beyond the generic Mining Code to reference.
GN.specific_barrier: No verified local suppliers of calibrated combustible gas detectors or hot works-specific PPE (e.g., leather welding screens, fire-resistant blankets) in Guinea; no national certification body for hot works competency assessment; absence of dedicated ESG or occupational safety legislation specific to hot works permitting requirements.
GN.workaround: Import calibrated gas detection equipment and fire suppression consumables through established mining supply chains (e.g., via Conakry-based industrial suppliers or regional freight); develop an in-house hot works competency assessment and sign-off process referencing ICMM or IFC Performance Standard benchmarks; embed permit-to-work and fire watch requirements in the site-level safety management system with expatriate or regionally contracted HSE personnel providing supervisory oversight.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading hot works requirements, with comprehensive regulatory coverage under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, successor WHS (Mines) Regulations, and AS 1674 standards, supported by readily available certified equipment, licensed gas testers, and trained hot works competency assessors across the Pilbara region.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading hot works requirements under the Mines Safety and Health Act 1999, MQSHA framework, and relevant Australian Standards (AS 1674), with trained personnel and regulatory infrastructure well-established, though equipment sourcing for remote Weipa/Cape York Peninsula operations requires pre-positioning of gas detection and fire suppression assets due to limited local commercial availability.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local commercial availability of specialist hot works consumables and calibrated gas detection equipment at Weipa/Cape York Peninsula, requiring pre-positioning or freight from Cairns or Brisbane.
QLD.workaround: Maintain a standing inventory of hot works PPE, calibrated gas detectors, and fire suppression equipment on-site at Weipa; establish a scheduled calibration and replenishment cycle via Cairns-based suppliers; ensure hot works competency assessments are conducted during site inductions before personnel arrive at the remote location.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement hot works controls referencing national occupational safety legislation (Resolution SRT 299/2011 and Law 19.587) and provincial requirements, but extreme altitude (~4,000m ASL), remote location, and limited local availability of calibrated gas detection equipment and fire-rated materials in Puna province create practical compliance gaps for industry-leading standards.
AR.specific_barrier: Calibrated combustible and oxygen-deficiency gas detectors suitable for high-altitude operation (requiring altitude-compensated sensors) are not reliably available from local suppliers in Salta, Jujuy, or Catamarca provinces; fire watch personnel with formal hot works competency certification are not locally available in Puna region; altitude affects flame behaviour and oxygen readings, requiring instrument recalibration protocols not addressed in standard Argentine SRT guidance.
AR.workaround: Source altitude-compensated gas detection instruments through Buenos Aires-based industrial suppliers or international import; develop a site-specific altitude correction protocol for gas readings referenced against manufacturer specifications; contract nationally certified welding/hot works supervisors from Buenos Aires or Mendoza during construction phases; include hot works altitude risk as a specific hazard in the site-level Job Hazard Analysis template.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most hot works requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (RSST), and the Quebec Fire Safety Act, with trained personnel and equipment readily available, but incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations and the remote James Bay/northern Quebec operational context introduce procedural uncertainty and logistical challenges for fully consistent compliance.
CA.specific_barrier: Remote northern Quebec mine sites (James Bay territory) face logistical constraints in maintaining continuously calibrated hot works gas detection equipment and sourcing replacement fire suppression consumables without multi-day freight lead times; Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, creating uncertainty about whether future site-specific environmental permit conditions may impose additional hot works restrictions near sensitive northern ecosystems.
CA.workaround: Maintain a minimum 30-day buffer stock of hot works consumables (electrode rods, fire blankets, gas detector calibration gas) at remote site locations; establish a calibration schedule aligned with freight cycles; monitor Bill 63 regulatory gazette notices to identify any site-specific conditions affecting hot works activities near watercourses or sensitive habitats in James Bay territory.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement core hot works permit and fire watch controls under the General Law on Labour Safety and the Fire Safety Law of Mongolia, but persistent weaknesses in enforcement infrastructure, limited local availability of calibrated gas detection equipment outside Ulaanbaatar, and the absence of a nationally recognised hot works competency certification scheme create gaps relative to industry-leading standards.
MN.specific_barrier: No nationally recognised hot works competency certification scheme exists in Mongolia; calibrated combustible gas detectors and fire-rated hot works PPE (leather aprons, welding screens, fire blankets meeting international standards) are not reliably available outside Ulaanbaatar, and remote Gobi Desert mine sites face multi-day freight delays for replacement or recalibration; Mongolia's EIA and site permitting framework lacks operational procedures that would specify hot works controls as a condition of approval.
MN.workaround: Develop and deliver an in-house hot works competency programme aligned with Australian or ICMM benchmarks, delivered by an expatriate or regionally contracted HSE trainer; import calibrated gas detection equipment through Ulaanbaatar-based industrial suppliers and maintain on-site buffer stocks; establish a hot works permit-to-work system within the site safety management system that does not depend on external regulatory prescription, referencing IFC Performance Standards as the compliance benchmark.

US.statement: The United States can comply with industry-leading hot works requirements under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.252 (welding, cutting, and brazing) and MSHA 30 CFR Part 56/57 standards, with readily available equipment and trained personnel nationally, but the pre-development status of the Resolution Copper project (Oak Flat) means site-specific hot works permit-to-work systems, fire prevention plans, and MSHA-required fire suppression provisions have not yet been operationally tested or formally approved at that site.
US.specific_barrier: Resolution Copper project remains in pre-development phase with no operational MSHA-approved fire prevention plan or site-specific hot works permit system yet established; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires formal determination of which standard applies to each work area before hot works procedures can be finalised, and this delineation has not yet been operationally tested at this site.
US.workaround: Develop and submit a site-specific fire prevention plan to MSHA during pre-operational planning phase; formally document the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary map for all work areas and embed hot works permit requirements accordingly; engage MSHA district office proactively during pre-development to align hot works procedure design with local inspector expectations before first works commence.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core manual tasks and ergonomics controls through internal procedures and imported expertise, but no dedicated ergonomics or manual handling legislation exists — compliance relies on generic Labour Code provisions and internal company standards with no specific regulatory benchmark to verify against.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated manual handling or ergonomics legislation in Guinea; generic Mining Code and Labour Code (Code du Travail) contain no specific ergonomic risk assessment requirements, maximum lift weight standards, or musculoskeletal disorder (MSD) prevention obligations; no locally certified ergonomists or occupational hygienists readily available; very limited availability of ergonomic equipment such as mechanical lifting aids, adjustable workstations, or anti-fatigue matting in-country.
GN.workaround: Adopt company internal ergonomics standard aligned to ILO Manual Handling Convention (No. 127) and ISO 11228 series as the de facto compliance benchmark; engage expatriate or remote-access occupational ergonomist for risk assessment and procedure sign-off; procure mechanical lifting aids and ergonomic equipment through international supply chain into Conakry; embed manual tasks training into site induction using translated Pular/Malinké materials given workforce literacy considerations.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading manual tasks and ergonomics requirements, supported by the Occupational Safety and Health Act 1984, the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, and WorkSafe WA's Code of Practice for Manual Tasks, with readily available ergonomic equipment, certified ergonomists, and a mature compliance ecosystem.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading manual tasks and ergonomics requirements under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (Qld), the Mines Safety and Health Act 1999, and the Managing the Risk of Musculoskeletal Disorders Code of Practice 2021, with qualified ergonomists available and relevant equipment accessible, notwithstanding remote logistics at Weipa which require advance procurement planning.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited availability of specialist ergonomics equipment (e.g., patient handling aids, height-adjustable plant interfaces) at remote Weipa/Cape York Peninsula site may require extended lead times from Cairns or Brisbane suppliers.
QLD.workaround: Establish minimum ergonomic equipment inventory on-site with scheduled replenishment; engage remote or fly-in ergonomist for periodic workstation and task assessments; embed manual tasks risk assessment into pre-task planning processes managed by on-site HSEC team.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with the substantive requirements of a manual tasks and ergonomics procedure through national Occupational Risk Law (Ley 24.557) and Resolution SRT 886/15 on ergonomics, but the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL significantly increases musculoskeletal and fatigue-related risk loads, and access to certified ergonomists and specialist lifting equipment in the Puna region is limited.
AR.specific_barrier: No specialist ergonomists or occupational physiotherapists based in the Puna alta operational zone (Salta/Jujuy/Catamarca at ~4,000m ASL); altitude-induced physiological effects (reduced oxygen, accelerated fatigue, impaired cognitive load capacity) are not addressed by standard SRT ergonomics resolution benchmarks, creating a gap between regulatory minimum and industry-leading practice; mechanical lifting aids and anti-vibration equipment face long supply lead times to remote Puna locations.
AR.workaround: Supplement SRT 886/15 compliance framework with altitude-specific manual tasks risk assessment protocol referencing ACGIH and ISO 11228 standards; engage specialist high-altitude occupational health physician to advise on modified weight limits and task duration limits at elevation; procure mechanical lifting aids via Santiago de Chile or Buenos Aires and factor 6–8 week lead times into logistics planning; implement mandatory buddy-system and reduced repetition limits for manual tasks during acclimatisation periods.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with manual tasks and ergonomics requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST) and CNESST regulatory framework, with qualified ergonomists available, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations and the remote James Bay/Eeyou Istchee operational context introduce procedural uncertainty and logistical barriers to consistent compliance.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, creating uncertainty about whether updated LSST ergonomics obligations under the reformed Act (which broadened psychosocial and musculoskeletal risk obligations) are fully in force; remote James Bay location limits access to on-site certified ergonomists (CRHA or OEA-certified) without fly-in resourcing; some specialised ergonomic equipment faces extended delivery times to northern Quebec.
CA.workaround: Apply current LSST and CNESST Programme de prévention requirements as the operative compliance baseline pending Bill 63 regulation finalisation; engage CRHA-certified ergonomist on fly-in/fly-out schedule for workstation assessment and MSD risk identification; establish remote ergonomic assessment capability via video-based task observation tools; pre-position critical ergonomic equipment (lifting assists, anti-vibration mounts) on-site to avoid supply chain delays.

MN.statement: Mongolia can achieve baseline manual tasks and ergonomics compliance through the Labour Law of Mongolia and General Safety Rules for Mining (2018), but the absence of specific ergonomics regulations, persistent enforcement weakness, limited in-country ergonomics expertise, and constrained availability of specialist lifting and ergonomic equipment mean that industry-leading standards require significant supplementation beyond what local regulatory requirements demand.
MN.specific_barrier: No standalone ergonomics or manual handling regulation in Mongolia equivalent to ISO 11228 or ILO Convention 127 requirements; no identifiable pool of certified occupational ergonomists based in-country; mechanical lifting aids (e.g., vacuum lifters, scissor lift tables, ergonomic hand trucks rated to industry-leading standards) have limited availability through Ulaanbaatar suppliers and face long import lead times; enforcement of any ergonomic control programme by the General Agency for Specialised Inspection (GASI) is characterised as persistently weak.
MN.workaround: Establish a company-internal ergonomics standard referencing ISO 11228-1/2/3 and ILO Convention 127 as the operative compliance benchmark independent of local regulatory requirements; engage an expatriate or Beijing/Seoul-based certified ergonomist for initial risk assessment and annual review on fly-in basis; procure lifting aids and ergonomic equipment through Ulaanbaatar international freight hub with 8–12 week lead time buffer; train site HSE officers in manual tasks risk assessment using NIOSH Lifting Equation and REBA/RULA tools to maintain on-site competency between specialist visits.

US.statement: The United States can comply with industry-leading manual tasks and ergonomics requirements through MSHA's Part 50 injury recording obligations, OSHA's General Duty Clause ergonomics enforcement pathway, and ACGIH/NIOSH guidelines, with readily available ergonomists and equipment, but the absence of a specific federal ergonomics standard (withdrawn in 2001) and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at Resolution Copper's surface processing areas create compliance definition ambiguity that requires careful internal standardisation.
US.specific_barrier: No specific federal OSHA ergonomics standard exists (the 2000 Ergonomics Program Standard was repealed by Congress in 2001), meaning ergonomics obligations are enforced only via the General Duty Clause (Section 5(a)(1)) with inconsistent citation precedent; MSHA does not have an equivalent ergonomics-specific standard for underground metallic mining operations, leaving industry-leading ergonomics programmes dependent on voluntary NIOSH/ACGIH guidance rather than enforceable regulatory benchmarks; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities requires dual-standard compliance management to avoid gaps.
US.workaround: Adopt NIOSH Lifting Equation, ACGIH TLV for Hand Activity, and ANSI/HFES 100 as the internal compliance benchmark to fill the regulatory standard gap; document the ergonomics programme as a formal written plan to support General Duty Clause defensibility; clearly map OSHA vs. MSHA jurisdictional boundaries across all work areas and apply the more protective standard where ambiguity exists; engage BCPE-certified ergonomist for programme design and periodic audit; leverage readily available ergonomic equipment supply chains in Arizona.

GN.statement: Guinea can comply with basic aviation operational requirements but faces significant practical barriers including very limited availability of fit-for-purpose charter aircraft, absence of dedicated aviation safety oversight aligned with ICAO Annex 6 industry standards, and unpredictable regulatory enforcement that undermines systematic safety management system (SMS) implementation.
GN.specific_barrier: No verified ICAO-compliant commercial aviation operators routinely servicing remote mining sites in Guinea's interior; Conakry International Airport (IATA: CKY) is the primary hub but in-country charter operators lack documented SMS and maintenance records consistent with IATA ISAGO or equivalent standards; Guinea's ANAC (Autorité Nationale de l'Aviation Civile) has limited institutional capacity to enforce ICAO standards consistently.
GN.workaround: Contract internationally certified charter operators (e.g., South Africa- or Europe-based operators with ISAGO/IS-BAO certification) for all project aviation movements; implement a corporate-level Aviation Service Provider (ASP) pre-qualification and audit process independent of local regulatory reliance; establish a dedicated helipads/airstrip with company-managed safety standards; apply IOGP 590/591 aviation guidelines as the governing standard in lieu of enforceable local equivalents.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading aviation procedure standards, supported by a mature Civil Aviation Safety Authority (CASA) regulatory framework, readily available ICAO-compliant charter operators experienced in remote Pilbara mining operations, and well-established industry norms for helicopter and fixed-wing transport to remote sites.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading aviation procedure standards under CASA regulation, with established charter operators servicing remote Cape York Peninsula and Weipa operations, though the remote location introduces practical considerations around emergency aviation response times and weather-related operational windows in the wet season.

AR.statement: Argentina's Puna operations (approx. 4,000m ASL) can comply with core aviation requirements under ANAC (Administración Nacional de Aviación Civil) oversight, but high-altitude operations introduce non-standard performance requirements for aircraft and crew that limit the pool of compliant operators, and limited aviation infrastructure at remote Puna sites creates practical gaps in industry-leading SMS implementation.
AR.specific_barrier: High-altitude operations at approximately 4,000m ASL in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca require aircraft with certified high-density-altitude performance (e.g., turbine-powered helicopters with documented high-altitude approval); limited number of Argentine charter operators with verified high-altitude Puna experience and IOGP 590/591-compliant SMS; no commercial helipad infrastructure at most remote lithium brine sites; nearest HEMS (Helicopter Emergency Medical Service) capable of sustained high-altitude operations is typically Salta city, representing significant response time.
AR.workaround: Pre-qualify only operators with documented high-altitude certification and SMS aligned to IOGP 590/591 or IS-BAO; commission site-specific helicopter performance assessments for each landing area; establish dedicated helipads with company-managed obstacle clearance and obstacle lighting; consider contracting Chilean or Bolivian operators with Altiplano experience if Argentine operator pool is insufficient; mandate crew high-altitude physiological training.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most aviation procedure requirements under Transport Canada's mature Civil Aviation regulatory framework (CARs), with readily available certified operators, but remote James Bay and Northern Quebec project locations introduce practical challenges including limited instrument approach infrastructure at remote airstrips, harsh winter operational conditions, and procedural complexity arising from James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement (JBNQA) obligations that may govern airstrip development or helicopter landing area approvals on affected lands.
CA.specific_barrier: Remote northern Quebec project sites may lack certified instrument approach procedures at nearby aerodromes, limiting operations in low-visibility conditions; airstrip or helipad construction on JBNQA Category I, II, or III lands requires consultation and potentially approval under JBNQA governance structures, adding timeline uncertainty; winter white-out and blowing snow conditions create periods where visual flight rules (VFR) operations are unsafe and IFR alternatives are infrastructure-constrained.
CA.workaround: Engage JBNQA governance bodies (Grand Council of the Crees / Cree Nation Government) early regarding any aviation infrastructure development; contract Transport Canada-compliant operators with documented northern/Arctic operations experience; implement a company-level weather minima policy more conservative than regulatory minimums; invest in site weather monitoring and real-time reporting; consider establishing a dedicated company aerodrome with NAV CANADA or private instrument approach procedures where operationally justified.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with basic aviation operational requirements under the Civil Aviation Authority of Mongolia (CAAM) framework, but the limited pool of locally available operators meeting international SMS and airworthiness standards, weak regulatory enforcement capacity, and the extreme continental climate (including severe winter icing and dust conditions) create material gaps relative to an industry-leading aviation procedure standard.
MN.specific_barrier: CAAM oversight capacity is limited and does not consistently enforce ICAO Annex 6 operational standards; locally based charter operators serving remote mining regions (South Gobi, Ömnögovi) have variable maintenance documentation and SMS maturity; no verified IATA ISAGO-registered ground handling at most remote Mongolian aerodromes; extreme winter temperatures (down to -40°C) require cold-weather operational procedures and aircraft modifications (engine pre-heaters, fuel systems) that not all operators maintain; dust and sand ingestion risks in summer require documented engine inspection protocols.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most industry-leading aviation procedure requirements under a mature FAA regulatory framework with readily available certified operators, but the Oak Flat / Superior, Arizona project location in a remote mountainous area introduces specific operational considerations including high-density-altitude conditions, complex terrain requiring instrument approach infrastructure assessment, and the need to carefully manage aviation-related noise and flight path planning given the cultural and spiritual significance of the Oak Flat area to the San Carlos Apache Tribe.
US.specific_barrier: Flight path planning over or near the Oak Flat cultural and sacred area may generate significant community and tribal opposition and reputational risk, even where FAA airspace rules technically permit such operations; Tonto National Forest airspace and terrain complexity requires careful route planning; high summer density altitude at the Superior, Arizona site (elevation approx. 960m, with extreme summer temperatures) reduces helicopter payload and performance margins — not a hard barrier but requires documented aircraft performance assessment per each operator.
US.workaround: Develop flight path protocols in consultation with San Carlos Apache Tribe to avoid or minimise overflight of culturally significant areas; include aviation noise and overflight considerations in FPIC engagement processes; pre-qualify only FAA Part 135-certified operators with documented SMS and mountainous/high-density-altitude operational experience; apply IOGP 590/591 as the governing standard above FAA minimums; implement a company-level aviation risk assessment tool for each flight sector.

GN.statement: Guinea lacks any dedicated process safety regulatory framework — there is no equivalent to IEC 61511, OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119), or an equivalent national standard — and the very limited availability of specialist process safety equipment (pressure relief systems, gas detection, SCADA, SIL-rated instrumentation) in-country makes implementation of an industry-leading process safety management system structurally impractical without near-total importation of hardware and expertise.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated process safety legislation or national PSM standard exists in Guinea; SIL-rated instrumentation, HAZOP/LOPA facilitation capability, and certified process safety engineers are not commercially available in-country; selective and discretionary enforcement means that even imported controls cannot be underpinned by regulatory assurance.
GN.workaround: Develop a self-contained, company-owned PSM system aligned to IEC 61511 / OSHA PSM principles as a voluntary standard above the regulatory baseline; import all safety-critical instrumentation and pressure-containing equipment from certified international suppliers; engage a third-party process safety engineering firm (e.g., based in Europe or South Africa) for HAZOP/LOPA studies and SIL verification; establish an in-house process safety competency register with expatriate-led training; document all controls against international benchmarks to demonstrate due diligence independent of the local regulatory framework.
