WA.statement: Western Australia has a mature and well-resourced process safety regulatory environment — including the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, the WHS (Mining) Regulations 2022, and Dangerous Goods Safety Act 2004 — with ready availability of certified process safety professionals, SIL-rated equipment, and HAZOP/LOPA facilitation services; the shift away from prescriptive requirements under new WHS Regulations is manageable given the high internal capability expected of major operators and the strong local consulting market.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a robust process safety framework under the MQSHA 1999, Safety and Health Management System requirements, and the Recognised Standard 18 (Control of Major Hazard Facilities), but limited equipment availability for specialist process safety instrumentation in remote Cape York Peninsula locations and the concurrent rehabilitation obligations at East Weipa create resource-stretch risks that may constrain full implementation of industry-leading PSM at pace.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited availability of specialist SIL-rated field instrumentation and process safety engineering contractors in the remote Weipa/Cape York Peninsula region; concurrent East Weipa closure compliance obligations may divert internal process safety management resources.
QLD.workaround: Pre-position critical process safety spares and instrumentation at site to mitigate supply chain delays; contract a Townsville or Brisbane-based process safety engineering firm on a retained basis for HAZOP, LOPA, and SIL verification; develop a clear internal resource allocation plan that separates closure compliance from active-operation PSM responsibilities; engage RSHQ early on site-specific MHF classification questions given the Norman Creek development timeline.

AR.statement: Argentina has a national framework referencing process safety principles through the Mining Code, provincial environmental licensing, and OHSAS/ISO 45001-equivalent expectations, but the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL in the Puna, limited in-country availability of SIL-rated process safety instrumentation, and significant provincial inconsistency in how process safety requirements are interpreted and enforced across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca create material implementation gaps relative to industry-leading standards.
AR.specific_barrier: Lack of in-country availability of SIL 2/3-rated instrumentation and certified functional safety engineers in the Puna region; provincial regulatory inconsistency means that HAZOP/LOPA documentation standards and MoC (Management of Change) expectations vary across operating provinces; altitude and remoteness create practical barriers to emergency response integration required under PSM.
AR.workaround: Procure all SIL-rated instrumentation through certified international suppliers (Buenos Aires import or direct from manufacturer); engage a Chilean or international process safety engineering firm familiar with high-altitude lithium brine operations for HAZOP and SIL verification; develop a harmonised company PSM standard that exceeds the highest provincial requirement and apply it uniformly across all Argentine operations; build altitude-specific emergency response protocols and pre-position first-responder capability at site.

CA.statement: Quebec has a well-developed process safety environment supported by the LSST, the Regulation respecting occupational health and safety in mines, and access to world-class process safety engineering expertise in Montreal and Quebec City, but incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations and ongoing uncertainty in the federal-provincial assessment interface introduce compliance planning uncertainty for future-state obligations, and James Bay/Northern Quebec Agreement obligations add procedural complexity for process hazard recognition in affected territories.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning that the full scope of future process safety-related permitting and assessment obligations cannot yet be definitively confirmed; BAPE review requirements for new mining phases may add timeline uncertainty to process safety-linked facility modifications.
CA.workaround: Design the PSM system to comply with the most demanding reasonably foreseeable interpretation of Bill 63 and the EQA Title II requirements; monitor MELCCFP regulation gazette publications and engage proactively with CNESST on process safety expectations for northern operations; retain Quebec-specialist environmental and safety legal counsel to track Bill 63 implementation in real time; engage with Cree Nation governance structures early where James Bay Agreement obligations may intersect with major hazard facility siting or modification approvals.

MN.statement: Mongolia has formal process safety obligations within its mining and environmental legislation, but the absence of operational EIA procedures and clear agency responsibilities, persistent weak enforcement, limited in-country availability of SIL-rated instrumentation and certified functional safety engineers, and the legislative instability created by over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law mean that industry-leading PSM implementation must be substantially self-managed by the operator without reliable regulatory scaffolding.
MN.specific_barrier: No certified functional safety engineers or HAZOP/LOPA facilitation capability resident in Mongolia; SIL-rated process instrumentation must be fully imported; EIA framework lacks national operational criteria meaning process hazard assessments cannot be validated against a defined regulatory standard; abrupt legislative changes may alter facility classification or permitting requirements mid-implementation.
MN.workaround: Import all safety-critical process instrumentation from certified international suppliers and maintain an in-country spare parts buffer; engage a third-party process safety engineering firm (Australia, South Korea, or Europe) for HAZOP, LOPA, and SIL verification on a periodic contracted basis; develop a self-standing company PSM standard benchmarked to IEC 61511 and OSHA PSM as the operative compliance reference; build regulatory change monitoring into the compliance calendar to detect and respond to Minerals Law or EIA framework amendments; document all PSM activities comprehensively to demonstrate international standard compliance independent of local enforcement action.

US.statement: The United States has one of the most comprehensive process safety regulatory frameworks globally — including OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119), EPA RMP (40 CFR Part 68), MSHA standards, and the 2024 Silica Final Rule — with ready availability of certified process safety engineers, SIL-rated equipment, and HAZOP/LOPA service providers; however, the pre-development status of the Oak Flat/Resolution Copper project means that site-specific PSM compliance has not yet been tested, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful boundary management to avoid compliance gaps.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities is not yet definitively mapped for this site, creating a risk of compliance gaps or duplicated/conflicting requirements; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) introduces new process ventilation and monitoring obligations that will require capital investment not yet confirmed; sustained Indigenous opposition and unresolved FPIC concerns may constrain the social licence to operate process safety-critical infrastructure.
US.workaround: Engage MSHA and OSHA jointly at pre-development stage to formally establish jurisdictional boundaries and agree on a unified compliance framework; develop a PSM system that satisfies both OSHA PSM and MSHA standards simultaneously to eliminate any gap at the interface; invest proactively in real-time silica monitoring and ventilation controls to meet the 2024 Final Rule PEL from first operation; incorporate Indigenous community engagement into process hazard review processes as a social licence measure and to surface site-specific knowledge of the operating environment.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement the procedural and administrative elements of a functional safety regime, but the absence of IEC 61508/61511-aligned local regulatory requirements, very limited local availability of certified functional safety engineers (TÜV FS Engineer or equivalent), and restricted access to SIL-rated instrumented safety system components creates material gaps against industry-leading standards.
GN.specific_barrier: No locally available TÜV-certified or IEC 61508/61511-competent Functional Safety Engineers; SIL-rated Safety Instrumented System (SIS) hardware (e.g., certified logic solvers, final elements) not commercially available in-country and subject to import delays and customs unpredictability; no Guinea-specific functional safety regulatory framework requiring SIL verification or safety lifecycle documentation.
GN.workaround: Engage internationally certified Functional Safety Engineers (TÜV FS Engineer / CFSE) on a fly-in-fly-out or remote basis for SIL determination, SIS design verification, and PSSR activities; pre-import all SIL-rated SIS hardware with adequate spares holdings and long-lead procurement planning; establish a company-owned functional safety management system (FSMS) that exceeds regulatory minimums and document all deviations from IEC 61511 with formal gap closure plans; implement rigorous proof-test procedures executable by trained site personnel to compensate for limited third-party verification access.

WA.statement: Western Australia provides a mature functional safety environment with IEC 61508/61511 recognised within the WHS (Mines) Regulations framework, ready availability of certified functional safety professionals and SIL-rated equipment suppliers in Perth and the broader Pilbara supply chain, and strong industry-leading practice via the MRIWA/DMIRS guidance ecosystem, although the removal of prescriptive requirements under new WHS Regulations increases the operator's burden to self-demonstrate SIL adequacy.

QLD.statement: Queensland supports full compliance with industry-leading functional safety standards under the MQSHA framework, with RSHQ's Safety and Health Management System requirements implicitly encompassing IEC 61511 for process plant and SIS applications, adequate (though not abundant) local access to functional safety competencies, and established supply chains for SIL-rated instrumentation through Brisbane and Townsville hubs, noting that remote Weipa logistics may introduce lead-time risk for specialised SIS hardware.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of SIL-rated SIS hardware and certified Functional Safety Engineers in Cape York/Weipa region, requiring sourcing from Brisbane or internationally with associated lead times.
QLD.workaround: Maintain strategic spares holdings of SIL-rated final elements and logic solvers at site; engage Brisbane-based or FIFO functional safety engineers for periodic SIL verification, PSSR, and proof-test witnessing; embed functional safety competency requirements into site safety management system and contractor prequalification criteria.

AR.statement: Argentina lacks a national regulatory instrument specifically mandating IEC 61508/61511 compliance or SIL-based safety lifecycle management for mining operations, functional safety engineering expertise is concentrated in Buenos Aires with very limited presence in Salta, Jujuy, or Catamarca provinces, and the extreme altitude and remoteness of Puna operations creates practical challenges for SIS commissioning, proof-testing, and specialist access.
AR.specific_barrier: No Argentine national or provincial mining regulation explicitly requiring SIL determination or IEC 61511 functional safety lifecycle documentation; absence of locally based TÜV-certified or CFSE-credentialed Functional Safety Engineers in the Puna operating provinces; altitude-related performance derating of some electronic SIS components must be factored into SIL calculations but is not addressed in local regulatory guidance.
AR.workaround: Adopt IEC 61511 as the company internal standard regardless of regulatory requirement, with full safety lifecycle documentation maintained voluntarily; contract Buenos Aires-based or international functional safety specialists for design verification, HAZOP/LOPA, and SIL determination phases; apply manufacturer altitude derating data to all SIS hardware specifications and document in the Safety Requirements Specification; build proof-test procedures executable by trained site instrument technicians with remote engineering oversight.

CA.statement: Quebec has a well-developed occupational health and safety legislative framework under the LSST/RSST and CNESST enforcement, and functional safety principles are recognised in industrial practice, but there is no Quebec-specific mining regulation that explicitly mandates IEC 61508/61511 SIL lifecycle methodology, the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations creates some near-term compliance planning uncertainty, and while functional safety engineers and SIL-rated equipment are readily available in Quebec, James Bay/northern project remoteness can create specialist access and equipment lead-time constraints.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations not yet fully operationalised as of March 2026, creating uncertainty about whether future regulatory updates will impose prescriptive functional safety lifecycle requirements; no explicit Quebec mining-sector mandate for IEC 61511 compliance, meaning the standard must be self-imposed as best practice rather than regulatory minimum.
CA.workaround: Implement IEC 61511 functional safety lifecycle as the company standard proactively, anticipating potential future regulatory prescription; monitor Bill 63 implementing regulations through consultation period and update FSMS accordingly; engage Quebec-based functional safety engineers (readily available in Montreal/Quebec City) for SIL verification and PSSR; maintain adequate SIS spares at James Bay sites to manage remote logistics risk.

MN.statement: Mongolia has no dedicated functional safety regulation aligned with IEC 61508/61511, enforcement of any safety instrumented system requirements is weak and inconsistent, locally available functional safety engineering expertise is very limited, and SIL-rated SIS hardware must be imported, but the procedural and management system elements of a functional safety programme can be implemented by a competent operator willing to self-impose international standards.
MN.specific_barrier: No Mongolia-specific regulatory requirement for SIL determination or IEC 61511 safety lifecycle compliance in mining; no identified locally based TÜV-certified or CFSE-credentialed Functional Safety Engineers; SIL-rated logic solvers, transmitters, and final control elements must be fully imported through Ulaanbaatar with associated customs delay and supply chain risk; UNDP-assessed weak EIA and process safety enforcement framework provides no regulatory backstop for functional safety performance.
MN.workaround: Self-impose IEC 61511 functional safety lifecycle as company mandatory standard, documented within the Safety and Health Management System; engage FIFO or remote international functional safety engineers for all SIL determination, design verification, and PSSR milestones; establish in-country strategic spares programme for all SIL-rated SIS components with minimum 12-month buffer stock; develop site-level proof-test procedures executable by trained instrument technicians with documented competency verification; engage Mongolian regulatory authorities proactively to align FSMS documentation with existing industrial safety legal requirements.

US.statement: The United States provides strong access to functional safety engineering expertise, SIL-rated equipment, and relevant standards (ANSI/ISA 84 / IEC 61511 adopted as industry norm for process safety), but MSHA's regulatory framework does not explicitly mandate an IEC 61511 safety lifecycle approach for hardrock mining operations in the way that OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119) does for covered chemical processes, creating a gap where the operator must self-impose rigorous functional safety lifecycle management, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful delineation to avoid SIS oversight gaps.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA 30 CFR Parts 56/57 do not explicitly require IEC 61511-aligned SIL determination or formal safety lifecycle documentation for hardrock mining SIS applications, meaning functional safety rigour must be self-imposed rather than regulatory-mandated; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities creates risk of functional safety lifecycle oversight falling between regulatory regimes if not explicitly managed; Oak Flat pre-development status means site-specific SIS design has not yet been reviewed or validated against final process design.
US.workaround: Adopt ANSI/ISA-84.00.01 (IEC 61511 MOD) as the mandatory company standard for all SIS applications regardless of whether MSHA explicitly requires it; prepare a formal OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary map for all facilities and assign explicit functional safety lifecycle ownership for each SIS loop; engage US-based certified Functional Safety Engineers (readily available) for SIL determination, design verification, and PSSR; integrate functional safety lifecycle milestones into the pre-development project schedule so that SIS design is verified before construction commissioning.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a Permit to Work system operationally, but the absence of dedicated ESG legislation and selectively enforced regulatory environment means there is no external statutory framework mandating or standardising PTW requirements, so the procedure must be entirely self-imposed and internally enforced without regulatory backstop.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated occupational health and safety or ESG-specific legislation exists that prescribes or validates a formal Permit to Work framework; the generic Mining Code and Environmental Code do not specify PTW requirements, meaning the system lacks regulatory anchoring and enforcement cannot be relied upon externally.
GN.workaround: Implement a fully self-contained, company-governed PTW system aligned to ICMM or ISO 45001 standards, with internal audit mechanisms that do not depend on regulatory inspection or enforcement; ensure all permit issuers and authorised persons are trained to a documented internal competency standard; embed PTW compliance into contractor management contracts given absence of state enforcement.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading Permit to Work requirements, supported by a mature WHS legislative framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act and successor WHS (Mining) regulations, readily available trained personnel, and well-established industry PTW practice across the Pilbara region.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading Permit to Work requirements under the well-developed Mine Safety and Health Act 1999 (MQSHA), Safety Management System obligations, and RSHQ oversight framework, though the multi-agency regulatory environment requires careful coordination to ensure PTW obligations are consistently applied across different permit categories and work types.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a functional Permit to Work system, but provincial primacy means PTW regulatory expectations vary across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, there is no single national PTW standard, and the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL introduces altitude-specific hazards (hypoxia, reduced cognitive function) that standard PTW templates do not address without specific adaptation.
AR.specific_barrier: No unified national Permit to Work standard exists; provincial occupational health and safety frameworks vary in prescriptiveness and enforcement capacity, and no verified evidence of province-level PTW-specific guidance was found for Salta, Jujuy, or Catamarca mining operations; altitude-related cognitive impairment risk is not addressed in generic PTW frameworks.
AR.workaround: Develop a single company PTW standard that meets or exceeds the most stringent provincial requirement across all three operating provinces; incorporate altitude-specific controls into PTW hazard identification sections including mandatory pre-task acclimatisation checks and restricted permit duration at high altitude; verify compliance requirements with each provincial mining authority individually given inconsistency of application.

CA.statement: Quebec can implement a Permit to Work system with strong alignment to the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST) and Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail (CNESST) requirements, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's reformed Mining Act regulations and the unresolved interaction between provincial and federal oversight frameworks introduce uncertainty around whether PTW obligations for new project phases will require updating as regulations are finalised.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft and subject to public consultation as of March 2026, meaning the full regulatory obligation set for new mining project phases is not yet confirmed; PTW procedures tied to specific work authorisations or environmental approvals may need revision once final regulations are gazetted.
CA.workaround: Implement PTW system to current CNESST and LSST standards immediately while monitoring Bill 63 final regulation publication; build a regulatory change review trigger into the PTW procedure document control cycle so that any new obligations from finalised regulations are captured and incorporated before project construction phases commence.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a Permit to Work system operationally, but persistent legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law, weak and inconsistently enforced occupational safety compliance, and limited availability of trained permit issuers and authorised persons outside Ulaanbaatar create practical gaps in sustaining a rigorous PTW system at remote mine sites.
MN.specific_barrier: No verified evidence of a Mongolia-specific statutory Permit to Work framework exists; occupational safety enforcement is characterised as weak, meaning PTW systems are unlikely to be externally validated or audited by regulators; limited pool of trained safety professionals in remote areas reduces the number of competent persons available to issue, check, and close high-risk work permits.
MN.workaround: Implement a self-contained PTW system to ICMM or ISO 45001 standards with all competency requirements met through internal training programmes; establish a minimum competency register for permit issuers and authorised isolating persons that does not depend on external licensing; conduct periodic third-party audits of PTW system effectiveness given absence of credible regulatory inspection; ensure PTW system documentation is maintained in both English and Mongolian to support local workforce compliance.

US.statement: The United States can comply with Permit to Work requirements, with MSHA's safety management expectations and OSHA standards providing a regulatory foundation, but the pre-development status of the Oak Flat project means site-specific PTW procedures have not yet been tested against actual operational conditions, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful procedural design to avoid coverage gaps in permit categories.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA and MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities creates a documented compliance gap risk where certain high-risk non-mining work activities (e.g. electrical, confined space, hot work at processing plant) may fall under OSHA rather than MSHA PTW expectations, requiring dual-standard awareness from permit issuers; no operational PTW track record exists for this specific site.
US.workaround: Develop a single integrated PTW procedure that explicitly maps each permit category (hot work, confined space entry, electrical isolation, working at height, ground disturbance) to the applicable regulatory standard — MSHA or OSHA — with the more stringent requirement applied where jurisdictions overlap; conduct pre-operational PTW system validation with both MSHA district office and relevant OSHA area office before first work commences.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core infectious disease controls but faces significant barriers including very limited availability of PPE (surgical masks, gloves, gowns, respirators), unreliable cold-chain infrastructure for vaccines, and no dedicated ESG or occupational health legislation requiring infectious disease management at mine sites — reliance on the generic Mining Code and Environmental Code leaves procedural requirements undefined and enforcement unpredictable.
GN.specific_barrier: Very limited commercial availability of medical-grade PPE (N95/FFP2 respirators, disposable gloves, isolation gowns) and no reliable in-country cold-chain or occupational health clinic network capable of supporting industry-standard infectious disease surveillance and vaccination programmes at remote mine sites in Guinea.
GN.workaround: Operator must import and stockpile medical-grade PPE and consumables through international supply chains; establish an on-site medical facility or contract a specialist occupational health provider (e.g., International SOS or similar); engage a local public health liaison with Guinea's Ministry of Health and Public Hygiene for disease surveillance integration; and draft a site-specific infectious disease control standard that fills the legislative gap, referencing WHO and IFC Performance Standard 2 as the compliance baseline in the absence of specific national requirements.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading infectious disease control requirements, supported by readily available PPE and medical supplies, a mature regulatory framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994 and Work Health and Safety (General) Regulations 2022, established occupational health service providers, and strong state public health infrastructure under the Public Health Act 2016.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with most industry-leading infectious disease control requirements under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999 and Public Health Act 2005, but the remote location of Weipa and the Cape York Peninsula creates practical challenges for maintaining cold-chain vaccine storage, sourcing limited medical consumables locally, and ensuring timely medical evacuation or infectious disease specialist response — equipment availability is characterised as limited rather than readily available.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local commercial availability of specialised infectious disease consumables and medical supplies in the Weipa/Cape York region, combined with remote-location constraints on timely access to infectious disease specialists or laboratory diagnostic services.
QLD.workaround: Establish a pre-positioned medical stockpile of PPE, rapid diagnostic test kits, and pharmaceuticals at site; maintain a standing contract with a fly-in fly-out occupational health physician and telehealth infectious disease specialist; coordinate with Queensland Health's Far North Queensland public health unit for surveillance and outbreak notification protocols; and include infectious disease scenarios in the site emergency response plan with defined medevac triggers.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement infectious disease controls at a procedural level, but the extreme high-altitude, remote operational environment (approximately 4,000m ASL in Puna salares) creates significant practical barriers to maintaining medical supply chains, cold-chain vaccine storage, and access to specialist infectious disease care, while provincial-level variation in occupational health enforcement and limited healthcare infrastructure in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca add further compliance uncertainty.
AR.specific_barrier: Absence of adequate cold-chain pharmaceutical storage and specialist infectious disease medical services within practical reach of high-altitude Puna operations, combined with limited provincial occupational health enforcement capacity and no defined national-level mining-specific infectious disease control standard.
AR.workaround: Operator must establish on-site refrigerated medical storage meeting cold-chain requirements; pre-stock antiviral and antibiotic supplies; contract an occupational health provider with Andean high-altitude experience (e.g., based in Salta or Jujuy capitals); implement a telemedicine protocol with an infectious disease specialist; align the procedure with Argentina's Ley 19.587 (Higiene y Seguridad en el Trabajo) and relevant SRT resolutions as the applicable national occupational health baseline; and engage provincial health ministries in each of the three provinces to ensure alignment with local public health notification requirements.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most infectious disease control requirements under the Loi sur la santé et la sécurité du travail (LSST), the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety, and Quebec's public health infrastructure, but remote James Bay/northern Quebec mine sites face practical constraints on medical supply availability, healthcare access, and cold-chain logistics, and the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations creates some residual uncertainty around future procedural requirements.
CA.specific_barrier: Remote northern Quebec mine sites (James Bay region) face limited local availability of medical consumables and restricted access to specialist infectious disease services, with the nearest major hospital facilities located in Val-d'Or, Rouyn-Noranda, or Chibougamau — distances that complicate rapid infectious disease response.
CA.workaround: Maintain a pre-positioned site medical cache including PPE, rapid antigen test kits, and first-line pharmaceuticals; establish a standing arrangement with a fly-in occupational health nurse or physician and telemedicine access to an infectious disease specialist; coordinate notification protocols with the Direction de santé publique de l'Abitibi-Témiscamingue or Nord-du-Québec as applicable; and monitor Bill 63 implementing regulations for any emerging occupational health obligations that may affect procedure requirements.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement basic infectious disease control measures supported by a trained workforce, but faces barriers including limited in-country availability of medical-grade PPE and pharmaceuticals in remote Gobi Desert mining regions, a weak and inconsistently enforced EIA and occupational health regulatory framework, unpredictable regulatory changes, and limited local healthcare infrastructure capable of supporting industry-standard infectious disease surveillance and outbreak response.
MN.specific_barrier: Limited commercial availability of medical-grade PPE (N95 respirators, isolation gowns, diagnostic test kits) outside Ulaanbaatar, and absence of specialist infectious disease medical services or equipped laboratory diagnostic capacity within practical reach of remote Gobi Desert mine sites such as Oyu Tolgoi.
MN.workaround: Operator must import and pre-position medical supplies and PPE via Ulaanbaatar distribution; establish an on-site medical clinic staffed by a resident nurse or physician with telemedicine access to an infectious disease specialist (potentially sourced from Australia or internationally); maintain a medical evacuation protocol to Ulaanbaatar (approx. 550km from Oyu Tolgoi) with defined infectious disease triggers; align the procedure with Mongolia's Law on Health (2011) and Labour Safety and Hygiene Law requirements as the applicable national baseline; and build in redundancy for supply chain disruptions given the remote location and cross-border freight dependencies.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most industry-leading infectious disease control requirements through OSHA's General Duty Clause, MSHA's health standards (30 CFR Parts 56/57), and CDC/NIOSH guidance frameworks, but the Resolution Copper project at Oak Flat remains in pre-development with no established site-specific compliance track record, the complex OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful procedural delineation, and the evolving regulatory environment (including the 2024 MSHA Silica Rule) necessitates ongoing procedure updates.
US.specific_barrier: The OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities creates a compliance gap risk for infectious disease controls — different respiratory protection standards (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 vs MSHA 30 CFR 56.5005) and recordkeeping requirements may apply to different areas of the same facility, requiring explicit procedural delineation that is not yet established for a pre-development site.
US.workaround: Develop a site-specific jurisdictional mapping document delineating OSHA and MSHA applicability zones across all facilities before operations commence; apply the more stringent standard where jurisdiction is ambiguous; align infectious disease procedure with both OSHA's Bloodborne Pathogens Standard (29 CFR 1910.1030) and MSHA health provisions; incorporate CDC and NIOSH guidance (including NIOSH Hierarchy of Controls) as the industry-leading baseline; and establish relationships with Pinal County and Arizona Department of Health Services for public health surveillance and outbreak notification integration.

GN.statement: Guinea has no dedicated radiation protection legislation or radiation-specific mining regulation; compliance relies on generic Mining and Environmental Codes, and while Guinea is a signatory to IAEA frameworks, there is no functional national radiation authority (ARSN has very limited operational capacity) and no domestic supply of calibrated dosimetry equipment or licensed radiation safety officers, meaning radiation control can only be implemented through entirely imported expertise and equipment with no local regulatory verification pathway.
GN.specific_barrier: No functional national radiation regulatory authority with enforcement capacity; no domestic supply of personal radiation dosimetry equipment (TLDs, electronic dosimeters) or calibrated survey instruments; no licensed Radiation Safety Officers (RSOs) available locally; no regulatory approval pathway for radiation source possession or use that is reliably actionable.
GN.workaround: Engage IAEA technical cooperation programmes for regulatory interface; import all dosimetry equipment and contract expatriate or regionally-based RSOs (e.g., from South Africa or France); self-regulate to IAEA Basic Safety Standards (GSR Part 3) as the de facto compliance framework; establish internal radiation management system documented to international standard given absence of enforceable local equivalent; budget for full equipment importation and calibration send-away cycles.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a comprehensive and mature radiation control framework under the Radiation Safety Act 1975 and Radiation Safety (General) Regulations 1983, administered by the Radiological Council of WA (transitioning under DEMIRS reforms), with full domestic availability of licensed RSOs, calibrated dosimetry suppliers (e.g., Landauer Australasia, Australian Radiation Protection Services), radiation source licensing, and well-established mining industry practice for naturally occurring radioactive material (NORM) and industrial radiation sources.

QLD.statement: Queensland operates a robust radiation safety framework under the Radiation Safety Act 1999 and Radiation Safety Regulation 2021, administered by the Department of Health's Radiation Safety Unit, with full availability of licensed RSOs, accredited training providers, calibrated dosimetry services, and established NORM management protocols relevant to bauxite and mineral sands mining contexts; equipment availability is noted as limited in the jurisdiction context but specialist radiation instrumentation can be sourced from Brisbane or interstate suppliers without structural impediment.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of specialist radiation equipment at remote Weipa/Cape York location, requiring logistics planning for instrument calibration send-away and emergency equipment deployment.
QLD.workaround: Establish on-site inventory of calibrated instruments with scheduled rotation to NATA-accredited calibration laboratories in Brisbane or Townsville; contract dosimetry services (e.g., Australian Radiation Protection Services) for TLD postal dosimetry; ensure RSO is site-based or on regular rotational presence given remoteness.

AR.statement: Argentina has a national radiation regulatory authority (ARN — Autoridad Regulatoria Nuclear) with a functional licensing and inspection regime under Decree 1390/98 and associated ARN norms, which is nationally administered and therefore less subject to provincial fragmentation than other environmental requirements; however, the extreme remote altitude environment (~4,000m ASL) of lithium operations in Salta/Jujuy/Catamarca creates practical barriers to equipment calibration, medical surveillance for radiation workers, and RSO presence, and limited availability of locally based radiation safety professionals requires importation from Buenos Aires.
AR.specific_barrier: No locally based licensed Radiation Safety Officers or ARN-registered radiation protection services within practical reach of high-altitude Puna operations; calibration of radiation instruments requires send-away to Buenos Aires or Córdoba (ARN-accredited laboratories), with extreme logistics due to remoteness and altitude; medical surveillance (haematological monitoring) for radiation workers difficult to execute at altitude with limited local medical infrastructure.
AR.workaround: Register all radiation sources with ARN and maintain ARN licence currency at national level; contract Buenos Aires-based RSO service providers for periodic site visits and remote oversight; use postal TLD dosimetry services (ARN-accredited suppliers); establish telemedicine link for occupational health surveillance requirements; maintain thorough internal records to demonstrate compliance during infrequent ARN inspections.

CA.statement: Quebec is subject to both federal (Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission — CNSC, under the Nuclear Safety and Control Act) and provincial (Loi sur la santé et la sécurité du travail — LSST, CNESST) radiation oversight, with full availability of dosimetry services (e.g., Health Canada's National Dose Registry, Landauer Canada) and licensed RSOs; however, the interaction between CNSC federal jurisdiction and Quebec's evolving Bill 63 provincial mining framework, plus the incomplete implementing regulations, creates potential for procedural uncertainty around radiation source licensing in the James Bay/northern Quebec project context, and BAPE review processes may intersect with radiation impact assessment requirements.
CA.specific_barrier: Incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations (as of March 2026) create uncertainty about whether provincial radiation-related environmental assessment requirements are fully defined for new northern Quebec mining projects; James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations may add procedural steps to radiation source licensing or NORM management approvals at northern sites.
CA.workaround: Maintain primary compliance with CNSC (federally) as the controlling radiation authority, which operates independently of the incomplete provincial reforms; engage CNSC early for Licence to Prepare Site and Licence to Operate applications; document NORM baseline assessments as part of EPA/BAPE environmental study; monitor Bill 63 regulation publication and engage CNESST proactively on occupational radiation exposure requirements under LSST.

MN.statement: Mongolia has a Nuclear Energy Agency (NEA) and radiation regulatory framework under the Law on Radiation Protection and Nuclear Energy Safety (2009, amended), with licensing requirements for radiation sources applicable to mining operations; however, enforcement is persistently weak, calibration laboratory infrastructure is limited (primarily Ulaanbaatar-based), no commercially available licensed RSO services exist outside the capital, and the general pattern of weak EIA implementation and regulatory unpredictability extends to radiation safety oversight, requiring operators to self-impose international standards with limited expectation of meaningful regulatory verification.
MN.specific_barrier: No licensed Radiation Safety Officer services or radiation protection consulting firms available outside Ulaanbaatar; instrument calibration must be conducted in Ulaanbaatar or sent internationally (e.g., to Australia or Germany), creating significant logistical burden for remote mine sites; NEA inspection capacity is limited and enforcement of radiation safety standards at mine sites is not reliably carried out.
MN.workaround: Self-regulate to IAEA Basic Safety Standards (GSR Part 3) and ICRP recommendations as the de facto compliance framework; contract Ulaanbaatar-based RSO or international RSO for periodic site visits; establish postal dosimetry programme with international accredited laboratory (e.g., Australian Radiation Protection Services or European equivalent); maintain comprehensive internal radiation management records; register all sources with NEA and maintain licence currency proactively given unpredictable regulatory engagement.

US.statement: The United States has a well-developed radiation regulatory framework applicable to mining, with NRC (for licensed radioactive materials), EPA (40 CFR Part 61 for NORM/TENORM at uranium and other mines), MSHA (30 CFR Part 57 for radon and radiation in underground mines), and Arizona state radiation control under the Arizona Radiation Regulatory Agency (ARRA); equipment and RSO services are readily available nationally, but the Oak Flat/Resolution Copper project involves a copper porphyry deposit where radiation control relates primarily to NORM/TENORM management in tailings and dust rather than discrete radioactive sources, and the overlapping MSHA/OSHA/NRC/EPA jurisdictional interface — compounded by the pre-development status of the project and unresolved FPIC obligations — means the specific applicable regulatory pathway for radiation source possession and TENORM management has not yet been tested or fully defined for this site.
US.specific_barrier: Overlapping and not-yet-tested jurisdictional interface between MSHA, EPA TENORM rules, and Arizona ARRA for the specific Oak Flat project configuration; regulatory pathway for TENORM management in copper tailings not yet operationally established for this pre-development site; pattern-of-violations enforcement risk under MSHA means any radiation-related non-compliance could trigger broader enforcement consequences.
US.workaround: Engage MSHA, EPA Region 9, and ARRA in pre-operational compliance planning to obtain written interpretive guidance on jurisdictional boundaries; develop a site-specific TENORM assessment and management plan as part of Environmental Impact Statement process; appoint a qualified RSO with specific US mining and TENORM experience; align procedure with MSHA 30 CFR Part 57 Subpart D (radiation) and EPA TENORM guidance documents; use NRC Agreement State (Arizona) framework for any discrete licensed radioactive material (e.g., nuclear density gauges).

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a functional isolation procedure operationally, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG or safety-specific legislation means there is no regulatory framework mandating lockout/tagout (LOTO) standards equivalent to ISO 17335 or ILO Code of Practice on LOTO, and the very limited availability of isolation hardware (lockout devices, hasp sets, multi-lock stations, energy isolation tags, and verification test equipment) requires importation, creating supply chain dependency and delays.
GN.specific_barrier: No Guinea-specific LOTO regulatory standard exists; isolation hardware (lockout stations, multi-lock hasps, LOTO padlock sets, voltage testers, discharge devices) is not commercially available in-country and must be sourced internationally, typically through Conakry via Dakar or Abidjan supply chains with lead times of 4–12 weeks.
GN.workaround: Adopt a company-owned isolation standard aligned with ILO LOTO Code of Practice or ICMM guidance as the governing document in lieu of absent local regulation; pre-position isolation hardware stockpiles on-site with minimum buffer quantities; conduct competency-based LOTO training in-house using imported training materials and translated into local languages; establish a dedicated isolation equipment register with scheduled inspection and replacement cycles managed centrally.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading isolation procedure requirements, supported by a mature and specific regulatory framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and AS 4024.1603 (Safety of Machinery — Isolation procedures), with readily available isolation hardware, licensed electrical workers, and a well-established LOTO training and competency assessment ecosystem.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with isolation procedure requirements under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, MQSHA Regulations, and associated standards, but the limited commercial availability of certain specialist isolation hardware (particularly multi-lock hasp sets, stored-energy verification instruments, and pneumatic isolation devices) in remote Cape York Peninsula/Weipa locations creates practical supply chain friction, and recent fatality data signals that LOTO implementation quality requires active reinforcement beyond procedural compliance.
QLD.specific_barrier: Remote location of Weipa operations means specialist isolation hardware and competency-based LOTO refresher training providers are not locally available — equipment and trainers must be sourced from Cairns or Brisbane, with supply lead times and logistical cost implications for consumable isolation materials (single-use tags, disposable lockout devices).
QLD.workaround: Maintain on-site buffer stock of all isolation consumables and hardware; schedule periodic LOTO competency verification visits from Cairns-based providers aligned with planned shutdowns; ensure the site isolation procedure explicitly references MQSHA Schedule 2 and AS/NZS 4024 series; implement a visible LOTO audit programme to address the gap between procedural existence and effective field execution identified in the independent RSHQ assessment.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement isolation procedures under Resolution SRT 299/2011 (national LOTO regulation) and applicable provincial mining safety decrees, but the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL in Puna operations introduces physiological and equipment reliability complications for isolation tasks, limited availability of specialist isolation hardware in-province requires supply from Buenos Aires or Santiago (Chile), and provincial regulatory inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca means the applicable enforcement standard and inspection regime will vary by project location.
AR.specific_barrier: At 4,000m ASL, worker cognitive performance during complex isolation sequences is demonstrably impaired by hypoxia; specialist electrical isolation verification instruments and pneumatic isolation devices rated for extreme temperature and altitude conditions are not routinely available in Jujuy or Salta provinces and require importation from Buenos Aires or internationally.
AR.workaround: Adapt isolation procedure to include altitude acclimatisation prerequisites before personnel perform isolation tasks; implement a two-person verification rule for all isolation steps at high-altitude sites; pre-position altitude-rated isolation hardware on-site; harmonise the procedure to the most stringent applicable provincial standard to ensure cross-provincial consistency; engage a Buenos Aires-based safety equipment supplier with scheduled replenishment logistics.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with isolation procedure requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (RSST Section 185–188 on lockout), and CNESST enforcement, with readily available isolation hardware and trained personnel, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations and the James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations for northern project areas introduce procedural uncertainty that may affect site-specific regulatory requirements as the compliance landscape continues to evolve through 2026.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft consultation as of March 2026, meaning that the final regulatory requirements for certain isolation-related obligations in new mining project contexts are not yet confirmed, creating a gap between current RSST requirements and anticipated future obligations that must be proactively monitored.
CA.workaround: Implement isolation procedure to existing RSST lockout requirements (Sections 185–188) and CSA Z460 Control of Hazardous Energy standard as the baseline, which exceeds current minimum regulatory requirements and provides a buffer against future regulatory uplift; monitor Bill 63 regulation gazette publications and engage CNESST proactively to confirm applicability of evolving requirements to project-specific contexts.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a functional isolation procedure operationally, but the regulatory framework for lockout/tagout under the Labour Safety and Hygiene Law and associated Mongolian National Standards (MNS series) lacks the specificity and enforcement consistency of industry-leading LOTO standards, specialist isolation hardware availability is limited in-country requiring importation through Ulaanbaatar distributors, and persistent weakness in regulatory enforcement means that compliance assurance must be driven internally rather than through regulatory pressure.
MN.specific_barrier: Specialist isolation hardware (multi-lock hasp systems, pneumatic and hydraulic isolation devices, stored-energy discharge verification instruments) is not routinely stocked by Mongolian safety equipment suppliers and must be imported from China, Australia, or Europe through Ulaanbaatar, with variable lead times; no verified Mongolian-language LOTO competency training curriculum aligned to international standards (ISO 17335 or equivalent) has been identified from commercially available providers in-country.
MN.workaround: Develop and maintain a company-owned isolation procedure aligned to ISO 17335 or ICMM guidance as the primary governing standard; source isolation hardware through established Ulaanbaatar import agents with pre-positioned site buffer stock; develop translated Mongolian-language LOTO training materials and deliver in-house competency assessment; implement a rigorous internal audit programme given the known gap between regulatory intent and enforcement reality.

US.statement: The United States can comply with isolation procedure requirements, which are governed by MSHA's 30 CFR Part 56/57 (lockout/tagout for surface and underground metal/nonmetal mines) and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 (Control of Hazardous Energy) where applicable at surface processing facilities, with readily available isolation hardware and training providers, but the pre-development status of the Oak Flat/Resolution Copper project means site-specific MSHA approval of the isolation procedure has not yet been obtained, and the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires explicit procedural delineation to avoid compliance gaps.
US.specific_barrier: The MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing and milling facilities has not yet been formally delineated for this specific site, creating a compliance gap risk where isolation procedures written to 30 CFR Part 56 may not fully satisfy OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 requirements for non-mining activities occurring in the same facility footprint.
US.workaround: Draft the isolation procedure to satisfy the more stringent of 30 CFR Part 56/57 and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 requirements at each identified work location; formally map the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary as part of pre-operational compliance planning and obtain MSHA district office concurrence; engage a qualified MSHA consultant to review the procedure prior to commencement of operations; ensure the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule interaction with isolation tasks in dusty environments is explicitly addressed.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core electrical safety controls but faces significant barriers including very limited local availability of certified electrical safety equipment (e.g., calibrated insulation testers, arc-flash PPE, lockout/tagout devices to IEC standards), no dedicated electrical safety legislation for mining beyond the generic Mining Code, and no established local pool of licensed or competent electricians certified to international standards such as IEC 60364 or equivalent.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated electrical safety legislation for mining; very limited local availability of arc-flash rated PPE, calibrated test equipment, and lockout/tagout hardware to IEC or equivalent standards; absence of formally licensed electrical competency certification regime in-country.
GN.workaround: Import all specialist electrical safety equipment and PPE (arc-flash suits, insulation resistance testers, LOTO devices) via Conakry through established freight channels; establish an internal company-defined electrical competency standard and assessment programme in lieu of absent national licensing; adopt IEC 60364 or AS/NZS 3000 as the company standard and document this in the procedure; schedule periodic third-party electrical audits by qualified international engineers flown in-country.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading electrical safety standards under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and AS/NZS 3000 Wiring Rules, with readily available certified equipment, licensed electricians, and a mature regulatory framework — the shift to self-managed risk under the new WHS Regulations requires robust internal documentation but does not create a structural barrier.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading electrical safety requirements under the Electrical Safety Act 2002, Electrical Safety Regulation 2013, and Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, but limited local equipment availability at remote Cape York/Weipa operations and the complexity of multi-agency oversight (RSHQ, DETSI, Electrical Safety Office) create practical gaps, particularly in sourcing specialist high-voltage testing equipment and arc-flash assessment services regionally.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of specialist high-voltage electrical testing equipment and arc-flash engineering assessment services in the remote Cape York Peninsula/Weipa region, requiring mobilisation from Cairns or Brisbane; multi-agency jurisdictional interface between RSHQ and the Electrical Safety Office requires careful coordination to avoid compliance gaps.
QLD.workaround: Establish a pre-positioned cache of calibrated electrical test equipment at site; engage a pre-contracted electrical engineering firm (e.g., based in Cairns) for periodic arc-flash studies and high-voltage maintenance; maintain a clear internal matrix mapping RSHQ versus Electrical Safety Office obligations for each work type; ensure all site electricians hold Queensland Electrical Worker licences with relevant mine endorsements.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement electrical safety controls drawing on national standards (IRAM and AEA 90364 based on IEC 60364) and provincial occupational safety frameworks, but extreme operational conditions at approximately 4,000m ASL affect equipment performance and worker capacity, specialist high-altitude rated electrical equipment and certified electricians experienced in Puna conditions are not readily locally available, and provincial regulatory inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca means compliance obligations cannot be assumed uniform.
AR.specific_barrier: Altitude-rated electrical equipment (switchgear, motors, transformers derated for ~4,000m ASL) and certified electricians experienced in Puna high-altitude mining operations are not readily available locally in Salta/Jujuy/Catamarca; provincial variation in electrical safety enforcement creates inconsistency risk.
AR.workaround: Specify and procure altitude-derated electrical equipment (per IEC 60664-1 altitude correction factors) from international suppliers via Buenos Aires or Santiago; engage an Argentine electrical engineering firm with high-altitude project experience (several operate out of Salta and Mendoza) to conduct arc-flash studies and equipment certification; develop a jurisdiction-specific compliance matrix for each province; consider augmenting with Chilean-certified electricians under bilateral labour arrangements given proximity.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply substantially with electrical safety requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Safety Code for the Construction Industry (S-2.1, r.4), and CSA Z462 (Workplace Electrical Safety) standards, with readily available equipment and trained workforce, but incomplete implementation of Bill 63 regulations and the evolving James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement interaction for northern project phases introduce procedural uncertainty that could affect timing of electrical installation approvals.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, creating uncertainty about whether any revised permitting or inspection requirements for electrical installations at new mining projects have changed; BAPE review timelines for new phases could delay energisation approvals for new electrical infrastructure.
CA.workaround: Adopt CSA Z462 and CSA C22.1 (Canadian Electrical Code) as explicit procedure standards to ensure compliance regardless of regulatory transition outcome; maintain close liaison with the Commission des normes, de l'équité, de la santé et de la sécurité du travail (CNESST) for procedural clarification; build regulatory transition contingency time into energisation and commissioning schedules for new infrastructure.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement basic electrical safety controls but faces meaningful barriers including limited local availability of calibrated high-voltage test equipment, arc-flash rated PPE, and lockout/tagout hardware to international standards, an absence of a formal national electrical safety licensing system equivalent to international norms, and a weak enforcement environment where regulatory oversight of electrical safety in mining is inconsistently applied.
MN.specific_barrier: No formal national electrician licensing or competency certification regime aligned to international mining electrical safety standards (e.g., IEC or equivalent); limited in-country commercial availability of arc-flash rated PPE (Category 2 and above), calibrated insulation and earth loop impedance testers, and IEC-compliant LOTO devices; nearest reliable supply hub is Beijing or Ulaanbaatar importers with long lead times.
MN.workaround: Import all specialist electrical safety equipment and arc-flash PPE from Beijing-based or international suppliers via Ulaanbaatar with pre-positioned on-site stock; establish a company-defined electrical competency assessment and authorisation scheme referencing IEC 60364 or AS/NZS 3000; employ an expatriate or third-country national licensed electrical engineer as Responsible Person for high-voltage systems; conduct annual third-party electrical safety audits by a qualified international firm; do not rely on Mongolian state electrical inspections as a compliance assurance mechanism.

US.statement: The United States can comply substantially with industry-leading electrical safety requirements under MSHA 30 CFR Parts 56/57 (surface and underground metal/nonmetal mines), NFPA 70E (arc-flash and electrical safety in the workplace), and OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart S for surface facilities, with readily available equipment and trained personnel, but the pre-development status of the Oak Flat/Resolution Copper project means site-specific electrical installation and inspection compliance under MSHA has not yet been tested, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful boundary management to avoid compliance gaps.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities is not yet formally delineated for this specific site, creating a risk of compliance gaps or double-regulation; MSHA operational compliance track record for this site has not yet been established, meaning initial inspections will set precedents under active scrutiny.
US.workaround: Engage MSHA Area Office proactively during design and pre-commissioning phases to agree jurisdictional boundaries and obtain advance review of electrical installation plans; adopt NFPA 70E as the company standard across all areas regardless of MSHA/OSHA jurisdiction to eliminate interface gaps; ensure all electrical workers hold documented MSHA Part 48 training completion and NFPA 70E qualified person credentials; conduct a formal arc-flash hazard analysis per NFPA 70E 130.5 prior to first energisation.

GN.statement: Guinea can comply with most requirements of a vehicles and driving procedure but faces practical barriers including very limited availability of fit-for-purpose light vehicles with certified safety features (e.g., roll cages, compliant seatbelts, ROPS/FOPS-certified cabs), unreliable road infrastructure, absence of formalised driver licensing standards equivalent to mining-industry norms, and no dedicated ESG or transport safety framework against which to benchmark compliance.
GN.specific_barrier: Very limited commercial availability of mining-specification light vehicles (e.g., ROPS/FOPS-certified 4WD with integrated roll protection and compliant restraint systems) and absence of accredited mine-site driver assessment or defensive driving training providers in-country.
GN.workaround: Operator must import or contract compliant vehicles directly through OEM channels (e.g., Toyota Land Cruiser 79-series with factory ROPS), establish an in-house driver competency assessment and defensive driving training programme delivered by expatriate or remotely-contracted trainers, implement a self-certifying pre-start inspection regime, and develop a fit-for-purpose journey management system that does not rely on functional public-sector enforcement or road authority oversight.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading vehicles and driving requirements, supported by readily available compliant vehicles, a mature regulatory framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994 and the new WHS (Mining) Regulations 2022, well-established defensive driving training providers, and a strong culture of journey management and light vehicle safety in the resources sector.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading vehicles and driving requirements under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, RSHQ oversight, and well-established industry codes of practice for vehicle and traffic management, though the remote Cape York Peninsula location of Weipa requires robust journey management and emergency response provisions given distance from emergency services.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with most vehicles and driving requirements but the extreme operational environment (approximately 4,000m ASL, remote Puna locations, arid conditions) and limited availability of locally-sourced mining-specification vehicles with appropriate high-altitude performance and cold-weather capability create practical compliance gaps, compounded by inconsistent provincial enforcement of transport safety standards and limited access to accredited defensive driving training providers in-country.
AR.specific_barrier: Extreme altitude and remoteness of Puna operations (approx. 4,000m ASL) creates vehicle specification requirements — including altitude-compensated engines, cold-start capability, hypoxia awareness protocols for drivers, and extended emergency supply requirements — that standard Argentine commercial vehicle supply chains and training providers are not routinely equipped to support.
AR.workaround: Operator must specify and procure vehicles with factory high-altitude engine calibration and appropriate cold-weather packages, develop altitude-specific driver fitness and acclimatisation protocols embedded within the procedure, implement a satellite-based journey management and tracking system given mobile network absence, pre-position emergency supplies and rescue equipment along defined travel routes, and contract defensive driving training through an international provider (e.g., ADAC, IAM RoadSmart, or equivalent) capable of delivering programme in-country or remotely.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most vehicles and driving requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Highway Safety Code, and applicable mining sector standards, but the remote James Bay and northern Quebec operating environment presents practical challenges including seasonal road access limitations (ice roads, spring break-up closures), extreme cold weather vehicle operability requirements, and the need for comprehensive journey management systems that account for limited emergency response coverage in remote areas.
CA.specific_barrier: Seasonal and weather-dependent road access in northern Quebec (including reliance on ice roads and remote tracks subject to spring break-up closures) means that standard journey management assumptions around road availability, travel time, and emergency response are unreliable for significant portions of the year.
CA.workaround: Operator must implement a seasonally-adapted journey management procedure with explicit ice road travel protocols (load limits, ice thickness monitoring, exclusion periods), extreme cold weather vehicle preparation standards (block heaters, arctic-rated lubricants, survival kit mandates), defined no-go periods during spring break-up, and satellite communication and tracking as standard for all remote travel given cellular network gaps; procedure should reference CSA standards for cold weather vehicle operation.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with most vehicles and driving procedure requirements at the operational level, but faces barriers including limited availability of mining-specification vehicles and parts in-country, weak and inconsistently enforced national road transport safety regulation, limited access to accredited defensive driving training providers, and harsh steppe and Gobi Desert operating conditions that demand vehicle specifications and journey management controls beyond standard commercial practice.
MN.specific_barrier: Limited in-country availability of mining-specification light vehicles with ROPS/FOPS certification and compliant restraint systems, combined with absence of accredited mine-site defensive driving training providers and unreliable supply chains for replacement safety-critical parts (e.g., tyres rated for extreme temperature ranges from -40°C to +40°C).
MN.workaround: Operator must import compliant vehicles and maintain a strategic spares inventory (particularly cold-weather-rated tyres, batteries, and filtration components), establish an in-house driver competency and defensive driving training programme delivered by contracted international trainer, implement satellite-based journey management and tracking as standard given remote locations and cellular network gaps, and define seasonal travel restrictions and vehicle cold-start protocols for winter operations.

US.statement: The United States can comply with the substantive requirements of a vehicles and driving procedure under MSHA 30 CFR Part 56 (surface metal and nonmetal mines) and applicable federal and Arizona state traffic regulations, and compliant vehicles and training providers are readily available, but the pre-development status of the Resolution/Oak Flat project means site-specific traffic management plans, haul road design approvals, and MSHA inspection benchmarks have not yet been established, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing areas requires careful procedural demarcation.
US.specific_barrier: Project remains in pre-development phase, meaning site-specific MSHA-approved traffic management plans, haul road design, and vehicle safety standards have not yet been operationally tested or formally reviewed by MSHA inspectors; additionally, the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities requires explicit procedural mapping to avoid compliance gaps.
US.workaround: Operator should proactively engage MSHA during design phase to align haul road and traffic management design with 30 CFR Part 56 requirements before construction begins, explicitly define the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary in the procedure with cross-references to site-specific traffic control plans, ensure defensive driving and pre-start inspection training programmes are benchmarked to MSHA expectations, and incorporate the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule requirements into vehicle cab air filtration and road dust suppression specifications.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a PPE procedure but faces significant practical barriers: certified PPE to international standards (ANSI/ISO/EN) is not reliably available in-country, procurement must rely on importation through Conakry with unreliable supply chains, and the absence of dedicated ESG or OHS legislation means there is no domestic regulatory framework specifically mandating PPE standards beyond generic Mining Code provisions.
GN.specific_barrier: Certified PPE (hard hats, respirators, safety footwear, high-visibility garments meeting EN/ANSI/ISO standards) is not commercially available in Guinea's domestic market; importation through Conakry port is subject to customs delays and inconsistent quality control, and no domestic regulatory PPE standard exists against which compliance can be formally verified.
GN.workaround: Establish a dedicated PPE import and stockpile programme managed centrally (e.g., via Conakry logistics hub), with minimum 90-day buffer stock on site; specify all PPE to EN or ANSI standards in the procedure regardless of the absence of local equivalents; conduct in-house PPE inspection and issuance training given the absence of third-party certification bodies; and document compliance against international standards (e.g., ICMM, IFC Performance Standards) rather than relying on domestic regulatory alignment.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with an industry-leading PPE procedure: certified PPE to Australian Standards (AS/NZS) is readily available, the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994 and the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022 provide a robust legal framework for PPE obligations, and the shift to self-managed risk under updated WHS Regulations aligns with an operator-led PPE procedure approach.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with PPE procedure requirements under a mature legislative framework (MQSHA, RSHQ), but the remote location of Weipa/Cape York Peninsula creates practical supply chain challenges for specialty PPE items (e.g., respiratory protective equipment for silica, arc-flash rated PPE), and limited local availability may require planned procurement cycles rather than on-demand replenishment.
QLD.specific_barrier: Specialty certified PPE items — including P2/P3 respirators, arc-flash rated garments, and hearing protection rated to AS/NZS 1269 — have limited availability from local suppliers at Weipa; the nearest major supply hubs (Cairns or Brisbane) are approximately 800–1,000 km away, creating potential for stockout risk in high-consumption categories.
QLD.workaround: Implement a scheduled forward-procurement and buffer-stock programme for specialty PPE held on site; establish a preferred supplier agreement with a Cairns-based distributor with guaranteed delivery SLAs; include PPE stock levels in routine site inspection checklists to prevent depletion below minimum thresholds.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a PPE procedure but the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL in the Puna (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca) introduces specific PPE requirements — altitude-rated respiratory protection, UV-protective eyewear, extreme cold and arid climate gear — that are not reliably sourced domestically to international certification standards, and provincial regulatory variation means the specific PPE standard required may differ across operational provinces.
AR.specific_barrier: Altitude-specific PPE (including supplemental oxygen delivery equipment, high-altitude rated respiratory protective devices, and UV-400 eye protection for high solar radiation at 4,000m ASL) is not reliably available through Argentine domestic supply chains; furthermore, certified PPE meeting EN/ANSI/NIOSH standards is inconsistently available outside Buenos Aires, requiring long supply routes to remote Puna operations.
AR.workaround: Centralise PPE procurement through Buenos Aires-based distributors with international certification verification; maintain minimum 60-day altitude-specific PPE buffer stock at each Puna site; include altitude physiology and PPE selection training in the induction programme; and specify international standards (EN, ANSI, NIOSH) explicitly in the procedure to manage provincial regulatory inconsistency by anchoring to a single global standard.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with PPE procedure requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST) and CNESST enforcement, with PPE readily available to CSA/ANSI standards; however, the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations introduces minor uncertainty about whether future regulatory updates will impose additional PPE-specific obligations not yet defined, and remote James Bay territory operations face practical supply logistics challenges.
CA.specific_barrier: For operations in remote James Bay/Northern Quebec territory, specialty PPE (cold-weather rated to CSA Z195 and Z94 series, extreme cold exposure suits, bear deterrent equipment) requires planned logistics from southern Quebec supply centres given the absence of local industrial suppliers, with winter weather potentially disrupting resupply schedules.
CA.workaround: Maintain seasonal forward-stocking of cold-weather and extreme environment PPE at James Bay sites ahead of winter; establish a supplier agreement with a Montreal or Val-d'Or distributor with helicopter or charter freight capability; monitor Bill 63 implementing regulations through CNESST updates and build a regulatory change review cycle into the procedure's annual review process.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a PPE procedure but faces persistent practical barriers: PPE certified to international standards (EN/ANSI/ISO) has limited domestic availability, the majority of compliant PPE must be imported through Ulaanbaatar, supply chains to remote steppe and Gobi operations are unreliable especially in winter, and weak enforcement of occupational health standards means there is limited regulatory pressure to maintain international-standard PPE compliance.
MN.specific_barrier: Certified PPE meeting EN or ANSI standards (respiratory protection, safety footwear to EN ISO 20345, arc-rated garments, cold-weather rated workwear for temperatures reaching -40°C) is not reliably available through Mongolian domestic retail or industrial supply; the majority must be imported, and counterfeit or non-certified PPE is documented as a risk in the Mongolian market.
MN.workaround: Procure all PPE centrally through verified international suppliers (e.g., via Singapore or Australian procurement hubs) with certification documentation required at point of purchase; implement incoming PPE inspection and batch recording at Ulaanbaatar before distribution to site; maintain 90-day buffer stock at remote operations; include PPE anti-counterfeiting checks (CE marking verification, manufacturer traceability) in the site inspection protocol.

US.statement: The United States can comply with an industry-leading PPE procedure with PPE readily available to ANSI/ISEA and NIOSH standards, and a clear regulatory framework under MSHA (30 CFR Parts 56/57) and OSHA (29 CFR 1910.132–138); however, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule reducing the permissible exposure limit to 50 µg/m³ requires proactive investment in upgraded respiratory protection (NIOSH-approved P100 or supplied-air respirators) and enhanced fit-testing programmes that represent a material compliance uplift over prior practice.
US.specific_barrier: The 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (effective August 2024 with phased compliance deadlines) requires half-face respirators with P100 filters or supplied-air respirators where silica exposures exceed the new 50 µg/m³ PEL, along with mandatory medical evaluations, fit-testing, and written respiratory protection programmes — representing a significant uplift from previous MSHA respiratory PPE requirements that must be operationalised before Resolution Copper reaches production.
US.workaround: Conduct baseline silica exposure monitoring during pre-production and construction phases to characterise exposure profiles; procure NIOSH-approved respiratory protection and establish a fit-testing programme compliant with OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 (applicable by reference under MSHA); ensure the PPE procedure explicitly references the 50 µg/m³ action level and specifies the required respirator selection hierarchy; and train supervisors on the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities to prevent gaps in respiratory protection coverage.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement basic barricading and demarcation in practice, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG or WHS legislation means there is no regulatory standard specifying barricade types, signage specifications, or demarcation requirements — compliance must be self-defined against an international benchmark with no local regulatory anchor, and selective enforcement creates unpredictability around what will be deemed acceptable.
