GN.specific_barrier: No Guinea-specific WHS or site safety regulation prescribing barricade, signage, or demarcation standards; physical barricading materials (water-filled barriers, certified safety signs, reflective tape, delineator posts to ANSI/ISO specifications) have very limited in-country availability and must be imported, extending lead times significantly.
GN.workaround: Adopt ICMM or ISO 45001-aligned internal standard as the compliance reference in the absence of local regulation; pre-order and stockpile certified barricading materials and compliant safety signage (multilingual including French and local languages) through Conakry import channels; establish a local fabrication or supplier arrangement for consumable items; document the self-imposed standard formally so it can be demonstrated to regulators if required.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading barricading, signage, and demarcation requirements, supported by the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, Australian Standards (AS 1743, AS/NZS 1906, AS 4602), readily available certified materials, and a trained workforce experienced in these requirements.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading barricading, signage, and demarcation requirements under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, Safety and Health Management System obligations, and applicable Queensland standards, noting that limited equipment availability at remote Cape York/Weipa locations requires supply chain planning but does not constitute a structural barrier.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with core barricading and demarcation requirements under provincial WHS frameworks and Resolution SRT 51/97 (signage) and general OHS law (Law 19.587), but the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL, limited local availability of certified barricading materials in remote Puna locations, and inconsistent provincial enforcement capacity create practical gaps requiring active management.
AR.specific_barrier: Certified high-visibility barricading materials, retro-reflective safety signs meeting IRAM or ISO specifications, and rigid water-filled or interlocking barrier systems are not reliably available through local suppliers in Salta, Jujuy, or Catamarca provinces for remote Puna operations; materials must typically be sourced from Buenos Aires or imported, with extended supply lead times to high-altitude sites.
AR.workaround: Establish a pre-positioned buffer stock of barricading consumables and certified signage at site or nearest provincial hub; engage a Buenos Aires-based industrial safety supplier with scheduled resupply logistics to site; develop bilingual (Spanish) signage meeting IRAM 10005 series standards produced in-country; align procedure to IRAM and ISO 45001 standards to demonstrate compliance intent to provincial labour and mining inspectorates with varying technical capacity.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with barricading, signage, and demarcation requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines (S-2.1, r.14), and CNESST enforcement; however, Bill 63's incompletely operationalised regulations introduce near-term uncertainty about whether any new mine-specific requirements will alter current standards, and remote James Bay/northern Quebec site logistics create practical supply challenges.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations are not yet finalised as of March 2026, meaning site-specific regulatory requirements for future project phases cannot be fully confirmed; additionally, certified barricading materials (interlocking barriers, compliant signage to CSA and Quebec standards) are not readily available from local suppliers in remote northern Quebec communities and must be freighted from Montreal or Val-d'Or, creating supply chain vulnerability.
CA.workaround: Implement current procedure to existing CNESST Mine Regulation standards as the confirmed baseline; monitor Bill 63 regulatory consultation outcomes and build a formal regulatory change management trigger into the procedure review cycle; establish a pre-positioned materials inventory at the nearest viable logistics hub (e.g., Radisson or Matagami) with minimum stock levels defined; engage a Quebec-licensed occupational health and safety materials supplier for scheduled replenishment.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement barricading and demarcation in practice but faces significant gaps: the EIA and WHS framework lacks operational procedures and clear agency accountability, certified barricading materials to international standards are not reliably available in-country outside Ulaanbaatar, and persistently weak enforcement means the regulatory baseline against which compliance is measured is inconsistent and unpredictable.
MN.specific_barrier: No Mongolia-specific mining WHS regulation prescribing barricade specifications, sign standards, or demarcation distances to an international benchmark has been verified; certified retro-reflective safety signs, rigid interlocking barriers, and demarcation systems meeting ISO or equivalent standards are not commercially available through domestic suppliers outside Ulaanbaatar, requiring importation for remote Gobi or steppe operations with extended lead times.
MN.workaround: Adopt ICMM Guidelines or ISO 45001 as the internal compliance reference and document this formally; source certified barricading materials and multilingual Mongolian/English safety signs through Ulaanbaatar industrial suppliers or import from China (where supply chains are established); pre-position buffer stocks at site given supply chain distance; train a designated site safety officer specifically in barricade and demarcation inspection to compensate for absence of reliable external regulatory audit.

US.statement: The United States has a well-defined federal regulatory framework for barricading and demarcation under MSHA 30 CFR Part 56 (surface metal and nonmetal mines) and OSHA 29 CFR 1910.144/145, with readily available certified materials, but the project's pre-development status means site-specific MSHA compliance has not yet been operationally tested, and the complex MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires deliberate management to avoid demarcation and signage gaps at the boundary between the two regimes.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing or beneficiation facilities creates a compliance interface risk where barricading and signage standards differ between the two agencies (e.g., OSHA 29 CFR 1910.145 vs. MSHA 30 CFR 56.20002–56.20003) and the demarcation of that boundary is not always clear in practice, particularly during construction phases where OSHA jurisdiction may apply before MSHA jurisdiction is triggered.
US.workaround: Develop a site-specific jurisdictional boundary map clearly delineating MSHA and OSHA zones and apply the more stringent standard at interfaces; engage an MSHA-experienced compliance consultant during the pre-development phase to review the barricading and demarcation procedure against 30 CFR Part 56 requirements specific to the Oak Flat site configuration; establish a procedure provision explicitly addressing the MSHA/OSHA transition point and require MSHA pre-inspection of all barricade and signage layouts prior to commencing operations.

GN.statement: Guinea can comply with core excavation and penetration controls in practice, but the absence of dedicated ESG legislation, no formal permit-to-work regulatory framework specific to excavation hazards, and very limited availability of specialist equipment (e.g., ground-penetrating radar for underground service detection, trench shoring systems, atmospheric gas monitors) mean the procedure must be substantially self-regulated and equipment sourced externally, with no reliable regulatory backstop.
GN.specific_barrier: No nationally available trench shoring/benching equipment supply chain, no regulatory requirement for competent person sign-off on excavation stability, and absence of utility mapping infrastructure or cadastral service registers making pre-excavation service identification unreliable or impossible in most locations.
GN.workaround: Operator must establish a self-contained permit-to-work system referencing international standards (e.g., ICMM, IFC EHS Guidelines), import or contract specialist detection equipment (GPR, multi-gas monitors) and shoring materials, conduct internal competent-person training and accreditation, and maintain a site-specific underground services register built from first-principles survey rather than relying on any national utility registry.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading excavation and penetration requirements under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and associated codes of practice, with readily available equipment, trained competent persons, and a mature permit-to-work culture, noting only that the July 2025 DEMIRS restructure may create minor short-term uncertainty in inspector contact points.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading excavation and penetration standards under MQSHA 1999, the Coal Mining Safety and Health Act 1999, and RSHQ-administered regulations, with a well-developed permit-to-work culture, available specialist equipment, and trained workforce, though the remote Cape York Peninsula location of Weipa operations may require additional logistics planning for equipment and emergency response relative to southeast Queensland sites.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with the core intent of excavation and penetration controls, but the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL (affecting worker physiological capacity, equipment performance, and emergency response times), limited local availability of specialist detection and shoring equipment, and provincially fragmented regulatory oversight across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca create meaningful gaps relative to industry-leading standards.
AR.specific_barrier: At high-altitude Puna sites, hypoxic conditions reduce worker cognitive and physical performance during excavation tasks; specialist trench support systems and ground-penetrating radar units are not reliably available in-country and must be sourced from Buenos Aires or imported, with lead times extending emergency response and maintenance capability; no consistent provincial standard for competent-person qualification in excavation safety.
AR.workaround: Implement altitude-specific work-rest regimes and medical fitness standards within the procedure; pre-position shoring equipment and detection tools at site rather than relying on local supply; establish a project-specific competent person accreditation program aligned to IRAM standards and IFC EHS Guidelines; define a single internal standard applied consistently across all three provincial jurisdictions to manage regulatory inconsistency.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most excavation and penetration requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Mining Act, and Quebec Environment Quality Act, with readily available equipment and a trained workforce, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63 regulations, the new ATI permitting layer for impact-causing work, and BAPE review obligations for new project phases introduce procedural uncertainty that may affect the timing and scope of excavation permitting approvals.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft as of March 2026, meaning the precise scope of environmental assessment triggers for excavation work at new project phases is not yet fully defined, creating a compliance planning gap for activities that may constitute 'impact-causing exploration work' under the ATI requirement effective May 2024.
CA.workaround: Engage proactively with the Ministère des Ressources naturelles et des Forêts to clarify ATI applicability to specific excavation activities; apply conservative interpretation of Bill 63 obligations pending final regulations; maintain dual compliance tracking against both current and anticipated regulatory requirements; ensure permit-to-work system is documented to demonstrate good-faith compliance pending regulatory finalisation.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with basic excavation and penetration controls, but limited local availability of specialist equipment (ground-penetrating radar, multi-gas monitors, engineered trench shoring systems), a persistently weak EIA and environmental compliance enforcement framework with no clear operational procedures, and unpredictable regulatory approval timelines mean that a self-regulated, internationally benchmarked system is required rather than reliance on domestic regulatory assurance.
MN.specific_barrier: No established domestic market for certified trench shoring systems or ground-penetrating radar services; Mongolia's EIA framework lacks operational procedures and national criteria per UNDP assessment, meaning no regulatory competent-person standard exists for excavation stability sign-off; utility and underground service mapping is unreliable or absent outside Ulaanbaatar, making pre-excavation service identification dependent entirely on operator-maintained records.
MN.workaround: Operator must import or contract specialist detection and shoring equipment, establish an internal competent-person certification program aligned to ICMM or equivalent international standard, build and maintain a site-specific underground services register from first-principles survey, and implement a self-contained permit-to-work system that does not rely on regulatory inspection or approval to function effectively.

US.statement: The United States has a comprehensive regulatory framework for excavation and penetration safety under MSHA 30 CFR Parts 56/57 (surface and underground metal/nonmetal mines) and OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart P (excavations), with readily available equipment and trained personnel, but the Oak Flat project remains pre-operational meaning site-specific competent persons and established permit-to-work systems have not yet been field-tested, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule introduces additional dust monitoring obligations during excavation, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful demarcation to avoid compliance gaps.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface facilities adjacent to the mine boundary is not yet defined through established operational practice at this pre-development site, creating a compliance gap risk for excavation work in transitional areas; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) requires real-time silica monitoring capability during excavation that must be procured and validated before operations commence.
US.workaround: Conduct pre-operational OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional mapping exercise to formally delineate the boundary and assign regulatory applicability to all planned excavation locations; procure and validate real-time silica monitoring equipment and competency ahead of first ground disturbance; establish the permit-to-work system and competent-person program during the pre-development phase so it is field-tested before operational excavation commences.

PRO-0030.procedure_title: Working Over Water and Marine Vetting Activities Procedure

GN.statement: Guinea lacks dedicated marine safety legislation, has no verified BOSIET/HUET training providers in-country, no established maritime vetting framework, and very limited availability of specialist marine safety equipment (life rafts, man-overboard systems, rescue craft to SOLAS/IMCA standards), making industry-leading compliance with a working-over-water and marine vetting procedure structurally impractical without extensive external sourcing.
GN.specific_barrier: No BOSIET/HUET providers verified within Guinea; no dedicated maritime safety or marine vetting regulatory framework; no domestic IMCA-compliant vessel inspection or vetting infrastructure; specialist water rescue and personal survival equipment (immersion suits, fast rescue craft) not commercially available in-country.
GN.workaround: Mobilise all marine safety training (BOSIET/HUET/sea survival) through accredited providers in Dakar (Senegal) or Abidjan (Côte d'Ivoire); import all specialist marine safety equipment and PPE via project logistics chain; engage an internationally accredited marine warranty surveyor and IMCA-registered vessel inspector on a fly-in/fly-out basis; develop a bespoke Guinea-specific marine vetting protocol given the absence of a domestic regulatory framework, referencing IMO, IMCA, and OCIMF standards as the compliance baseline; accept that formal regulatory endorsement of this protocol may be unachievable or indefinitely delayed given selective enforcement and institutional capacity limitations.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a mature and well-enforced marine safety regulatory framework under the Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations and the Marine and Harbours Act, with readily available BOSIET/HUET training providers, IMCA-accredited vessel inspection services, and full commercial availability of industry-standard marine safety equipment, enabling full compliance with an industry-leading working-over-water and marine vetting procedure.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a comprehensive regulatory framework for marine safety (Transport Operations (Marine Safety) Act 1994, WHS Act, RSHQ oversight) and BOSIET/HUET training is available in Brisbane and Mackay, but limited equipment availability in the remote Cape York/Weipa operational area and the practical challenges of regulatory inspection frequency and emergency marine response in that location introduce compliance gaps that require active management.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited commercial availability of IMCA-standard marine safety equipment and specialist rescue craft at or near Weipa/Cape York Peninsula; nearest BOSIET/HUET providers are in Mackay or Brisbane, requiring worker mobilisation for training; remote location increases emergency marine response times beyond industry-standard benchmarks.
QLD.workaround: Pre-position a dedicated inventory of marine safety equipment (immersion suits, fast rescue craft, life rafts, throw lines, PPE) at the Weipa operational base; arrange BOSIET/HUET training for all water-adjacent workers in Mackay or Brisbane prior to site mobilisation and maintain records centrally; establish a site-specific marine emergency response plan with defined escalation to Queensland Water Police and AMSA; conduct internal marine vetting audits using an IMCA-registered surveyor mobilised to site.

AR.statement: Argentina's mining operations in the assessed jurisdictions (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca) are conducted at high-altitude Puna salares at approximately 4,000m ASL — environments characterised by brine ponds and salt flat water bodies rather than navigable marine or riverine waters — and no verified information can be found confirming that marine vetting requirements, BOSIET/HUET providers, or IMCA-standard vessel inspection services exist or are applicable in this operational context, making a determination on marine-specific compliance feasibility impossible.
AR.specific_barrier: Procedure applicability is unverified: lithium brine operations in the Argentine Puna do not involve conventional marine or navigable waterway environments; no verified BOSIET/HUET providers, marine vetting infrastructure, or applicable national maritime safety framework has been identified for this inland high-altitude operational context.
AR.workaround: Conduct a formal procedure applicability review to determine whether PRO-0030 applies to brine pond/evaporation pond working-over-water activities in the Puna context; if applicable, define a bespoke risk assessment framework referencing relevant IMO/IMCA principles adapted for non-navigable inland brine environments; source any required marine safety training from Buenos Aires or Santiago (Chile) providers; engage a marine or aquatic safety specialist to develop site-specific controls given the absence of a domestic framework for this environment.

CA.statement: Quebec has a functioning marine safety regulatory framework under the Canada Shipping Act 2001 (federal) and provincial occupational health and safety legislation, with BOSIET/HUET training available in Montreal and Quebec City, but James Bay project locations are remote, marine safety equipment availability is limited near site, and the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63 regulations introduces procedural uncertainty for water-adjacent activity approvals.
CA.specific_barrier: Nearest BOSIET/HUET providers to James Bay operational areas are in Montreal or Quebec City, requiring significant worker travel; commercial availability of IMCA-standard fast rescue craft and specialist marine safety equipment near remote northern Quebec project sites is limited; regulatory interface between federal Transport Canada marine jurisdiction and Quebec provincial OHS requirements for inland water operations requires careful navigation.
CA.workaround: Pre-position marine safety equipment inventory at the James Bay project camp or nearest logistical hub; schedule BOSIET/HUET training in Montreal or Quebec City as part of site induction mobilisation; develop a site-specific inland water safety plan referencing Transport Canada small vessel regulations and Quebec OHS standards; engage a Transport Canada-recognised marine safety auditor for annual vetting reviews; monitor Bill 63 implementing regulations for any provisions affecting water-body activity permitting.

MN.statement: Mongolia has no identified domestic BOSIET/HUET training providers, no IMCA-accredited vessel inspection or marine vetting infrastructure, no dedicated maritime safety legislation applicable to inland industrial water operations, and very limited commercial availability of industry-standard marine safety equipment, while the primary Mongolian mining operations assessed are inland steppe environments where the applicability of a marine vetting procedure to any water-adjacent activities would itself need to be established.
MN.specific_barrier: No BOSIET/HUET providers identified within Mongolia; no IMCA-registered marine surveyors or vessel vetting services operating domestically; no dedicated inland waterway or occupational marine safety regulatory framework; specialist marine safety equipment (immersion suits, SOLAS-rated life rafts, fast rescue craft) not commercially available in-country.
MN.workaround: If the procedure is determined applicable to any water-adjacent activities at Mongolian operations, mobilise all BOSIET/HUET training through accredited providers in Beijing (China) or Seoul (South Korea); import all required marine safety equipment via project logistics; engage an internationally accredited marine warranty surveyor on a fly-in/fly-out basis from Singapore, Australia, or South Korea; develop a Mongolia-specific marine activity risk assessment and vetting protocol referencing IMO and IMCA standards in the absence of a domestic regulatory framework; obtain project-specific approval from the relevant Mongolian ministry given the absence of applicable legislation.

US.statement: The United States has a comprehensive federal marine safety framework (US Coast Guard, OSHA 1910.132, MSHA where applicable) with BOSIET/HUET providers available in major centres, but the Arizona/Oak Flat operational context is an inland, high-desert environment with no navigable waterways, meaning the applicability of a marine vetting procedure to site-specific water-adjacent activities must first be established, and if applicable, the nearest specialist marine training and equipment sources are in Phoenix or Los Angeles, adding logistical complexity.
US.specific_barrier: Procedure applicability requires verification for the inland Arizona operational context; no commercial marine training providers or IMCA-standard vessel inspection services are located near the Oak Flat/Superior, Arizona project site; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface for water-adjacent activities at surface facilities adds compliance coordination complexity.
US.workaround: Conduct a formal procedure applicability scoping assessment for the Oak Flat operational area to identify any water-crossing, dewatering pond, or tailings storage facility water-adjacent activities that trigger PRO-0030 requirements; if applicable, source BOSIET/HUET training through providers in Phoenix, AZ or Los Angeles, CA; develop a site-specific inland water safety plan referencing USCG, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.132, and ANSI/ASSE Z359 standards; engage an IMCA-registered or USCG-certificated marine surveyor for periodic vetting reviews mobilised to site.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core traffic management controls but faces significant practical barriers including very limited availability of compliant light vehicles with ROPS/seatbelts, speed-limiting devices, and proximity detection systems, compounded by the absence of dedicated ESG or mining traffic safety legislation and selective enforcement creating unpredictable compliance obligations.
GN.specific_barrier: Very limited commercial availability in Guinea of fit-for-purpose mine site vehicles with factory-installed ROPS, four-point harnesses, and speed limiters; proximity detection/collision avoidance systems (e.g. Hexagon, Wenco) have no established local supply chain or maintenance support; no dedicated mining traffic management regulatory standard exists — reliance on generic Mining Code only.
GN.workaround: Operator must import or procure compliant vehicles and proximity detection systems through regional suppliers (e.g. Conakry-based heavy equipment agents or Dakar/Abidjan intermediaries); establish an owner-maintained vehicle inspection and speed limiter calibration programme; develop a bespoke site traffic management plan that references ICMM Good Practice Guidance as the de facto standard in the absence of local legislation; engage with CPDM or Ministry of Mines to document voluntary adoption of international standards as a compliance baseline.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading traffic management standards under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and associated CoPs, with readily available compliant vehicles, traffic management equipment, and a well-established trained workforce.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading traffic management requirements under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, MQSHA Regulations, and RSHQ guidance, with a mature regulatory framework and trained workforce, though limited availability of some specialised traffic management equipment in remote Cape York/Weipa locations may require advance procurement planning.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of specialised traffic management equipment (e.g. collision avoidance systems, automated gate systems, variable message signs) at Weipa/Cape York Peninsula, requiring sourcing from Cairns, Townsville, or Brisbane with associated lead times.
QLD.workaround: Establish a pre-positioned equipment inventory at site and maintain vendor supply agreements with Cairns or Townsville-based suppliers to ensure timely replenishment; factor supply lead times into traffic management planning for the Norman Creek and Kangwinan development phases.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement traffic management controls at a functional level but faces material challenges from limited availability of compliant mine vehicles and proximity detection systems in remote high-altitude Puna locations (~4,000m ASL), inconsistent provincial regulatory requirements across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and no unified national mining traffic safety standard.
AR.specific_barrier: No nationally uniform mining traffic management regulation — provincial primacy means Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca each apply different interpretations of the Mining Code (Law 24,196) and provincial safety decrees; commercial availability of ROPS-compliant LVs and collision avoidance systems is limited in northwest Argentina, with Salta City and Jujuy being the nearest supply centres but with restricted inventories for specialised mining traffic equipment; extreme altitude (4,000m ASL) affects vehicle performance and braking specifications used in standard traffic management plans.
AR.workaround: Develop province-specific traffic management annexes referencing each provincial authority's requirements alongside ICMM/IOGP standards as the baseline; procure compliant vehicles and traffic management systems through Buenos Aires or Chilean border suppliers (Antofagasta corridor) with planned logistics for remote delivery; adjust vehicle stopping-distance and speed limit calculations to account for high-altitude performance degradation; engage provincial mining secretariats proactively to confirm accepted traffic management plan formats.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most traffic management requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), Mining Act, and Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines (ROHSM), with readily available equipment and trained personnel, but ongoing regulatory transition under Bill 63's incomplete implementing regulations and complex interactions with James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations for northern sites create procedural uncertainty that may affect plan approval timelines.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the precise regulatory obligations for site traffic management plans under the reformed Mining Act regime are not yet fully confirmed; BAPE review requirements for new project phases may introduce delays in approving traffic management infrastructure changes; James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement consultation obligations add procedural steps for northern Quebec sites.
CA.workaround: Develop traffic management plan to the current ROHSM standard and ICMM Good Practice Guidance simultaneously to future-proof against Bill 63 finalisation; engage CNESST and MRNF early to confirm interim compliance expectations during the regulatory transition period; build James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement consultation timelines into traffic management plan approval schedules for northern sites.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement baseline traffic management controls but faces significant barriers from limited local availability of compliant mine vehicles and traffic safety systems, a weak and procedurally unclear EIA and operational safety framework, persistent enforcement unpredictability, and limited local supply of proximity detection or collision avoidance technology.
MN.specific_barrier: No Mongolian-language proximity detection or collision avoidance system support infrastructure; no nationally mandated traffic management plan format for mines; limited availability of ROPS/seatbelt-compliant light vehicles outside Ulaanbaatar-based dealers.
MN.workaround: Procure compliant vehicles and proximity detection systems through Ulaanbaatar-based international mining equipment agents (e.g. Caterpillar Mongolia, Komatsu Mongolia) with vendor-supported maintenance contracts; adopt ICMM Good Practice Guidance on surface mine traffic management as the operational standard; develop bilingual (English/Mongolian) traffic management plan to facilitate regulator engagement; maintain buffer stock of critical traffic management components (signage, barriers, speed limiters) on site given supply chain fragility; establish a dedicated internal vehicle inspection programme given limited third-party inspection services.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most traffic management requirements under MSHA 30 CFR Part 56 (surface metal/nonmetal) including haulage road standards, traffic controls, and equipment requirements, with readily available compliant equipment and trained personnel, but the pre-development status of the Resolution Copper project means site-specific MSHA-approved traffic management plans have not yet been developed or tested, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing areas requires careful delineation to avoid compliance gaps.
US.specific_barrier: Resolution Copper project remains in pre-development phase — no site-specific MSHA-approved mine traffic management plan or haulage road design has been submitted or approved; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing and materials handling facilities requires explicit demarcation in traffic management documentation to avoid dual-agency compliance gaps; evolving MSHA enforcement posture under pattern-of-violations provisions creates elevated risk if early traffic management deficiencies are cited.
US.workaround: Develop a comprehensive traffic management plan aligned to MSHA 30 CFR Part 56.9100–56.9340 haulage and access road standards and ICMM guidance during the pre-development phase; conduct a formal OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary assessment and document the interface in the traffic management plan; engage MSHA Metal/Nonmetal district office proactively for pre-operational consultation on traffic management plan adequacy before construction traffic volumes commence.

GN.statement: Guinea can comply with most tools and equipment procedure requirements but faces significant practical barriers due to very limited in-country availability of certified/calibrated tools, specialist inspection services, and no dedicated ESG or equipment safety legislative framework beyond generic Mining and Environmental Codes, meaning procurement and third-party verification must be sourced externally.
GN.specific_barrier: Very limited in-country availability of certified tools, calibrated inspection equipment, and no accredited third-party tool/equipment inspection or testing services — tools requiring statutory inspection (e.g. lifting gear, pressure equipment, insulated electrical tools) cannot be independently verified in-country without importing inspectors or sending equipment abroad.
GN.workaround: Establish a structured importation and pre-deployment inspection programme using accredited third-party inspection bodies (e.g. Bureau Veritas, SGS, Intertek) operating from Dakar or Abidjan to certify equipment before deployment; maintain on-site inventory of critical spare tools to reduce turnaround time; develop a site-specific tool register and internal inspection regime with documented competency to partially substitute for unavailable external services; align procedure documentation to Mining Code generic requirements to satisfy selective enforcement risk.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading tools and equipment procedure requirements, with readily available certified equipment, mature WHS legislative framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act and new WHS Regulations, accredited inspection and testing services widely available across the Pilbara, and a trained workforce — the only noted consideration is that the shift away from prescriptive requirements places greater internal onus on the operator to self-manage risk.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading tools and equipment procedure requirements under the MQSHA framework and relevant plant and equipment standards, with an established multi-agency regulatory structure, available trained workforce, and accessible (if somewhat limited in remote areas) equipment supply chains to Cape York Peninsula — though remote location logistics for Weipa require proactive procurement planning for specialist tools.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited equipment availability in Queensland relative to southern jurisdictions means specialist or calibrated tools may require longer lead times or freight from Brisbane/Townsville to the Cape York Peninsula, creating potential gaps in tool availability during the concurrent East Weipa rehabilitation and new Norman Creek development phase.
QLD.workaround: Maintain elevated on-site tool inventory buffer and scheduled replenishment cycles aligned to barge and freight schedules; establish preferred supplier agreements with Townsville-based industrial equipment distributors; ensure inspection and calibration scheduling accounts for transport lead times to avoid compliance lapses during peak concurrent activity periods.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with most tools and equipment procedure requirements but faces material practical barriers due to limited equipment availability, the extreme high-altitude operating environment (approximately 4,000m ASL) which affects tool performance and maintenance intervals, provincial regulatory inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and import friction for specialised or calibrated equipment under Argentina's historically complex import licensing regime.
AR.specific_barrier: Argentina's import controls and foreign exchange restrictions have historically delayed or prevented timely importation of specialised tools and calibrated inspection equipment; additionally, no established in-province calibration or certified inspection services exist at altitude for specialist mining tools, requiring equipment to be transported to Salta or Buenos Aires for statutory inspection.
AR.workaround: Engage a licensed customs broker experienced in mining equipment importation under Argentina's SIMI/SIRA import authorisation framework; establish a calibration rotation schedule that accounts for transit time to accredited laboratories in Salta or Buenos Aires; procure redundant sets of critical calibrated tools to maintain operational continuity during calibration cycles; apply altitude-adjusted maintenance intervals and manufacturer guidance for tools used above 3,500m ASL; document provincial regulatory requirements separately for each of Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca to manage jurisdictional inconsistency.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most tools and equipment procedure requirements under a mature federal and provincial WHS framework (LSST, RSST, and applicable CSA standards), with readily available equipment and trained workforce, but compliance planning faces uncertainty from Bill 63's incomplete implementing regulations, the ATI requirement for exploration-stage work, and obligations under the James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement for projects in affected northern territories where the procedure may intersect with community engagement requirements.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the full scope of future provincial compliance obligations for equipment management at new mining projects has not yet been finalised, creating uncertainty for procedure design intended to achieve long-term compliance.
CA.workaround: Design the tools and equipment procedure to meet current RSST and CSA standards as a baseline, with a built-in review trigger upon finalisation of Bill 63 regulations; engage Quebec legal counsel to monitor regulatory gazette publications and draft regulation consultations; ensure procedure scope explicitly addresses JBNQA community consultation obligations where tool and equipment activities intersect with impact-causing exploration work under the May 2024 ATI requirement.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with most tools and equipment procedure requirements but faces meaningful barriers from limited in-country availability of certified and calibrated tools, a weak and operationally unclear EIA and equipment safety enforcement framework, legislative instability creating unpredictable compliance obligations, and persistent implementation gaps that mean formal compliance on paper does not reliably translate to consistent on-the-ground enforcement or third-party verification capability.
MN.specific_barrier: No verified accredited in-country calibration or third-party inspection services for specialist mining tools and lifting equipment exist outside Ulaanbaatar, meaning equipment used at remote sites (e.g. Oyu Tolgoi corridor) requires lengthy transport to the capital for statutory inspection, and the EIA framework lacks operational procedures that would clarify equipment-related environmental compliance triggers.
MN.workaround: Establish a dual inspection regime: use international accredited inspection bodies (e.g. Bureau Veritas Mongolia, SGS Mongolia — both have Ulaanbaatar presence) for statutory tool and lifting equipment certification on a scheduled rotation; maintain site-level internal inspection records to bridge gaps between formal inspection cycles; build election-period procurement buffers into tool supply planning to mitigate approval and import stalls; align procedure documentation to both the 2006 Minerals Law (as amended) and Labour Safety Law requirements to address overlapping provisions; monitor legislative amendment activity and build a procedure review cadence around it.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most tools and equipment procedure requirements under well-defined MSHA (30 CFR Parts 56/57) and OSHA standards with readily available equipment and trained workforce, but the pre-development status of the Resolution Copper project at Oak Flat means site-specific MSHA compliance has not yet been established in practice, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful delineation within the procedure to avoid regulatory gaps.
US.specific_barrier: The OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities is not self-executing and requires explicit site-specific demarcation — tools and equipment used in areas where jurisdiction is ambiguous (e.g. crusher facilities, conveyor transfer points) risk being subject to conflicting standards or inspection gaps if the procedure does not explicitly address which regulator's equipment standards apply at each work area.
US.workaround: Conduct a formal OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional mapping exercise for all surface facilities as part of pre-operational planning, documented in a site-specific jurisdictional matrix appended to the procedure; specify applicable equipment standards (29 CFR 1910/1926 vs 30 CFR 56/57) by work area; engage MSHA's Metal and Nonmetal district office early to confirm inspection expectations; incorporate the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) requirements into tool selection and dust-generating equipment controls within the procedure; ensure tools and equipment records are maintained to the standard required for MSHA pattern-of-violations defence.

GN.statement: Guinea can partially comply with an industry-leading Security Management Procedure, but the Voluntary Principles on Security and Human Rights (VPSHR) implementation is severely complicated by military governance context, selective enforcement, weak rule of law, and limited availability of trained private security providers certified to international standards.
GN.specific_barrier: No domestic regulatory framework mandating VPSHR or equivalent human rights-based security standards; no verified pool of licensed private security contractors trained to international standards (e.g., ICoCA-accredited providers) operating in Guinea; military governance creates inherent risk that state security force engagement protocols required under VPSHR cannot be reliably implemented or monitored.
GN.workaround: Engage an ICoCA-accredited international private security provider capable of deploying to Guinea; develop a bespoke VPSHR implementation plan including documented public security engagement protocols with Guinean military/gendarmerie; implement robust grievance mechanism and third-party human rights monitoring; accept that full VPSHR compliance cannot be externally verified given the governance environment and document residual risk accordingly.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with an industry-leading Security Management Procedure, with a mature legal framework, readily available ICoCA-accredited or equivalent private security providers, clear WA Police engagement protocols, and established VPSHR alignment practices among major mining operators in the Pilbara.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with an industry-leading Security Management Procedure; licensed security providers operating under the Security Industry Act 2003 (Qld) are available, relevant legislative obligations are well-defined, and the remote Weipa location is serviced by established security contractors with Cape York Peninsula operational experience.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with most requirements of an industry-leading Security Management Procedure, but the extreme remoteness and high-altitude Puna environment (~4,000m ASL) in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca significantly limits the availability of international-standard private security providers, and provincial-level inconsistency in security licensing and oversight creates compliance gaps relative to VPSHR requirements.
AR.specific_barrier: No verified ICoCA-accredited private security provider with established operational presence at ~4,000m ASL in the Puna; provincial variability in private security licensing standards means provider quality cannot be assumed consistent across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca; community tension around lithium brine projects and the Glacier Law dispute increases security risk profile without a commensurate increase in qualified local provider capacity.
AR.workaround: Contract an internationally accredited security firm (ICoCA member) with Latin American high-altitude operational capability; supplement with trained in-house site security personnel; develop province-specific VPSHR engagement protocols reflecting differing provincial regulatory environments; establish formal community liaison mechanisms to reduce tension-driven security incidents.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most requirements of an industry-leading Security Management Procedure under Canadian federal and provincial frameworks, but James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement (JBNQA) obligations and the complexity of operating in Cree and Inuit territories require specific adaptations to standard security protocols, particularly regarding community access rights and Indigenous consent processes.
CA.specific_barrier: JBNQA obligations and Bill 5 intersections with northern territory governance create procedural requirements around security arrangements that may affect site access control protocols; incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations mean the full scope of community engagement obligations that interact with security planning (e.g., access restrictions, grievance mechanisms) is not yet finalised as of March 2026.
CA.workaround: Engage legal counsel specialising in JBNQA to map security procedure requirements against treaty obligations before finalising access control and exclusion zone protocols; monitor Bill 63 regulation finalisation and update procedure accordingly; involve Cree and Inuit rights-holders in development of site access and security engagement protocols to ensure FPIC alignment.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with core elements of an industry-leading Security Management Procedure, but legislative instability, weak enforcement institutions, limited availability of internationally trained private security providers, and the practical difficulty of engaging state security forces in a principled VPSHR-aligned manner given governance unpredictability all represent material compliance gaps.
MN.specific_barrier: No verified ICoCA-accredited private security provider with established Mongolian operations identified; domestic private security industry operates under the Law on Private Detective and Security Activities but is not demonstrably trained to VPSHR or international mining security standards; regulatory unpredictability means that security arrangements approved under current frameworks may be subject to abrupt change.
MN.workaround: Import or contract an internationally accredited security management firm to design the security programme and train local guards; implement a documented VPSHR-aligned public security engagement protocol with the Mongolian National Police; establish an independent incident reporting and grievance mechanism; build regulatory change triggers into the procedure to allow rapid adaptation if legislation shifts.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most requirements of an industry-leading Security Management Procedure, but the unresolved Indigenous rights dispute at Oak Flat and sustained opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe and national organisations create a materially elevated human rights risk context that standard security management procedures do not adequately address without specific VPSHR and FPIC adaptations.
US.specific_barrier: The sacred significance of Oak Flat to the San Carlos Apache Tribe and the absence of a completed, good-faith FPIC process means that conventional site access control and exclusion zone security measures risk being characterised as instruments of rights suppression, exposing the operator to legal challenge, reputational damage, and potential interference with social licence; no industry-standard security procedure designed for a non-contested site will be adequate without specific human rights impact assessment integration.
US.workaround: Commission a standalone Human Rights Impact Assessment (HRIA) specifically addressing security arrangements at Oak Flat; develop a VPSHR-compliant security plan with documented engagement with the San Carlos Apache Tribe and other affected Indigenous communities; establish an independent, accessible grievance mechanism; limit use of state security forces and document all interactions per VPSHR requirements; engage an independent human rights monitor.

PRO-0034.procedure_title: Water Quality Protection and Water Management Procedure

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core water quality protection measures but faces significant barriers including the absence of dedicated water quality standards for mining operations, selective and unpredictable enforcement under the generic Environmental Code, very limited availability of laboratory analytical equipment and certified water testing facilities in-country, and weak institutional capacity for independent EIA oversight of water management plans.
GN.specific_barrier: No certified in-country water quality laboratory network capable of supporting industry-standard monitoring frequencies; no dedicated mining water quality regulatory instrument exists — compliance must be mapped against generic Environmental Code provisions with no ESG-specific guidance; field water quality monitoring equipment (multiparameter sondes, portable turbidimeters, flow meters) has very limited commercial availability in Guinea.
GN.workaround: Engage accredited regional laboratory (e.g., in Dakar or Abidjan) for periodic sample analysis with chain-of-custody protocols; procure and import field monitoring equipment through international suppliers; develop a site-specific water management plan that maps controls to both the Mining Code and Environmental Code to create a defensible compliance record; retain an independent environmental consultant to provide third-party verification given weak institutional oversight; build internal analytical capacity through a dedicated site laboratory where feasible.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a comprehensive and well-established water quality and water management regulatory framework under the Rights in Water and Irrigation Act 1914, Environment Protection Act 1986, and associated DWER policies, with readily available equipment, trained workforce, and mature industry practice, though Rhodes Ridge site-specific water management obligations remain undefined pending EPA referral and approval.

QLD.statement: Queensland provides a mature and well-defined water quality and water management regulatory framework under the Environmental Protection Act 1994, Water Act 2000, and associated DETSI/DESI policies applicable to mining operations at Weipa, with trained personnel and adequate equipment availability, though the concurrent East Weipa rehabilitation closure obligations and Norman Creek development approvals add procedural complexity to water management planning.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement water quality protection measures but faces material regulatory uncertainty arising from the unresolved legal interaction between the Wetlands Law and Puna salar environments, the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment transferring protected-area designation to financially conflicted provincial governments, extreme operational conditions at approximately 4,000m ASL affecting monitoring reliability and sampling logistics, and inconsistent provincial regulatory capacity across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca for water management plan review and enforcement.
AR.specific_barrier: Unresolved legal status of wetlands and glacier buffer zone designations in Puna salar environments creates uncertainty about which water bodies require protection under which instrument and which authority has jurisdiction; limited availability of accredited water quality laboratories capable of analysing brine-chemistry parameters at high-altitude remote locations; provincial regulatory inconsistency means water management plan requirements and approval timelines vary unpredictably across the three relevant provinces.
AR.workaround: Commission specialist hydrogeological and brine-chemistry studies to characterise baseline water quality independent of regulatory outcome; engage provincial environmental agencies in each of Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca early to agree site-specific water management plan content and approval pathways; use accredited Argentine national laboratories (CONICET-affiliated or INTI) for analytical support with supplementary international laboratory verification; design water management plans to the most stringent applicable provincial standard to create a defensible baseline regardless of jurisdictional determination; monitor Wetlands Law and Glacier Law legal challenges and adapt accordingly.

CA.statement: Quebec has a substantive water quality and water management regulatory framework under the Environment Quality Act and the Water Withdrawal and Protection Regulation, with readily available equipment and trained workforce, but compliance planning is complicated by incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations as of March 2026, the interaction of new mandatory BAPE environmental assessment requirements with water management plan approvals, and unresolved federal-provincial jurisdictional boundaries following the Supreme Court's October 2023 Impact Assessment Act ruling for projects in James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement territories.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft consultation as of March 2026, meaning the final regulatory requirements for water management under the reformed Mining Act regime are not yet confirmed; federal-provincial jurisdictional uncertainty post-SCC October 2023 ruling creates ambiguity about which environmental assessment triggers apply to water management components of projects in affected territories; ATI requirement for exploration-stage water-affecting activities adds permitting steps not previously required.
CA.workaround: Design water management plans to satisfy both existing EQA requirements and the anticipated Bill 63 reformed regime to avoid re-approval; engage MELCCFP early to clarify which water withdrawal and protection regulations apply to specific project phases; monitor Bill 63 regulation finalisation and update water management plans accordingly; for James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement territory projects, obtain specific legal advice on federal versus provincial water quality jurisdiction post-SCC ruling and engage Cree Nation governance bodies proactively on water-related concerns.

MN.statement: Mongolia has formal water quality and mining water management obligations under the Water Law 2012, Law on Environmental Protection, and Law on Environmental Impact Assessment, but effective compliance is materially undermined by an EIA framework characterised by UNDP as lacking operational procedures and national criteria, persistent weak enforcement, limited availability of certified water quality monitoring equipment and accredited laboratories outside Ulaanbaatar, and legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law creating overlapping and sometimes contradictory obligations.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks clear operational procedures and national criteria for water quality assessment in mining contexts per UNDP assessment, meaning there is no reliable regulatory standard against which to calibrate a water management plan; accredited water quality laboratories with capability for full suite mining-relevant parameters (heavy metals, TDS, turbidity, pH, conductivity) are concentrated in Ulaanbaatar and may lack capacity or turnaround times suitable for operational monitoring at remote mine sites; field water quality monitoring equipment has limited in-country commercial availability.
MN.workaround: Develop a site-specific water quality monitoring plan referencing international standards (ICMM Water Stewardship, IFC Performance Standard 6) to fill the gap where national criteria are absent; establish a contract with the most capable accredited Ulaanbaatar laboratory with defined turnaround times and chain-of-custody protocols, supplemented by portable field instrumentation maintained on site; import calibrated multiparameter monitoring equipment through international suppliers; build internal water quality monitoring capacity at site level to reduce dependence on external laboratory turnaround; document all monitoring results comprehensively to demonstrate good-faith compliance independent of enforcement consistency.

US.statement: The United States has a highly developed water quality regulatory framework applicable to mining under the Clean Water Act (including Section 404/401 permitting), Safe Drinking Water Act, CERCLA, and Arizona state water law, with readily available equipment and trained workforce, but the Oak Flat project remains in pre-development phase with site-specific water quality permits not yet obtained, complex overlapping federal and state water quality jurisdictions must be navigated concurrently, and unresolved Indigenous community opposition creates social licence risk that could affect water-related permitting timelines and public comment processes.
US.specific_barrier: Site-specific Clean Water Act Section 404 discharge permits and Section 401 state water quality certifications have not yet been obtained for Oak Flat, meaning the actual water quality protection obligations and any site-specific conditions imposed by EPA or the Army Corps of Engineers are not yet defined; the block cave mining method proposed at Resolution Copper creates subsidence-related groundwater connectivity risks that will require complex hydrogeological modelling and water management planning not yet completed; San Carlos Apache Tribe opposition may generate substantive objections during Clean Water Act public comment periods, potentially delaying or conditioning permits.
US.workaround: Commission comprehensive pre-permit baseline water quality characterisation across all potentially affected water bodies including groundwater, surface water, and springs of cultural significance; engage EPA Region 9 and Army Corps of Engineers early to understand likely Section 404/401 permit conditions; develop a detailed hydrogeological model addressing subsidence-related groundwater impacts specific to block cave method; engage San Carlos Apache Tribe formally on water quality concerns with genuine consultation to reduce the risk of substantive Clean Water Act objections; design water management controls to exceed minimum Clean Water Act standards to strengthen permit applications and reduce legal challenge risk.

GN.statement: Guinea can meet basic air quality monitoring obligations under the generic Environmental Code but lacks any dedicated ESG or air quality-specific regulatory framework, calibrated dust monitoring equipment is very difficult to source locally, and selective enforcement means compliance outcomes are unpredictable regardless of procedural rigour.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated air quality or ESG-specific regulatory instrument exists; calibrated particulate matter monitors (e.g. DustTrak, OSIRIS, or equivalent real-time dust monitors) and laboratory-grade isokinetic stack sampling equipment are not commercially available in Guinea and must be imported; no accredited environmental testing laboratories in-country for air quality sample analysis.
GN.workaround: Procedure must be adapted to reference the generic Environmental Code and Mining Code as the applicable regulatory baseline; dust monitoring equipment must be sourced through international procurement and maintained under contract with an external service provider; laboratory analysis should be routed through accredited facilities in Conakry or internationally (e.g. South Africa or France); a self-imposed internal air quality standard aligned with IFC Performance Standard 3 and WHO Air Quality Guidelines should substitute for absent national standards to provide a defensible compliance benchmark.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a comprehensive and well-enforced air quality regulatory framework under the Environmental Protection Act 1986, the Environmental Protection (Noise) Regulations, DMIRS dust guidance, and EPA assessment processes, with ready availability of accredited monitoring equipment, certified environmental consultants, and a mature compliance infrastructure.

QLD.statement: Queensland operates a mature air quality compliance regime under the Environmental Protection Act 1994, Environmental Protection (Air) Policy 2008, and associated Environmental Authority conditions, with DETSI oversight and readily accessible monitoring equipment and environmental services, noting that limited equipment availability in remote Cape York Peninsula locations requires logistics planning but does not constitute a structural barrier.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of specialised air quality monitoring equipment and accredited environmental service providers in the remote Weipa/Cape York Peninsula location, requiring advance logistics and procurement planning.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with air quality requirements at a functional level, but provincial primacy means applicable standards and approval processes differ materially across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and the extreme high-altitude operational environment (~4,000m ASL) creates practical challenges for equipment calibration, personnel health monitoring, and monitoring instrument performance that require explicit procedural adaptation.
AR.specific_barrier: No single unified national air quality standard applies to mining operations — each province sets its own emission limits and monitoring requirements, creating inconsistency; standard particulate matter monitoring instruments require altitude and pressure correction at ~4,000m ASL as factory calibrations are typically validated at sea level, and some optical instruments underperform in high-altitude arid conditions; limited availability of accredited air quality testing laboratories in the Puna region.
AR.workaround: Procedure must be jurisdictionally tiered to reference the applicable provincial environmental legislation and emission standards for each province (Ley General del Ambiente 25.675 as national baseline, plus provincial equivalents); instrument calibration protocols must include altitude correction factors; laboratory analysis should be routed to accredited facilities in Salta, Tucumán, or Buenos Aires; the procedure should adopt IFC PS3 and WHO guidelines as the de facto standard where provincial requirements are less stringent or ambiguous.

CA.statement: Quebec has a functional air quality regulatory framework under the Environment Quality Act (EQA) and the Regulation respecting the quality of the atmosphere (RQCA), with well-equipped and trained personnel available, but Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning site-specific environmental authorisation conditions for new projects are not yet fully defined, and BAPE review requirements for new mining projects add procedural uncertainty to approval timelines.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations are not yet fully operationalised as of March 2026, meaning the reformed authorisation regime — including how air quality conditions are set and enforced for new mining projects — remains partially undefined; the new BAPE mandatory review process for all new mining projects introduces procedural delay risk for obtaining site-specific air quality authorisation conditions.
CA.workaround: Procedure should reference both the current EQA and RQCA as the operative baseline and flag that site-specific Environmental Authorisation (EA) conditions under the evolving Bill 63 regime will supersede generic requirements once issued; proactive pre-consultation with the Ministère de l'Environnement, de la Lutte contre les changements climatiques, de la Faune et des Parcs (MELCCFP) is recommended to clarify applicable air quality conditions ahead of formal authorisation; procedure should be structured for easy amendment once Bill 63 regulations are finalised.

MN.statement: Mongolia has formal air quality obligations under the Law on Air (2012, amended) and EIA framework, but the EIA framework lacks clear national criteria and operational procedures per UNDP assessment, limited availability of calibrated monitoring equipment and accredited laboratories outside Ulaanbaatar creates practical compliance gaps, and legislative instability and weak enforcement mean the regulatory baseline is unreliable for long-term compliance planning.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks operational procedures and national air quality criteria for mining specifically, per UNDP assessment; calibrated real-time particulate matter monitoring equipment (e.g. DustTrak, TEOM, or equivalent) has limited commercial availability outside Ulaanbaatar, and there are no accredited environmental laboratories in the South Gobi region capable of performing stack emission or ambient air quality analysis to international standards; frequent legislative amendments create uncertainty about which emission standards are currently operative.
MN.workaround: Procedure should adopt IFC Performance Standard 3 and WHO Air Quality Guidelines as the primary compliance benchmark to fill the gap left by absent or unclear national criteria; monitoring equipment must be sourced from Ulaanbaatar or internationally and maintained under a specialist service contract; laboratory samples should be sent to accredited facilities in Ulaanbaatar (e.g. National Centre for Laboratory) or internationally; procedure should include a regulatory tracking mechanism to capture frequent legislative amendments and assess impact on compliance obligations.

US.statement: The United States has a rigorous and well-enforced air quality regulatory framework applicable to the Resolution Copper project, including the Clean Air Act (National Ambient Air Quality Standards), EPA permitting (Title V and/or Prevention of Significant Deterioration), the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule reducing the permissible exposure limit to 50 µg/m³, and Arizona state implementation plan requirements, with readily available monitoring equipment and trained personnel, but the project remains in pre-development and site-specific permit conditions have not yet been established, requiring the procedure to be designed for future compliance obligations not yet fully defined.
US.specific_barrier: The 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (reducing the PEL to 50 µg/m³, with full enforcement phased from 2024–2026) represents a materially more stringent occupational air quality standard than the previous 100 µg/m³ PEL, requiring investment in enhanced dust suppression, engineering controls, and real-time personal exposure monitoring systems that must be factored into procedure design; site-specific Title V / PSD air quality permit conditions for Resolution Copper have not yet been issued, meaning the full regulatory compliance profile cannot be completely defined at this stage.
US.workaround: Procedure should be structured around the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule 50 µg/m³ PEL as the operative occupational standard from the outset; it should reference NAAQS and Arizona SIP requirements as the ambient air quality baseline and include a placeholder framework for site-specific permit conditions to be incorporated once the EPA/ADEQ permitting process is complete; continuous real-time personal dust exposure monitoring should be specified as the minimum monitoring standard to meet the more stringent silica PEL.

PRO-0036.procedure_title: Chemically Reactive Mineral Waste Control Procedure

GN.statement: Guinea lacks any dedicated ESG or acid mine drainage (AMD) / chemically reactive waste legislation, specialist geochemical laboratory capacity for ARD characterisation is absent in-country, and selective enforcement means even good-faith compliance cannot guarantee regulatory protection.
GN.specific_barrier: No in-country accredited geochemical testing laboratories capable of acid-base accounting (ABA) or net acid generation (NAG) testing for ARD characterisation; no dedicated regulatory framework for chemically reactive mineral waste management beyond generic Mining and Environmental Code provisions.
GN.workaround: Samples must be shipped to accredited international laboratories (e.g., South Africa, France, or Australia) for ABA/NAG/NAPP testing, adding cost and turnaround time; a site-specific Acid Rock Drainage Management Plan must be developed to international standards (e.g., GARD Guide, MAC/INAP framework) and adopted as the de facto compliance instrument in the absence of national regulation; engage DNEF and the Ministry of Mines proactively to seek written endorsement of the international-standard plan to reduce selective enforcement risk.

WA.statement: Western Australia provides a comprehensive and well-established regulatory framework for chemically reactive mineral waste control under the Mining Rehabilitation Fund Act, Environmental Protection Act, and DMIRS guidelines (including the Acid Mine Drainage guidelines), with readily available specialist geochemical consultants and laboratory services.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a mature regulatory framework for acid mine drainage and chemically reactive waste under the Environmental Protection Act 1994, the Environmental Protection (Waste Management) Policy, and DESI/DETSI guidelines, but limited local availability of specialist geochemical testing equipment and contractors in the remote Weipa/Cape York Peninsula region adds practical compliance complexity.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited availability of specialist acid-base accounting laboratory services and geochemical consultants in the Cape York Peninsula region, requiring mobilisation from Cairns, Brisbane, or interstate, increasing response time for reactive waste characterisation during active mining operations.
QLD.workaround: Establish standing service agreements with accredited geochemical laboratories in Brisbane or Townsville for sample dispatch and turnaround; maintain a pre-characterised waste rock database for known ore types at Weipa to reduce dependency on real-time testing; align chemically reactive waste management plan with the concurrent East Weipa rehabilitation programme to demonstrate integrated compliance to DETSI.

AR.statement: Argentina's Mining Code (Law 24.196) and provincial environmental regulations address mineral waste management in principle, but the constitutional primacy of provinces creates inconsistent application across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, specialist geochemical laboratory capacity is limited domestically, and the extreme high-altitude operational environment (approx. 4,000m ASL) complicates reactive waste monitoring and containment infrastructure.
AR.specific_barrier: No nationally standardised acid mine drainage or chemically reactive waste management guideline equivalent to international best practice; inconsistent provincial regulatory requirements mean that a compliant approach in one province may not satisfy obligations in another; limited in-country accredited geochemical laboratory capacity for ABA/NAG analysis.
AR.workaround: Develop a single international-standard Chemically Reactive Waste Management Plan (referencing GARD Guide and MAC/INAP) as the baseline document, then layer province-specific regulatory requirements on top for each operational province; contract geochemical testing through Argentine universities (e.g., UBA, CONICET-affiliated labs) or ship samples to Chilean or Brazilian accredited laboratories; engage provincial environmental agencies in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca individually to confirm acceptance of the international-standard approach.

CA.statement: Quebec has substantive mineral waste management obligations under the Environment Quality Act (Title I and II), the Mining Act (as amended by Bill 63), and the Directive 019 sur l'industrie minière, with readily available specialist geochemical services, but incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations and the pending BAPE review requirement for new project phases create regulatory uncertainty around final approval of chemically reactive waste management plans.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the precise regulatory requirements for chemically reactive mineral waste management under the reformed Mining Act regime are not yet fully operationalised, creating uncertainty about what the final compliance standard will require for new project phases.
CA.workaround: Apply Directive 019 and existing Environment Quality Act obligations as the current compliance baseline while monitoring draft regulations through the 45-day public consultation process; engage MELCCFP proactively to confirm acceptance of the international-standard ARD management approach (GARD Guide / MAC/INAP) as interim compliance; ensure chemically reactive waste characterisation and management plans are sufficiently robust to withstand BAPE scrutiny when mandatory provincial EA is triggered for new development phases.

MN.statement: Mongolia has mineral waste and environmental protection obligations under the Law on Environmental Impact Assessment and the Law on Minerals, but the EIA framework lacks clear operational procedures and national criteria for chemically reactive waste characterisation per UNDP assessment, local geochemical laboratory capacity is limited, and legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law creates compliance planning uncertainty.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks national criteria and clear agency procedures specifically governing acid mine drainage and chemically reactive mineral waste characterisation and management, meaning there is no defined regulatory standard to comply with; limited in-country accredited laboratory capacity for acid-base accounting or net acid generation testing.
MN.workaround: Adopt the GARD Guide (Global Acid Rock Drainage Guide) and MAC/INAP international framework as the operative technical standard in the absence of national criteria, and document this approach explicitly in the EIA and Environmental Management Plan submitted to MEGDT; ship geochemical samples to accredited laboratories in Australia, South Korea, or China for ABA/NAG testing; engage MEGDT and the Minerals Resources Authority of Mongolia (MRAM) proactively to secure written confirmation that the international-standard plan satisfies regulatory requirements, to provide protection during periods of regulatory unpredictability.

US.statement: The United States has a well-developed federal and state regulatory framework for chemically reactive mineral waste control under SMCRA, the Clean Water Act (NPDES permitting), EPA Subtitle D solid waste regulations, and MSHA standards, with readily available specialist geochemical services, but the Oak Flat project remains in pre-development phase with no site-specific compliance track record established and complex overlapping federal and state permitting requirements under NEPA and the APA.
US.specific_barrier: The Resolution Copper project at Oak Flat is still in pre-development/NEPA review phase, meaning site-specific chemically reactive mineral waste management plans have not yet been reviewed and approved by EPA, MSHA, or the relevant state agencies; the specific waste characterisation baseline for the underground block cave ore body and associated waste rock has not been publicly finalised in a regulatory-approved document.
US.workaround: Develop and submit a comprehensive Acid Mine Drainage Prediction and Management Plan as part of the NEPA Environmental Impact Statement, referencing EPA's Technical Document on Acid Mine Drainage Prediction and SMCRA requirements; ensure the plan addresses Arizona ADEQ solid waste and water quality permit requirements concurrently; establish engagement with EPA Region 9 and MSHA Metal/Nonmetal District to align on acceptable technical standards ahead of formal permit submission to avoid iterative review delays.

PRO-0037.procedure_title: Land Disturbance Control and Rehabilitation Procedure

GN.statement: Guinea can partially comply with land disturbance control and rehabilitation requirements through its Mining Code and Environmental Code, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG or rehabilitation-specific legislation, selective enforcement, unpredictable approval timelines, and very limited availability of specialised rehabilitation equipment (e.g., tracked seeding machinery, erosion control geotextiles, topsoil management plant) prevent full industry-leading compliance.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated rehabilitation or land disturbance control regulatory framework exists beyond generic Mining Code provisions; very limited in-country availability of specialised rehabilitation equipment such as hydraulic seeders, geotextile erosion matting, and compaction monitoring instruments; and selective enforcement means rehabilitation bond or closure plan requirements may not be consistently applied or verified.
GN.workaround: Develop a standalone internal Land Disturbance and Rehabilitation Management Plan that references and interprets Mining Code and Environmental Code obligations to fill the legislative gap; procure specialist rehabilitation equipment and seed stock from Conakry or import from Dakar/Abidjan; engage a recognised international environmental consultancy to design and audit progressive rehabilitation milestones; and establish self-imposed bond or financial assurance mechanism to demonstrate commitment independent of inconsistent state enforcement.
