WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading land disturbance control and rehabilitation requirements, supported by one of the world's most mature mining rehabilitation frameworks including the Mining Rehabilitation Fund, mandatory progressive rehabilitation under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act and Mining Act 1978, DMIRS/successor agency oversight, and readily available equipment and trained specialists.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading land disturbance control and rehabilitation requirements under the Environmental Protection Act 1994 (including progressive rehabilitation obligation under PRPs), the Mineral Resources Act, and concurrent East Weipa rehabilitation obligations providing practical compliance experience, though limited availability of some specialised rehabilitation equipment in the remote Cape York Peninsula may require forward logistics planning.
QLD.specific_barrier: Remote Cape York Peninsula location creates logistical constraints on timely delivery of specialised rehabilitation materials (e.g., certified native seed stock, erosion control products, compaction testing equipment) and reduces frequency of regulatory inspection, which may slow corrective action cycles.
QLD.workaround: Establish pre-positioned rehabilitation material stockpiles at site; develop a site-specific Progressive Rehabilitation and Closure Plan (PRCP) that accounts for seasonality and access constraints; leverage lessons from concurrent East Weipa mine cessation rehabilitation programme to build internal capability; and engage NRMMRRD early on approval pathways for the Norman Creek and Kangwinan developments to integrate disturbance footprint planning.

AR.statement: Argentina can partially comply with land disturbance control and rehabilitation requirements through Mining Code Article 250 environmental protection obligations and provincial environmental licensing regimes, but the unresolved Glacier Law/wetlands interface for Puna salares, conflicted provincial authority under the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment, inconsistent cross-provincial regulatory application across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and the extreme high-altitude operational environment create material gaps preventing full industry-leading compliance.
AR.specific_barrier: The April 2026 Glacier Law amendment transferring protected-area designation to financially conflicted provincial governments creates unresolvable legal ambiguity over which land areas can be disturbed and rehabilitated in the Puna; the unresolved Wetlands Law interaction with salares means rehabilitation success criteria for brine operation footprints cannot be definitively established; and at approximately 4,000m ASL, revegetation and topsoil rehabilitation are technically constrained by extreme aridity, frost, and limited native seed stock availability.
AR.workaround: Commission a jurisdiction-specific legal opinion mapping the current state of Glacier Law, Wetlands Law, and provincial environmental licence requirements for each of Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca; develop conservative disturbance footprint minimisation controls that remain defensible under any foreseeable regulatory interpretation; engage IANIGLA and provincial authorities jointly to pre-agree rehabilitation success criteria; source specialised high-altitude native seed stock and consult Instituto Nacional de Tecnología Agropecuaria (INTA) on revegetation feasibility at altitude; and implement adaptive rehabilitation monitoring with documented contingency protocols.

CA.statement: Quebec can substantially comply with land disturbance control and rehabilitation requirements under the Mining Act (as amended by Bill 63), the Environment Quality Act, and the Financial Guarantee for Mine Site Rehabilitation regime, but incomplete implementing regulations under Bill 63 as of March 2026, mandatory BAPE review for new project phases, the new ATI requirement for impact-causing exploration, and unresolved federal-provincial assessment interaction following the October 2023 Supreme Court ruling introduce procedural uncertainty that prevents full compliance certainty for planned new project phases.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft consultation as of March 2026, meaning the precise rehabilitation plan content requirements, financial guarantee calculation methodology, and progressive rehabilitation milestone obligations under the reformed regime are not yet legally confirmed; the mandatory BAPE review process for new mining phases adds a procedurally uncertain timeline to disturbance authorisation.
CA.workaround: Design land disturbance control and rehabilitation plans to the most stringent interpretation of both existing Mining Act requirements and anticipated Bill 63 obligations to be regulation-ready upon finalisation; engage MERN and MELCCFP proactively to clarify expected plan content standards under the incoming regime; ensure financial guarantee provisions are conservatively calculated to accommodate possible upward revision; and for James Bay territory operations, conduct early consultation under JBNQA to identify any specific land rehabilitation obligations under Title II of the EQA.

MN.statement: Mongolia can partially comply with land disturbance control and rehabilitation requirements through obligations under the 2012 Law on Environmental Impact Assessment and the Minerals Law (as amended), including prescribed reclamation fund contributions and post-mining land restoration duties, but the EIA framework's lack of operational procedures and national criteria, persistent weak enforcement, legislative instability from over 100 Minerals Law amendments, and limited in-country availability of specialised rehabilitation equipment prevent full industry-leading compliance.
MN.specific_barrier: The UNDP-assessed absence of operational procedures, national rehabilitation success criteria, and clear agency procedures within the EIA framework means there is no defined regulatory benchmark against which industry-leading rehabilitation performance can be formally verified; limited in-country availability of specialised land rehabilitation equipment such as hydroseeders, geotextile erosion control systems, and precision topsoil reinstatement machinery requires importation; and election-cycle permitting stalls may interrupt progressive rehabilitation approval workflows.
MN.workaround: Adopt an internationally recognised rehabilitation standard (e.g., ICMM Mine Closure Framework or IFC Performance Standard 6) as the internal compliance benchmark in the absence of defined national criteria; document all progressive rehabilitation activities against this standard to demonstrate good-faith compliance and build a verifiable track record; import or contract specialist rehabilitation equipment from China or South Korea; establish rehabilitation plan approval milestones that anticipate election-period delays with adequate schedule float; and engage the Ministry of Environment and Climate Change proactively to validate rehabilitation success criteria in writing at the outset of operations.

US.statement: The United States can largely comply with land disturbance control and rehabilitation requirements under SMCRA (where applicable), NEPA environmental impact obligations, EPA stormwater and erosion control regulations (including NPDES Construction General Permit), and MSHA surface disturbance standards, but the Oak Flat project remains in pre-development with no established operational compliance track record, unresolved Indigenous sacred site and FPIC disputes create social licence risk that could disrupt disturbance authorisations, and complex overlapping federal and state permit requirements add procedural complexity.
US.specific_barrier: Sustained San Carlos Apache Tribe opposition and unresolved FPIC obligations create a material risk that land disturbance authorisations and associated NEPA Records of Decision could face successful legal challenge, effectively blocking or interrupting disturbance activity approval regardless of technical compliance quality; the pre-development status means site-specific NPDES, reclamation bond, and stormwater pollution prevention plan (SWPPP) obligations under EPA and relevant state agency have not yet been operationally tested.
US.workaround: Implement a robust Free, Prior and Informed Consent process and government-to-government consultation framework that goes beyond minimum legal requirements, documented transparently to reduce legal challenge risk; develop a comprehensive SWPPP, reclamation bond, and progressive land disturbance control plan to SMCRA and EPA best-practice standards in advance of first disturbance; engage legal counsel experienced in federal NEPA and NHPA Section 106 consultation to stress-test the disturbance authorisation pathway; and establish a third-party environmental monitor acceptable to tribal stakeholders to build trust in on-ground rehabilitation performance.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core biodiversity protection measures through its Environmental Code and Mining Code, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG or biodiversity-specific legislation, selective enforcement, and very limited availability of qualified ecological survey equipment and specialist biodiversity consultants in-country means that industry-leading biodiversity offsetting, no-net-loss frameworks, and credible baseline surveys cannot be fully implemented without significant external resourcing.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated biodiversity or ESG legislation exists; no in-country availability of specialist ecological survey equipment (e.g., acoustic bat detectors, eDNA sampling kits, camera trap networks) or accredited biodiversity consultants capable of delivering IUCN-aligned biodiversity assessments or Biodiversity Management Plans to international standard.
GN.workaround: Engage internationally accredited ecological consultants (e.g., from Dakar, Abidjan, or Europe) to conduct baseline surveys and develop a Biodiversity Management Plan aligned with IFC Performance Standard 6 and the Mitigation Hierarchy; use satellite remote sensing and drone-based habitat mapping to partially compensate for equipment gaps; operate under IFC PS6 as the de facto compliance standard in the absence of domestic ESG instruments; document all biodiversity commitments within the ESIA to create a contractual compliance anchor given weak domestic enforcement.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a comprehensive and well-resourced biodiversity compliance framework under the Environmental Protection Act 1986, Biodiversity Conservation Act 2016, and EPA referral processes, with readily available specialist ecological consultants, survey equipment, and established offset frameworks, and although Rhodes Ridge is in pre-development EPA referral stage with cumulative effects scrutiny adding complexity, no structural barrier to full compliance exists.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a mature biodiversity compliance framework under the Nature Conservation Act 1992, Biodiversity Act 2004 (offsets), Environmental Protection Act 1994, and the Reef 2050 Plan obligations where applicable, with trained ecological consultants and survey equipment readily available and established biodiversity offset registers, though equipment listed as limited availability may create minor resourcing friction for simultaneous survey campaigns across multiple project phases at Weipa.
QLD.specific_barrier: Equipment listed as limited availability in Queensland context may slow concurrent biodiversity baseline survey programs across active development, rehabilitation closure (East Weipa), and new construction phases simultaneously.
QLD.workaround: Pre-schedule ecological survey windows to align with regulatory field assessment seasons; contract specialist ecological firms from Brisbane or Cairns with sufficient lead time; leverage existing Weipa operational biodiversity data to supplement new baseline surveys and reduce equipment demand peaks.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement biodiversity procedures under provincial environmental licensing frameworks and national minimum standards, but the legally unresolved interaction between the Wetlands Law and Puna salares, the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment transferring protected-area designation to financially conflicted provincial governments, extreme high-altitude operational environment (~4,000m ASL), and limited in-country availability of specialist biodiversity equipment and Puna-ecosystem ecologists create material gaps in achieving industry-leading no-net-loss and Mitigation Hierarchy compliance.
AR.specific_barrier: Legal status of Puna salares under the Wetlands Law is unresolved, meaning biodiversity offset and habitat classification decisions rest on contested legal ground; no national-level Biodiversity Offset Register or standardised offset metric exists; specialist ecologists with accredited expertise in high-altitude puna and salares ecosystems are scarce in-country.
AR.workaround: Align biodiversity procedure with IFC PS6 as the overarching standard to provide stability above the volatile provincial/national legislative interface; engage specialist puna ecosystem ecologists through international environmental consultancies with Andean experience (e.g., firms operating in Chile/Bolivia); document habitat classification decisions and their legal basis carefully given Wetlands Law uncertainty; establish direct engagement with provincial environmental agencies in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca to confirm offset acceptability criteria before committing offset designs.

CA.statement: Quebec has substantive biodiversity protection obligations under the Environment Quality Act, the Act Respecting Threatened or Vulnerable Species, and federal Species at Risk Act, with readily available specialist ecological expertise and equipment, but Bill 63's implementing regulations remaining incomplete as of March 2026, the introduction of mandatory BAPE review for new mining projects, ATI requirements for exploration work, and James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations for northern projects create procedural incompleteness and timeline uncertainty that prevents full compliance certainty at this time.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations are not yet finalised, meaning the specific biodiversity assessment and offset requirements under the reformed Mining Act regime are not yet fully defined; James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations introduce Indigenous co-management requirements for biodiversity planning that must be satisfied in addition to provincial requirements.
CA.workaround: Implement biodiversity baseline surveys and management planning now under existing EQA and federal SARA frameworks to avoid procedural delay when Bill 63 regulations are finalised; engage Cree and Inuit co-management bodies early where projects fall within JBNQA territory; monitor MELCC/MELCCFP regulatory gazette for Bill 63 regulation publication and build adaptive compliance triggers into the Biodiversity Management Plan.

MN.statement: Mongolia has a legal basis for biodiversity requirements under the Law on Environmental Protection, Law on Special Protected Areas, and EIA Law, but UNDP-assessed gaps in operational EIA procedures and national criteria, persistent weak enforcement, legislative instability from over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law, limited in-country availability of specialist biodiversity survey equipment, and government prioritisation of extraction over environmental rigour mean that industry-leading biodiversity compliance requires significant self-imposed standards above what the regulatory framework currently demands or enforces.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks standardised national criteria for biodiversity impact significance determination and no nationally recognised biodiversity offset standard or register exists; specialist ecological survey equipment (acoustic monitoring, eDNA, camera trap networks at scale) has limited in-country availability and calibration/maintenance support.
MN.workaround: Adopt IFC PS6 and the Mitigation Hierarchy as the operative standard, documented within the ESIA and ESMP, to provide an internationally credible compliance anchor above the deficient domestic framework; source specialist ecological survey equipment and accredited biodiversity consultants from international providers (e.g., via Ulaanbaatar offices of international environmental consultancies or from South Korea/Australia); establish internal biodiversity monitoring protocols with third-party verification to compensate for weak state enforcement and provide investor-grade assurance.

US.statement: The United States has a rigorous and enforceable biodiversity compliance framework under the Endangered Species Act, National Environmental Policy Act, Clean Water Act Section 404, and Arizona state equivalents, with readily available specialist ecological expertise and equipment, but the project's pre-development status means site-specific biological assessments and Section 7 ESA consultations with USFWS have not yet been finalised, sustained Indigenous opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe introduces FPIC and cultural landscape biodiversity obligations that remain unresolved, and the sacred significance of Oak Flat to affected communities creates a reputational and legal risk layer beyond standard ESA compliance.
US.specific_barrier: ESA Section 7 Biological Opinion and any associated Incidental Take Permit from USFWS have not yet been finalised for the Resolution Copper project site; the cultural and spiritual landscape values of Oak Flat (Chi'chil Biłdagoteel) asserted by the San Carlos Apache Tribe are not captured within standard ESA or NEPA biodiversity assessment frameworks, creating a gap between regulatory compliance and FPIC-aligned biodiversity stewardship.
US.workaround: Complete ESA Section 7 consultation and any required Section 404 permitting with Compensatory Mitigation Plans before operational phase; engage a specialist cultural landscape and Indigenous ecological knowledge consultant to incorporate Traditional Ecological Knowledge into biodiversity baseline and management planning; align biodiversity procedure with ICMM Position Statement on Indigenous Peoples and Minerals and IFC PS6/PS7 to address the gap between legal compliance and social licence requirements; document No Net Loss commitments in a publicly disclosed Biodiversity Management Plan to respond to environmental NGO scrutiny.

PRO-0039.procedure_title: Hazardous Materials and Non-Mineral Waste Control Procedure

GN.statement: Guinea can meet core hazardous materials and non-mineral waste control requirements through operator-led systems, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG or hazardous waste legislation means compliance must be built almost entirely on internal standards rather than a supporting regulatory framework, and selective enforcement means formal compliance provides no reliable protection.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated hazardous waste classification, storage, transport, or disposal legislation exists in Guinea — the Mining Code and Environmental Code provide no specific instrument for hazmat or non-mineral waste control; additionally, licensed hazardous waste treatment and disposal contractors with certified facilities are effectively absent in Guinea, requiring all hazardous waste to be managed on-site or exported at significant cost and logistical complexity.
GN.workaround: Operator must adopt a self-contained hazardous materials management system aligned to an international standard (e.g., IFC Performance Standard 3, ISO 14001) independent of local regulatory requirements; all hazardous waste streams requiring specialist disposal must be contractually managed through certified international or regional contractors (e.g., routed via Dakar or Abidjan); a detailed Hazardous Materials Register and site-specific waste management plan should be maintained as the primary compliance instrument; engage with Guinean authorities proactively to document voluntary compliance above the regulatory baseline to mitigate selective enforcement risk.

WA.statement: Western Australia provides a comprehensive and well-enforced regulatory framework for hazardous materials and non-mineral waste control — including the Dangerous Goods Safety Act 2004, Environmental Protection Act 1986, Contaminated Sites Act 2003, and relevant WHS Regulations — with readily available trained personnel, licensed waste contractors, and accredited hazmat equipment suppliers supporting full industry-leading compliance.

QLD.statement: Queensland provides a mature and detailed regulatory framework for hazardous materials and non-mineral waste under the Work Health and Safety Act 2011, Dangerous Goods Safety Management Act 2001, and Environmental Protection Act 1994 (Waste Management Policy), with RSHQ and DETSI oversight, and the Cape York/Weipa operational context is well-established with contractor and logistics networks capable of supporting compliant hazmat management.
QLD.specific_barrier: Equipment availability is noted as limited in the context, and the remote Cape York Peninsula location may constrain the frequency and choice of licensed hazardous waste disposal contractors available for collection runs, potentially extending on-site interim storage periods beyond preferred industry norms.
QLD.workaround: Develop a scheduled bulk hazardous waste collection and transport programme with licensed Queensland contractors (e.g., Clean Earth, Veolia, Cleanaway) using road-freight or barge logistics from Weipa; ensure on-site interim storage facilities are designed and registered to accommodate extended hold periods between collections in full compliance with EP Act storage conditions.

AR.statement: Argentina's provincial-primacy model means hazardous materials and non-mineral waste compliance obligations vary materially across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, with no guaranteed consistency of framework or enforcement; at 4,000m ASL in remote Puna locations, access to licensed hazardous waste contractors and certified disposal facilities is severely constrained, requiring significant logistical planning.
AR.specific_barrier: No certified hazardous waste treatment or disposal facilities are located within practical proximity of high-altitude Puna mining operations — the nearest registered facilities are in lowland provincial capitals or Buenos Aires, requiring hazardous waste to be transported hundreds of kilometres over difficult terrain; provincial regulatory frameworks for hazmat classification and manifest requirements differ across the three provinces relevant to lithium operations.
AR.workaround: Establish a harmonised internal hazardous materials management standard that meets or exceeds the most stringent of the three applicable provincial frameworks; contract a national licensed waste management operator (e.g., Ecosan, Aguas y Redes, or equivalent OPDS/provincial authority-registered contractor) with logistics capability to service remote highland sites; design on-site hazardous waste accumulation areas with extended interim storage capacity compliant with each province's Environmental Impact Declaration conditions; maintain a tri-province regulatory monitoring function to track divergent legislative updates.

CA.statement: Quebec has a well-developed hazardous materials and waste regulatory framework under the Environment Quality Act (Chapter IV — Hazardous Materials) and the Regulation Respecting Hazardous Materials (RHM), but Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, creating transitional uncertainty about final compliance obligations for new project phases, and James Bay/northern territory operations face additional complexity under JBNQA obligations.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63's incomplete implementing regulations mean that the precise waste classification, manifest, and disposal requirements for new mining project phases are not fully defined as of March 2026, creating a compliance planning gap for projects entering development; in remote James Bay territory, the number of licensed hazardous waste transporters and disposal facilities with practical northern logistics capability is limited, potentially constraining disposal frequency and contractor choice.
CA.workaround: Apply the existing RHM and Environment Quality Act hazardous materials regime as the operative baseline while monitoring Bill 63 regulation finalisation; engage Quebec's MELCCFP proactively to confirm transitional compliance pathway; for northern operations, pre-qualify and contract licensed hazardous waste logistics providers with demonstrated northern Quebec capability (e.g., Clean Harbors, Stericycle, or provincial equivalents) and design accumulation areas for extended interim storage between collection cycles consistent with RHM storage time limits.

MN.statement: Mongolia has relevant hazardous materials and waste provisions under the Law on Environmental Protection, Law on Waste, and associated regulations, but UNDP-assessed gaps in EIA operational procedures, persistently weak enforcement, limited availability of certified hazardous waste contractors outside Ulaanbaatar, and legislative instability from over 100 Minerals Law amendments create material compliance uncertainty for remote mine sites.
MN.specific_barrier: Certified hazardous waste treatment, transport, and disposal contractors with the capability to service remote Gobi or steppe mine sites are extremely limited outside Ulaanbaatar — no verified network of licensed regional hazmat disposal providers comparable to Australian or Canadian equivalents has been identified; Mongolia's waste regulatory framework lacks the operational procedures and national criteria that would define minimum industry-standard compliance benchmarks.
MN.workaround: Operator must implement a self-sufficient on-site hazardous materials management system aligned to IFC Performance Standard 3 and ISO 14001 as the primary compliance instrument, independent of variable local regulatory enforcement; establish contractual relationships with the limited licensed Ulaanbaatar-based waste management contractors for scheduled collection; design on-site interim storage and, where necessary, pre-treatment infrastructure (e.g., for used oils, chemical reagents) to manage waste streams that cannot be frequently removed from remote sites; maintain detailed internal hazardous materials registers and waste manifests to demonstrate compliance to international financiers and the Mongolian Environment Agency.

US.statement: The United States has a comprehensive and stringent federal and state hazardous materials and waste regulatory framework — including RCRA, CERCLA, OSHA HazCom (29 CFR 1910.1200), MSHA 30 CFR Part 56/57, DOT hazmat transport regulations, and state-level analogues — with readily available trained personnel and licensed contractors, but the Oak Flat project's pre-development status means site-specific compliance systems have not yet been established or tested, and the complex federal-state-MSHA-OSHA jurisdictional interface requires careful delineation.
US.specific_barrier: The project remains in pre-development phase with no operational RCRA waste management plan, SPCC plan, or hazmat emergency response programme formally established or tested on-site; the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires explicit written demarcation to prevent compliance gaps in hazmat handling and labelling obligations between the two regimes.
US.workaround: Develop and obtain regulatory review of site-specific RCRA Part B permit application (or applicable small-quantity generator plan), SPCC plan, and Emergency Response Plan prior to construction commencement; prepare and document a written MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary map for all facility areas to assign unambiguous regulatory ownership of hazmat compliance obligations; engage EPA Region 9 and Arizona ADEQ early to align state and federal hazardous waste classification and disposal pathway requirements; note that copper mining waste may qualify for the Bevill Amendment exclusion under RCRA but secondary processing residues will not, requiring careful stream-by-stream classification.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a Management of Change procedure at operational level, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG legislation means there is no regulatory framework mandating or defining MoC requirements, unpredictable approval timelines undermine the timely regulatory-change notifications that MoC processes depend on, and very limited equipment availability constrains the technical assessment capacity required for change risk evaluations.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated ESG or safety management system legislation in Guinea means MoC has no statutory anchor; selective and discretionary enforcement means regulatory-triggered change events (e.g. new permit conditions, revised EIA requirements) cannot be anticipated or scheduled, making the 'regulatory change' trigger within a MoC process unreliable; combined with very limited availability of specialist technical assessors needed for change risk review.
GN.workaround: Adopt a company-driven MoC procedure anchored to international standards (ICMM, ISO 45001, IFC Performance Standards) rather than local regulation; establish an internal MoC trigger list that explicitly includes political/regulatory announcements as change drivers given Guinea's political instability; maintain a retained roster of expatriate or regionally contracted technical SMEs to conduct change risk assessments where local specialist capacity is absent; build generous buffer timelines into all regulatory-change MoC workflows to account for unpredictable ministry approval periods.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading MoC requirements, supported by a mature WHS and environmental regulatory framework, readily available equipment and trained workforce, though the July 2025 DEMIRS restructure into successor agencies requires the MoC procedure to be updated to correctly identify current regulatory authorities for change notifications and approvals.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading MoC requirements under the well-developed MQSHA, Resources Safety and Health Queensland framework and EP Act regime, though the complexity of the multi-agency regulatory environment (RSHQ, DETSI, NRMMRRD) means MoC documentation must explicitly map which agency holds approval authority for each category of change to avoid coordination gaps.
QLD.specific_barrier: Multi-agency regulatory structure creates a non-trivial risk that MoC change notifications are directed to the wrong authority or that inter-agency handoffs cause compliance delays — a practical rather than structural barrier given limited equipment availability for some specialised change assessments at the remote Cape York Peninsula site.
QLD.workaround: Develop a Queensland-specific change categorisation matrix within PRO-0040 that assigns each change type to its primary regulatory approver (RSHQ for safety-critical changes, DETSI for environmental, NRMMRRD for resource tenure impacts); establish a pre-agreed regulatory liaison protocol with each agency for the Weipa operations; maintain contracted logistics capability for specialist assessor mobilisation to the remote site.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a core MoC procedure operationally, but provincial primacy over environmental licensing means that regulatory-change triggers within MoC must be managed independently across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca with no assumption of consistent requirements, and the legally unresolved interaction between the Wetlands Law and Puna salares operations means some change assessments (particularly water and land-use changes) cannot be resolved against a stable regulatory baseline.
AR.specific_barrier: Provincial jurisdictional inconsistency means a single MoC procedure cannot reference a uniform regulatory approval pathway — a change acceptable under Jujuy's provincial environmental authority may require different documentation or approvals under Catamarca's; the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment further destabilises the environmental change-assessment baseline for high-altitude operations; limited availability of qualified Puna-environment specialists at 4,000m ASL constrains on-site technical change risk assessment capacity.
AR.workaround: Develop province-specific annexes to PRO-0040 for Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca that map local approval authorities, document requirements, and known regulatory sensitivities; establish a standing legal/regulatory monitoring function to capture Glacier Law and Wetlands Law developments as automatic MoC triggers; contract specialist environmental consultants with Puna high-altitude experience for change risk assessments requiring field input; apply a precautionary hold on changes affecting water, glacier, or wetland classifications until the Wetlands Law regulatory position is clarified.

CA.statement: Quebec can implement MoC at operational level with strong foundational capability (trained workforce, readily available equipment, mature federal/provincial safety framework), but incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations means the regulatory change-approval pathway for new project phases is not yet fully defined, and the new ATI requirement for impact-causing exploration work introduces an additional permitting trigger that must be embedded in the MoC trigger list.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft/consultation as of March 2026, meaning MoC workflows that require provincial environmental approval for certain change categories cannot be fully mapped to confirmed regulatory steps; the Supreme Court's October 2023 IAA ruling creates residual uncertainty about when federal oversight re-engages alongside provincial BAPE review, complicating the regulatory authority identification step within MoC for major changes.
CA.workaround: Insert a standing regulatory monitoring checkpoint into the Quebec MoC workflow requiring quarterly review of Bill 63 regulation gazette updates and flagging any new implementing regulation as an automatic MoC trigger for procedure revision; maintain dual-track regulatory mapping (provincial BAPE and federal residual oversight) for any major physical or environmental change until the IAA jurisdictional boundary is clarified post-Supreme Court ruling; engage Quebec MRNF and MELCCFP proactively during the Bill 63 consultation period to understand likely final approval pathway requirements before they are gazetted.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a functional MoC procedure at site level, but over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law have created a confused and overlapping regulatory baseline making it difficult to determine which regulatory requirements are current, EIA framework gaps (no national criteria or clear agency procedures per UNDP assessment) undermine the environmental change-assessment component of MoC, and election-cycle approval stalls create predictable but uncontrollable delays in obtaining regulatory clearance for change events.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks operational procedures and national criteria (UNDP-assessed finding), meaning MoC workflows requiring an environmental impact determination for a proposed change cannot reference a defined regulatory methodology or approval standard; legislative instability from 100+ Minerals Law amendments means the regulatory baseline against which change impacts are assessed is unreliable; limited availability of qualified independent technical assessors in-country constrains specialist change risk review capacity.
MN.workaround: Anchor the environmental assessment component of the MoC procedure to IFC Performance Standards and ICMM guidelines rather than relying on Mongolia's undefined EIA criteria, explicitly documenting this approach in the procedure; maintain a consolidated regulatory amendment tracker updated at minimum quarterly to identify which Minerals Law provisions are currently operative; build election-cycle buffer periods into MoC approval timelines (particularly in the 6-month window around scheduled elections); retain a Ulaanbaatar-based regulatory counsel and international environmental consultant on standing engagement to support change risk assessments where local specialist capacity is insufficient.

US.statement: The United States can implement a robust MoC procedure with readily available equipment and trained workforce, but the overlapping MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities creates a documented compliance gap risk in determining which agency's change-approval or notification requirements apply to a given change event, and the evolving 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule requires the MoC procedure to explicitly treat any change affecting dust-generating activities as a triggered silica exposure reassessment event.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities — a change to processing equipment or facility layout may simultaneously engage OSHA's Process Safety Management (29 CFR 1910.119) requirements and MSHA's Part 46/47 training and equipment standards, with no clear delineation in the MoC procedure creating a gap risk; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) has not yet been operationalised in a site compliance track record, meaning MoC workflows for dust-related changes must be designed to a standard not yet field-tested at Resolution Copper's pre-development site.
US.workaround: Develop a US-specific MoC change categorisation matrix that explicitly maps each change type to MSHA, OSHA, or dual jurisdiction, with a default rule that any ambiguous change is treated as dual-jurisdiction until confirmed otherwise by legal review; embed a mandatory silica exposure reassessment trigger into all MoC workflows involving drilling, blasting, crushing, or material handling changes, referencing the 50 µg/m³ PEL as the compliance threshold; establish a pre-development relationship with the relevant MSHA district office to seek informal pre-change guidance on novel or ambiguous change categories before formal submission.

GN.statement: Guinea cannot realistically comply with an industry-leading Business Resilience and Recovery Procedure because the combination of unpredictable licence revocations (2025 mass revocations sweeping compliant operators), selective and discretionary enforcement, approval timelines of months to years, and military-governance-driven sudden policy shifts means that the foundational assumption of a stable, predictable regulatory environment — on which any credible BCP/BRR framework depends — is structurally absent.
GN.specific_barrier: No stable or predictable regulatory or legal operating environment: the 2025 mass licence revocation event demonstrated that formal compliance does not protect against sudden operational disruption by government action, making it impossible to baseline threat scenarios or define recovery objectives against an authority whose behaviour cannot be modelled; compounded by very limited in-country infrastructure and service availability to support continuity of operations during a disruption.
GN.workaround: Procedure must be substantially adapted for Guinea: (1) a sovereign/political risk module must be added as a primary threat scenario, including pre-positioned legal counsel, liaison protocols with Ministry and Presidency, and documented escalation paths; (2) recovery time objectives must be stress-tested against scenarios where regulatory approval is unavailable for 12+ months; (3) critical operational dependencies (communications, logistics, personnel evacuation) must be pre-contracted through regional hubs (e.g., Conakry or Dakar) given very limited local availability; (4) board-level political risk insurance and operational continuity reserves should be treated as mandatory controls rather than optional enhancements.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with an industry-leading Business Resilience and Recovery Procedure, supported by a mature regulatory framework, readily available equipment and services, a trained workforce, and well-established emergency management infrastructure, with only modest near-term procedural adaptation needed to account for the July 2025 DEMIRS agency restructure.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with an industry-leading Business Resilience and Recovery Procedure given its mature multi-agency regulatory framework, available trained workforce, and established emergency management systems, though the procedure should explicitly address the concurrent compliance obligations arising from East Weipa mine cessation and the Norman Creek development transition as compounding resilience risk scenarios.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with most requirements of a Business Resilience and Recovery Procedure but faces material adaptation gaps due to the unresolved legal interaction between the Glacier Law amendment and the Wetlands Law with Puna brine operations, extreme remote high-altitude operating conditions (~4,000m ASL), and provincial regulatory inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, all of which create threat scenarios that a standard BRR procedure does not address.
AR.specific_barrier: No single coherent national regulatory authority for environmental and operational continuity planning in the Puna region: provincial primacy combined with the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment transferring protected-area authority to financially conflicted provincial governments means that regulatory standing for key operational sites could be challenged or revoked without reliable federal backstop, creating a sovereign/regulatory disruption scenario analogous to — though less severe than — Guinea; additionally, extreme altitude and remoteness limits availability of recovery contractors, medical evacuation services, and replacement equipment.
AR.workaround: Procedure must be adapted to: (1) include province-specific regulatory disruption scenarios for each of Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca with differentiated response protocols; (2) pre-contract high-altitude-capable emergency medical evacuation and equipment recovery services (likely requiring Chilean or Buenos Aires-based providers given limited Puna in-country capacity); (3) build legal monitoring triggers for Glacier Law and Wetlands Law developments into the procedure's threat horizon scanning module; (4) document mutual aid and recovery support arrangements across provincial boundaries given that a regulatory shutdown in one province cannot assume reciprocal support from adjacent provinces.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most requirements of a Business Resilience and Recovery Procedure given readily available equipment, a trained workforce, and an established regulatory framework, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations, the unresolved federal-provincial assessment interface following the 2023 Supreme Court IAA ruling, and JBNQA obligations in northern project areas create regulatory uncertainty that must be explicitly built into the procedure's threat and recovery planning scenarios.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft/consultation as of March 2026, meaning the regulatory baseline against which compliance continuity and recovery objectives are set is not yet finalised; the Supreme Court's October 2023 ruling on the federal Impact Assessment Act has left the federal-provincial regulatory interface unresolved, creating a scenario where an approval obtained under one regime could be legally challenged under another, disrupting operational continuity without the operator having committed any compliance failure.
CA.workaround: Procedure should be adapted to: (1) include a regulatory change monitoring and response module that tracks Bill 63 regulation finalisation and federal IAA legislative developments, with defined triggers for procedure review; (2) ensure recovery planning for northern Quebec operations explicitly incorporates JBNQA consultation and accommodation obligations as a precondition for resuming operations after any disruption, given that a restart without renewed Indigenous engagement could trigger additional legal challenge; (3) build scenario planning for approval-in-principle being challenged or deferred due to the IAA constitutional uncertainty.

MN.statement: Mongolia can comply with most elements of a Business Resilience and Recovery Procedure but faces significant adaptation requirements due to legislative instability (100+ amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law), election-cycle permitting stalls, the 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law's non-compensated 66%+ state participation mandate for strategic deposits, and persistently weak EIA and enforcement frameworks, all of which undermine the predictable regulatory environment that a credible BRR procedure requires.
MN.specific_barrier: The 2024 Sovereign Wealth Fund Law mandating state ownership of up to 66% in strategic deposits with non-compensated participation represents a structural business continuity threat that has no established precedent in industry-standard BRR frameworks: if a project is retrospectively designated strategic, the ownership and control structure underlying the entire resilience and recovery plan could be invalidated without compensation, making it impossible to commit to recovery timelines, financing arrangements, or decision-authority chains in advance; compounded by election-cycle approval freezes that can halt recovery-critical permits for 6–12 months.
MN.workaround: Procedure must be adapted to: (1) include a sovereign ownership risk module addressing the Sovereign Wealth Fund Law's strategic designation trigger criteria, with pre-agreed escalation and negotiation protocols; (2) build extended recovery timelines into all scenarios to account for election-cycle permitting freezes (minimum 12-month regulatory approval buffer); (3) pre-position critical equipment and consumables in-country given limited local availability and long lead times; (4) define clear decision-authority chains that remain functional under partial or majority state co-ownership scenarios, including protocols for disagreements with the state shareholder during a recovery event.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most requirements of a Business Resilience and Recovery Procedure given readily available equipment, a trained workforce, and a well-defined federal and state regulatory framework, but the pre-development status of the Oak Flat/Resolution Copper project, sustained Tribal and community opposition raising unresolved FPIC concerns, and the complex MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities create specific gaps that require procedure adaptation before the site is operational.
US.specific_barrier: Sustained San Carlos Apache Tribe opposition to the Oak Flat project — grounded in the sacred significance of the site — means that the social licence dimension of business continuity and recovery cannot be resolved through technical or regulatory compliance alone: a recovery plan that does not address the risk of Tribal-led legal injunctions, congressional legislative intervention (e.g., repeal of the 2015 land exchange provision), or site access disruption due to protest action will be materially incomplete; this is a named, active, and unresolved threat to operational continuity with no analogous precedent in standard BRR frameworks for US mining operations.
US.workaround: Procedure should be adapted to: (1) include a social licence and Indigenous rights risk module as a standalone primary threat scenario, with defined engagement protocols, legal monitoring triggers (congressional activity, court filings), and response escalation paths that are separated from standard regulatory compliance pathways; (2) address MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface explicitly in the recovery procedure to ensure that surface processing facility restarts following a disruption do not inadvertently fall into a compliance gap between agencies; (3) build the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) into recovery restart checklists as a mandatory pre-return-to-operations verification item; (4) given pre-development status, establish compliance track record benchmarks now so that operational BRR testing can begin at earliest practicable opportunity before first ore.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core monitoring and measuring activities but faces significant barriers from very limited availability of calibrated instrumentation, certified metrology services, and accredited environmental monitoring laboratories, compounded by the absence of dedicated ESG legislation providing clear measurable standards against which to benchmark compliance.
GN.specific_barrier: No accredited in-country environmental or occupational monitoring laboratories; calibrated monitoring instruments (gas detectors, noise dosimeters, dust monitors, water quality analysers) are not commercially available locally and must be imported; no national metrology authority with ISO 17025-equivalent calibration services readily accessible.
GN.workaround: Source and import certified monitoring equipment with documented calibration traceability to international standards (e.g., NIST or BIPM); establish in-house calibration programme with equipment sent periodically to accredited labs in Dakar, Abidjan, or Europe; engage international environmental monitoring contractors for periodic verification campaigns; adopt ICMM and IFC Performance Standard 6 benchmarks as the measurable compliance standard in the absence of Guinea-specific ESG metrics; document all monitoring results independently to protect against politically selective enforcement.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading monitoring and measuring requirements given readily available calibrated equipment, accredited laboratories, a mature regulatory framework under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, Mine Safety and Inspection Regulations, and Environmental Protection Act 1986, and a highly trained workforce — noting that Rhodes Ridge site-specific monitoring obligations remain to be finalised through the EPA referral process.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading monitoring and measuring requirements under the well-established MQSHA, EP Act, and associated standards, with access to accredited laboratories and monitoring equipment through Brisbane and Cairns supply chains, though the remote Cape York Peninsula location introduces practical logistics for equipment calibration turnaround and third-party inspection frequency.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of specialised monitoring equipment and accredited calibration services at or near the Weipa/Cape York Peninsula site, requiring sourcing from Cairns or Brisbane with associated logistics lead times.
QLD.workaround: Establish a site-based calibration schedule with sufficient spare calibrated instrument sets to allow rotation; utilise NATA-accredited mobile laboratory services where feasible; maintain calibration register with strict recall intervals aligned to equipment manufacturer specifications and RSHQ expectations.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with most monitoring and measuring requirements but faces material barriers from limited availability of specialised high-altitude monitoring equipment locally, legally unresolved wetlands and glacier boundary definitions creating uncertainty about what must be monitored and to what standard, and inconsistent provincial oversight capacity across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca that undermines uniform compliance assurance.
AR.specific_barrier: No nationally unified monitoring standard for lithium brine operations on Puna salares; IANIGLA's glacier and periglacial monitoring protocols are in regulatory flux following the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment; accredited environmental laboratories capable of water quality, brine chemistry, and dust monitoring at approximately 4,000m ASL are not reliably available in-province, with most accredited services located in Salta city, Jujuy city, or Buenos Aires.
AR.workaround: Engage accredited laboratories in Salta or Jujuy with documented chain of custody for samples transported from high-altitude sites; apply IFC Performance Standard 3 and 6 monitoring frameworks as the measurable standard pending finalisation of provincial requirements; implement robust baseline monitoring programme now to establish pre-disturbance data ahead of regulatory clarification; engage a qualified independent environmental monitor to provide third-party verification and insulate against provincial conflict-of-interest risks created by the Glacier Law amendment.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most monitoring and measuring requirements given readily available equipment and a trained workforce, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63 implementing regulations and the unresolved federal-provincial assessment interface following the 2023 Supreme Court IAA ruling mean that the full suite of mandatory monitoring parameters, reporting formats, and submission timelines for future project phases is not yet definitively established.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft as of March 2026, meaning monitoring and reporting obligations for projects subject to the new mandatory provincial environmental assessment regime (including BAPE review) are not yet fully defined; the ATI requirement for impact-causing exploration work introduces additional monitoring trigger points whose procedural requirements are still being tested in practice.
CA.workaround: Apply existing Quebec Environment Quality Act (Title I and II) monitoring requirements as the current compliance baseline; engage proactively with MELCCFP to confirm monitoring parameters and reporting formats ahead of regulatory finalisation; design monitoring programme to IFC Performance Standard standards as a defensible ceiling that exceeds minimum provincial requirements and provides compliance buffer during the regulatory transition period; track Bill 63 regulation publication through the Gazette officielle du Québec and update monitoring plan within 90 days of final regulation coming into force.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a functional monitoring and measuring programme but faces persistent barriers from an EIA framework assessed by UNDP as lacking operational procedures and national criteria, limited availability of calibrated monitoring instruments outside Ulaanbaatar, weak enforcement creating no consistent external compliance benchmark, and legislative instability that may alter monitoring obligations without predictable notice.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework has no nationally standardised monitoring criteria or clear agency procedures per UNDP assessment, meaning there is no verified regulatory baseline against which a monitoring programme can be calibrated; specialised environmental and occupational monitoring instruments (continuous air quality monitors, noise dosimeters, water quality multi-parameter sondes) are not readily available outside Ulaanbaatar and must be imported or sourced from international suppliers with significant lead times.
MN.workaround: Adopt IFC Performance Standard 1 and 3 monitoring frameworks and World Bank EHS Guidelines as the operative compliance standard given the absence of national criteria; source calibrated monitoring equipment from international suppliers (South Korea, China, or Europe) and establish a site-based calibration programme with instruments rotated to Ulaanbaatar or international calibration facilities; engage an international environmental monitoring specialist to design and audit the programme; maintain comprehensive internal monitoring records to demonstrate good faith compliance independent of enforcement variability.

US.statement: The United States can substantially comply with monitoring and measuring requirements given readily available equipment and a trained workforce, but the Oak Flat project's pre-development status means site-specific MSHA and EPA monitoring obligations under the final Environmental Impact Statement have not yet been operationalised, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule requiring personal exposure monitoring to the new 50 µg/m³ PEL demands specific instrumentation and sampling protocols not yet tested at this site, and the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful programme design to avoid monitoring gaps.
US.specific_barrier: The 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (effective June 2024, enforcement phased) requires personal breathing zone monitoring to 50 µg/m³ PEL using NIOSH 7500/7602 or equivalent methods — programme design, instrument procurement, and laboratory accreditation arrangements have not yet been established for the Oak Flat site given its pre-development status; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary monitoring responsibilities at surface processing areas require explicit delineation to avoid compliance gaps.
US.workaround: Develop a pre-operational monitoring plan that explicitly maps MSHA jurisdiction (underground and surface mine areas) versus OSHA jurisdiction (processing facilities) with no gaps; procure IOM or CIP10 personal dust samplers with NIOSH 7500 laboratory analytical contracts with AIHA-accredited laboratories ahead of operations commencing; engage a Certified Industrial Hygienist (CIH) to design the silica monitoring programme compliant with the 2024 Final Rule; incorporate community air quality monitoring commitments into the FPIC and stakeholder engagement process to build social licence alongside regulatory compliance.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core data and records management practices but faces meaningful barriers including very limited availability of reliable digital infrastructure (internet connectivity, cloud services, enterprise document management systems), weak institutional capacity for regulatory records submission and verification, and no ESG-specific legislative framework specifying records retention or data governance obligations, creating ambiguity around what records must be kept and for how long.
GN.specific_barrier: Very limited availability of enterprise-grade document management systems, reliable cloud storage infrastructure, and broadband connectivity in-country; no ESG-specific legislative records retention requirements against which compliance can be measured; selective enforcement means records may be demanded or ignored unpredictably by authorities.
GN.workaround: Deploy offline-capable document management software with scheduled sync to cloud when connectivity is available; maintain physical (paper) records as primary backup for all critical compliance documents; define internal records retention schedules based on international best practice (e.g. ICMM, IFC Performance Standards) rather than relying on absent local legislative specificity; designate a Conakry-based records custodian with satellite connectivity to bridge remote site gaps.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a mature and well-defined regulatory framework for records management in the mining sector, including obligations under the Mining Act 1978, Environment Protection Act 1986, Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and associated codes of practice, with readily available digital infrastructure, trained personnel, and established industry practice for document control and data governance.

QLD.statement: Queensland has a comprehensive multi-agency regulatory framework with explicit records and data management obligations under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, Environmental Protection Act 1994, and associated regulations, supported by mature digital infrastructure and a trained workforce, though operators must carefully coordinate records obligations across RSHQ, DETSI, and NRMMRRD to avoid compliance gaps.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement data and records management practices meeting most industry-leading requirements, but provincial primacy means records retention obligations, environmental data submission requirements, and ESG reporting standards vary materially across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and the extreme operational environment at approximately 4,000m ASL with limited connectivity adds practical challenges to real-time data management and regulatory submission.
AR.specific_barrier: No nationally harmonised mining-sector records retention or ESG data management standard — each province (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca) may impose different records submission formats, retention periods, and reporting portals; limited reliable internet connectivity at high-altitude Puna sites makes cloud-based document management systems difficult to operate continuously.
AR.workaround: Develop a jurisdiction-matrix mapping records obligations by province and maintain compliant record sets for each relevant authority; use offline-capable document management software with periodic synchronisation at lower-altitude base facilities; engage local provincial legal counsel in each relevant province to confirm current records submission requirements and formats, given ongoing regulatory flux around Glacier Law and Wetlands Law interactions.

CA.statement: Quebec has strong foundational data and records management obligations under the Environment Quality Act, the Mining Act (as amended by Bill 63), and applicable federal requirements, but the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's implementing regulations as of March 2026 means certain records obligations for new project phases are not yet fully defined, and the evolving ATI permitting layer introduces new data and documentation requirements that are still being clarified.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft/consultation phase as of March 2026, meaning records management obligations for new mining project phases subject to mandatory provincial environmental assessment (including BAPE review) are not yet fully specified or tested in practice; the new ATI requirement effective May 2024 introduces documentation obligations whose precise scope and format remain subject to administrative interpretation.
CA.workaround: Implement records management against current confirmed obligations under the existing Environment Quality Act and Mining Act provisions while proactively tracking Bill 63 draft regulations through the 45-day consultation process; engage Quebec MRNF and MELCCFP directly to confirm current ATI documentation standards; build document management system architecture flexible enough to accommodate additional fields and retention periods as final regulations are gazetted.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement foundational data and records management practices, but persistent legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law, a weak and under-procedured EIA framework lacking clear agency procedures per UNDP assessment, limited availability of enterprise document management technology in-country, and unpredictable regulatory demands around election periods create ongoing compliance uncertainty around what records must be maintained, in what format, and for how long.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks defined operational procedures and national criteria (per UNDP assessment), meaning environmental data records and submission formats are not clearly specified; over 100 legislative amendments create ambiguity in which version of records obligations applies at any given time; limited in-country availability of enterprise-grade document management systems and specialist records management expertise.
MN.workaround: Maintain records to international best practice standards (IFC Performance Standards, ICMM) as the primary baseline independent of locally specified requirements; engage a Ulaanbaatar-based legal and regulatory monitoring service to track legislative amendments and advise on records obligation changes in near-real-time; use cloud-hosted document management platform accessible from Ulaanbaatar office with offline capability at site; conduct bi-annual internal records audits to verify alignment with current regulatory requirements.

US.statement: The United States has clearly defined and enforceable records management obligations under MSHA (30 CFR Parts 50, 56, 57), EPA, and applicable state regulations, with readily available digital infrastructure and trained personnel, but the project's pre-development status means site-specific compliance records obligations under MSHA and EPA have not yet been fully established in practice, and the complex overlapping federal-state regulatory interface requires careful mapping to ensure all records retention and reporting obligations are captured without gaps.
US.specific_barrier: Project is in pre-development phase — MSHA inspection history, EPA permit records obligations, and state-level data submission requirements are not yet operationally tested or confirmed for this specific site; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires explicit records management protocol to avoid gaps in incident and exposure data documentation; 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) introduces new air monitoring data records obligations requiring proactive system design.
US.workaround: Proactively develop a records obligation register mapping all applicable federal (MSHA, EPA, NEPA) and state requirements by operational area before first activities commence; engage MSHA district office early to confirm inspection and records submission expectations; design the document management system to capture silica exposure monitoring records in formats meeting the 2024 Final Rule requirements from day one; establish clear OSHA/MSHA boundary protocols with corresponding records ownership assignments for surface vs. underground operational areas.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a performance assessment and auditing procedure in practice, but the complete absence of dedicated ESG legislation means there is no regulatory benchmark against which audit conformance can be formally measured, selective enforcement creates unpredictability around what compliance outcomes are recognised by authorities, and limited institutional capacity means third-party audit verification and regulatory acceptance of audit findings cannot be assumed.
GN.specific_barrier: No ESG-specific regulatory framework exists against which audit performance can be formally benchmarked; selective and discretionary enforcement means audit findings may not translate to regulatory protection or predictable compliance outcomes; weak institutional capacity limits the availability of accredited local auditors competent in international standards such as ISO 14001 or ICMM frameworks.
GN.workaround: Anchor the audit procedure to international voluntary frameworks (ICMM Performance Expectations, IFC Performance Standards) rather than local regulatory requirements as the primary benchmark; engage internationally accredited third-party auditors flown in from regional hubs (e.g., Dakar, Abidjan) rather than relying on local audit capacity; document all audit findings and corrective actions meticulously to demonstrate good-faith compliance effort in the event of discretionary enforcement action; build political risk and enforcement unpredictability explicitly into the audit scope as a governance risk item.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading performance assessment and auditing requirements, supported by a mature multi-agency regulatory framework, readily available accredited auditors, strong institutional capacity, and clear legislative audit obligations under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act, Mining Act, and Environmental Protection Act, notwithstanding transitional uncertainty from the July 2025 DEMIRS restructure.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading performance assessment and auditing requirements under a mature framework encompassing RSHQ, DETSI, NRMMRRD, the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act, and the Environmental Protection Act, with readily available accredited auditors; however, the multi-agency complexity requires robust internal coordination to ensure audit scope covers all regulatory touchpoints, and concurrent rehabilitation obligations at East Weipa add audit scope complexity.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a performance assessment and auditing procedure but faces material compliance gaps due to provincial primacy creating inconsistent audit benchmarks across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, legally unresolved Wetlands Law and Glacier Law interactions creating uncertain regulatory targets for environmental performance metrics, and limited availability of internationally accredited auditors at high-altitude remote Puna operations (~4,000m ASL).
AR.specific_barrier: Provincial primacy means audit benchmarks and performance criteria differ materially across the three project provinces with no guarantee of consistency; the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment and unresolved Wetlands Law interactions mean environmental performance indicators for lithium brine operations lack a stable legal reference point; internationally accredited ESG auditors with high-altitude operational experience are not readily available locally and must be sourced externally.
AR.workaround: Develop jurisdiction-specific audit checklists for each province (Salta, Jujuy, Catamarca) reflecting their individual regulatory requirements rather than a single national standard; use IFC Performance Standards and ICMM frameworks as the stable international baseline where provincial requirements are ambiguous or in flux; engage Buenos Aires- or Santiago-based internationally accredited audit firms with Andean operational experience; include regulatory change monitoring as a standing audit agenda item given ongoing Glacier and Wetlands Law instability; factor altitude and remoteness logistics into audit scheduling and resource planning.

CA.statement: Quebec can implement a performance assessment and auditing procedure with good foundational regulatory support under the Environment Quality Act, Mining Act, and CNESST framework, but Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026 meaning the reformed audit compliance baseline is not yet fully operationalised, and the Supreme Court's October 2023 IAA ruling creates ongoing uncertainty about how federal and provincial audit obligations interact for future project phases.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations not yet finalised as of March 2026, meaning the full scope of audit obligations under the reformed Mining Act regime cannot be comprehensively defined; incomplete regulatory operationalisation creates a moving target for performance benchmarks particularly regarding mandatory BAPE review requirements and ATI permitting obligations introduced May 2024; federal-provincial jurisdictional uncertainty post-IAA ruling complicates determination of which agency's audit findings carry regulatory authority.
CA.workaround: Implement a two-layer audit structure: a stable baseline layer referencing existing EQA, CNESST, and current Mining Act requirements, and a forward-looking compliance layer that tracks Bill 63 implementing regulations as they are finalised and incorporates them progressively; engage Quebec environmental law specialists to monitor regulatory developments on a rolling basis; maintain proactive engagement with MRNF and MELCCFP to clarify audit expectations under the transitional regime; document ATI compliance as a discrete audit domain given its novelty as a permitting layer.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a performance assessment and auditing procedure but faces significant structural challenges including legislative instability from over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law creating overlapping and contradictory audit reference points, an EIA framework characterised by UNDP as lacking operational procedures and national criteria, weak enforcement meaning audit findings may not generate regulatory response, and limited availability of internationally accredited auditors in-country.
MN.specific_barrier: EIA framework lacks operational procedures and national criteria per UNDP assessment, meaning environmental performance audit benchmarks are undefined or inconsistently applied by regulators; over 100 legislative amendments to the Minerals Law create overlapping provisions that make it difficult to establish a coherent and stable regulatory audit checklist; internationally accredited auditors (e.g., ISO 14001 lead auditors, ICMM-aligned practitioners) are not readily available in Mongolia and must be sourced from regional hubs such as Beijing or Singapore; election-cycle approval stalls may delay regulatory review of audit submissions.
MN.workaround: Adopt international frameworks (IFC Performance Standards, ISO 14001, ISO 45001) as the primary audit standard given the weakness of domestic regulatory benchmarks; engage internationally accredited audit firms from regional hubs (Beijing, Singapore, or Ulaanbaatar offices of major international consultancies) for formal third-party audits; build a legislative tracking mechanism into the audit procedure to flag and incorporate relevant Minerals Law amendments as they occur; schedule formal audits to avoid known election-period regulatory stalls; document all audit outcomes and corrective actions in detail to demonstrate due diligence in the event of abrupt regulatory changes or enforcement actions.

US.statement: The United States can implement a performance assessment and auditing procedure with strong regulatory support from MSHA, EPA, and applicable state agencies, and readily available accredited auditors, but the pre-development status at Oak Flat means site-specific compliance audit baselines under MSHA and EPA have not yet been established, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule introduces new performance metrics requiring proactive audit system design, OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities must be explicitly scoped into audit coverage, and unresolved FPIC obligations and Indigenous rights issues represent a material social performance audit gap that standard regulatory audits do not capture.
US.specific_barrier: Project remains in pre-development phase meaning operational MSHA and EPA compliance audit baselines have not yet been established or tested; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) requires audit systems to be designed from the outset to capture and report against the new exposure limit; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities is a recognised compliance gap requiring explicit audit scope definition; FPIC obligations to the San Carlos Apache Tribe and other Indigenous communities are not fully satisfied, creating a social performance audit domain that falls outside standard regulatory audit frameworks but is required under IFC Performance Standard 7 and ICMM Position Statement on Indigenous Peoples.
US.workaround: Develop pre-operational audit protocols benchmarked against MSHA and EPA requirements ahead of first ore, establishing compliance baselines before operations commence; explicitly scope the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface into the audit procedure with a dedicated checklist section; incorporate the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule as a standalone audit domain with occupational exposure monitoring and reporting requirements; extend the social performance audit scope beyond regulatory compliance to include FPIC status, community grievance mechanism effectiveness, and Indigenous rights obligations under IFC PS7 and ICMM standards; engage independent social auditors with Indigenous rights expertise to assess and report on these obligations separately from regulatory compliance audits.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a management review procedure in practice, but the complete absence of ESG-specific legislation and a weak, selectively enforced regulatory framework means the procedure cannot be benchmarked against or verified by any dedicated statutory standard, reducing its external credibility and auditability.
GN.specific_barrier: No ESG-specific legislative or regulatory framework exists against which management review outputs (objectives, targets, corrective actions) can be formally anchored; selective enforcement and unpredictable approval timelines further undermine the ability to close out action items requiring regulatory interaction.
GN.workaround: Anchor the management review procedure to an internationally recognised voluntary standard (e.g. ISO 14001, ISO 45001, ICMM Performance Expectations) as a substitute compliance reference; document all review outputs with full audit trails to demonstrate good-faith compliance independent of local regulatory confirmation; build political and regulatory uncertainty scenarios explicitly into management review agenda items.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading management review requirements, with a mature legislative framework (WHS Act 2020, Mining Act 1978, EP Act 1986), readily available trained personnel, and well-established ISO management system infrastructure supporting structured management review processes.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading management review requirements under a mature multi-agency framework (MQSHA, EP Act, RSHQ oversight), with trained personnel available and established management system practice, though the procedure should explicitly address concurrent rehabilitation obligations at East Weipa and forward compliance planning for the Norman Creek and Kangwinan projects.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a management review procedure but the fragmented provincial regulatory primacy across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, combined with legally unresolved Wetlands Law and post-Glacier Law amendment uncertainty, means management review outputs cannot reliably close out regulatory compliance action items in a predictable timeframe.
AR.specific_barrier: Provincial jurisdictional inconsistency means that compliance status determinations and corrective action closure against regulatory requirements differ materially across the three provinces; the unresolved Wetlands Law interaction with Puna salares operations means a category of environmental compliance items cannot be definitively closed at management review, and the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment creates a moving target for protected-area compliance status.
AR.workaround: Structure management review agenda to treat provincial regulatory compliance as jurisdiction-specific line items rather than a unified national status; maintain a live regulatory uncertainty register as a standing agenda input; assign dedicated provincial regulatory tracking responsibility to named individuals in each province; use ISO 14001 and ISO 45001 as stable internal reference standards to provide consistency across provincial variability.
