CA.statement: Quebec can implement a management review procedure with trained personnel and readily available resources, but incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63 implementing regulations and ongoing federal-provincial jurisdictional uncertainty following the October 2023 Supreme Court ruling mean that some regulatory compliance action items generated by management review cannot be definitively closed against a stable legislative baseline.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft and subject to public consultation as of March 2026, meaning the reformed Mining Act compliance regime is not yet fully defined; this creates a category of forward-looking compliance obligations that cannot be accurately scoped or closed out in management review until regulations are finalised.
CA.workaround: Maintain a dedicated regulatory development tracker as a standing management review input item; engage Quebec MRNF and MELCCFP directly to monitor regulation finalisation timelines; document management review outputs against the current operative legislative text while flagging pending regulatory changes as open items with assigned owners and review-by dates; ensure ATI permitting obligations (effective May 2024) are explicitly covered in the review agenda for exploration-stage activities.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a management review procedure in practice, but legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the 2006 Minerals Law, a weak EIA framework lacking operational procedures, and unpredictable enforcement mean that management review outputs cannot be reliably anchored to a stable regulatory compliance baseline, and action items requiring government approval or EIA closure are subject to significant delay.
MN.specific_barrier: The EIA framework lacks national criteria and clear agency procedures per UNDP assessment, meaning environmental compliance action items generated by management review cannot be closed against a defined regulatory standard; over 100 Minerals Law amendments create a high administrative burden to maintain a current and accurate legal compliance register as a management review input.
MN.workaround: Adopt ISO 14001 and ISO 45001 as primary internal compliance reference frameworks to provide stability independent of legislative volatility; maintain a dedicated legal register with a named owner responsible for tracking Minerals Law amendments and EIA procedural updates; build election-cycle regulatory stall risk into management review forward planning; escalate EIA-related compliance items to senior management with explicit risk-acceptance documentation where regulatory closure is not achievable within review cycle.

US.statement: The United States can implement a management review procedure against a well-defined federal and state regulatory framework, but the pre-development status of the Oak Flat project, unresolved FPIC obligations with the San Carlos Apache Tribe, and the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule compliance transition mean that a significant category of social licence, human rights, and new regulatory compliance action items will remain open or contested across management review cycles.
US.specific_barrier: Sustained, unresolved Indigenous opposition and contested FPIC obligations mean that social licence and human rights compliance items cannot be closed out through standard management review corrective action processes; additionally, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) introduces new occupational hygiene compliance obligations that require proactive investment and monitoring infrastructure not yet established at a pre-development site.
US.workaround: Include a dedicated standing agenda item for Indigenous engagement and FPIC status at every management review cycle, with documented executive-level accountability; integrate MSHA Silica Rule compliance planning as a time-bound action item with milestones tied to project development phases; ensure OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary determinations for surface processing facilities are resolved and documented as a pre-operational management review output; engage specialist Indigenous rights legal counsel to advise on FPIC documentation standards for management review records.

GN.statement: Guinea can achieve baseline scaffolding compliance but faces material gaps: no dedicated scaffolding regulations exist under the Mining Code or Labour Code, certified scaffold erectors and inspectors are not commercially available in-country, and branded scaffold systems (e.g., Layher, Harsco) are very limited in availability, requiring importation from Conakry suppliers or neighbouring countries.
GN.specific_barrier: No licensed or formally certified scaffold inspectors or competent erectors available in Guinea; no dedicated scaffolding regulations under Guinean law; branded modular scaffold systems not readily available outside Conakry.
GN.workaround: Import certified scaffold systems and competent scaffold supervisors/inspectors from Europe or South Africa for initial setup and periodic inspection; establish an in-house competency programme referencing AS/NZS 4576 or BS EN 12811 as surrogate standards; document all inspections and tag scaffolds to demonstrate due diligence against international benchmark standards given absence of local regulatory equivalents.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading scaffolding requirements: the Work Health and Safety (General) Regulations 2022 (WA) prescribe scaffolding licensing tiers (basic, intermediate, advanced) under Schedule 3, licensed scaffolders are readily available in the Pilbara, scaffold equipment suppliers (e.g., Alsco, Brand Scaffolding) operate regionally, and WorkSafe WA provides clear regulatory oversight.

QLD.statement: Queensland can fully comply with industry-leading scaffolding requirements under the Work Health and Safety Regulation 2011 (Qld) and the associated scaffolding licensing framework administered by WHSQ; licensed scaffolders are available, however remote Weipa/Cape York Peninsula logistics may require advance mobilisation planning for licensed scaffold crews and equipment given limited local availability.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of licensed scaffold contractors and equipment in the Weipa/Cape York Peninsula region, requiring mobilisation from Cairns or further south.
QLD.workaround: Pre-qualify and place on retainer licensed scaffold contractors based in Cairns; maintain a minimum on-site scaffold inventory for routine work; plan scaffold-intensive maintenance windows in advance to allow contractor mobilisation and avoid operational delays.

AR.statement: Argentina has national scaffolding provisions under Resolution SRT 503/14 (MTESS) and general construction safety under Decree 911/96, but enforcement is inconsistent across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca provinces; at high-altitude remote Puna sites (~4,000m ASL), certified scaffold erectors and compliant equipment are not locally available, requiring importation from Buenos Aires or Mendoza, and altitude-related physiological factors (fatigue, reduced cognitive function) add practical compliance complexity not addressed in standard procedure.
AR.specific_barrier: No locally available certified scaffold erectors or compliant modular scaffold systems at high-altitude Puna operations (~4,000m ASL); provincial enforcement of SRT 503/14 is inconsistent and capacity to inspect scaffolding at remote sites is very limited.
AR.workaround: Mobilise certified scaffold erectors and compliant scaffold systems from Buenos Aires or Mendoza contractors; implement altitude acclimatisation requirements for scaffold erection crews as a supplementary control; establish an internal scaffold inspection and tagging register maintained by a designated competent person on-site; adapt the procedure to reference Resolution SRT 503/14 and Decree 911/96 explicitly.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with core scaffolding requirements under the Safety Code for the Construction Industry (S-2.1, r.4) administered by the CNESST, and certified scaffold erectors are available; however, for remote James Bay territory operations, mobilisation of CNESST-compliant scaffold crews and equipment to site is logistically complex, and the interaction of Bill 5 and James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations may add procedural steps for work in affected territories.
CA.specific_barrier: Remote James Bay/northern Quebec location creates significant logistical burden for mobilising CNESST-licensed scaffold contractors and compliant equipment; regulatory uncertainty from incomplete Bill 63 implementing regulations may affect how construction-phase scaffold obligations are interpreted for new project phases.
CA.workaround: Pre-qualify CNESST-licensed scaffold contractors with demonstrated remote northern Quebec experience; plan scaffold-intensive work campaigns to coincide with supply logistics windows; maintain on-site scaffold equipment inventory sufficient for routine maintenance; monitor Bill 63 implementing regulation publication and update the procedure as required.

MN.statement: Mongolia has scaffolding provisions referenced within MNS construction safety standards and the Labour Safety and Hygiene Law 2008, but there are no dedicated mining-specific scaffolding regulations, formally certified scaffold inspectors are not available in-country outside Ulaanbaatar, modular scaffold systems have limited availability outside the capital, and weak enforcement means compliance relies entirely on operator self-governance.
MN.specific_barrier: No formally certified scaffold inspectors available outside Ulaanbaatar; no dedicated scaffolding regulations under Mongolian mining law; modular scaffold systems (e.g., system scaffold) have limited availability in provincial/remote mining regions requiring Ulaanbaatar or Chinese importation.
MN.workaround: Import certified modular scaffold systems from China or procure through Ulaanbaatar suppliers; deploy an expatriate or externally trained competent scaffold supervisor to site; establish an in-house scaffold inspection and tagging regime referencing ISO 19427 or AS/NZS 4576 as surrogate standards; include scaffold competency in the site training matrix and document all inspections to maintain an auditable compliance record.

US.statement: The United States has comprehensive scaffolding regulations under OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart Q (construction) and 29 CFR 1910.28 (general industry), and scaffold equipment and competent persons are readily available nationally; however, the OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities (MSHA 30 CFR Parts 56/57 apply underground and at mine sites, OSHA applies at processing facilities) requires careful boundary management to ensure the correct regulatory scaffold standard is applied at each work location, and the pre-development project phase means site-specific scaffold plans and inspection regimes have not yet been established.
US.specific_barrier: OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing versus mine site locations creates a compliance interface risk where the incorrect scaffold standard (29 CFR 1926 Subpart Q vs 30 CFR 56/57) could be applied; site-specific scaffold plans not yet established given pre-development phase.
US.workaround: Develop a jurisdictional boundary map for the project site that explicitly assigns OSHA or MSHA scaffold standard applicability to each work area; establish site-specific scaffold plans and competent person designations prior to first construction activities; include OSHA/MSHA interface clarification in the procedure as a project-specific appendix.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core commissioning safety controls but faces material gaps in specialist commissioning personnel availability, absence of dedicated process safety or commissioning-specific regulation, and very limited local availability of safety-critical equipment such as calibrated gas detectors, pressure testing rigs, and lockout/tagout hardware required for energised system commissioning.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated commissioning safety regulatory framework exists beyond generic Mining Code provisions; specialist commissioning engineers, process safety competency assessors, and safety-critical instrumentation (e.g., calibrated PSV test equipment, LOTO station hardware, toxic gas detection systems) are not commercially available in-country and must be fully imported or expatriate-mobilised; selective enforcement context means regulatory sign-off on pre-commissioning documentation cannot be guaranteed on a predictable timeline.
GN.workaround: Mobilise an expatriate commissioning safety team with full equipment kits from regional hubs (e.g., Dakar or Abidjan); develop a standalone commissioning safety management plan that exceeds the Mining Code minimum to satisfy international lender requirements in lieu of domestic ESG instruments; build extended equipment lead times into commissioning schedule; obtain advance written confirmation from CPDM or relevant ministry on documentation acceptance to mitigate approval unpredictability.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading commissioning safety standards, with a mature WHS (Mines) regulatory framework, readily available specialist commissioning personnel and safety-critical equipment, and established industry practice for pre-commissioning, energisation, and handover protocols under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act and successor WHS Regulations.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading commissioning safety requirements under the Mines Safety and Health Act 1999, MQSHA, and associated standards, with a well-developed regulatory framework, trained workforce, and established commissioning safety practice, though the remote Cape York location of Weipa operations may require additional logistical planning for specialist personnel and equipment mobilisation.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited local availability of some specialised commissioning equipment (e.g., high-range torque calibration tools, specialist process analyser technicians) in remote Cape York Peninsula may extend mobilisation lead times.
QLD.workaround: Pre-position critical commissioning equipment and specialist personnel at site ahead of commissioning windows; establish a commissioning readiness checklist integrated with RSHQ notification requirements; coordinate multi-agency sign-off (RSHQ, DETSI) early in the commissioning planning phase to avoid concurrent approval bottlenecks.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement commissioning safety procedures at a functional level but faces significant challenges from extreme high-altitude operational environment (~4,000m ASL) affecting personnel physiological safety during commissioning activities, limited in-country availability of specialist commissioning engineers and calibrated safety instrumentation, and inconsistent provincial regulatory oversight that creates uncertainty around formal commissioning sign-off requirements across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca.
AR.specific_barrier: No national commissioning safety standard specific to mining exists; provincial regulatory capacity to review and approve commissioning documentation varies significantly and cannot be assumed consistent; high-altitude hypoxic conditions require altitude-acclimatisation protocols and modified work-rest regimes not typically embedded in standard commissioning procedures; specialist process safety and commissioning personnel with high-altitude experience are limited in-country.
AR.workaround: Supplement the standard commissioning procedure with a high-altitude physiological safety annex covering acclimatisation requirements, buddy systems, and medical fitness standards; mobilise experienced commissioning engineers from Buenos Aires or internationally; engage each relevant provincial mining authority (e.g., Secretaría de Minería of Salta/Jujuy/Catamarca) early to align on documentation and sign-off expectations; import calibrated safety instrumentation with redundancy given supply chain distances.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most commissioning safety requirements under the LSST, Règlement sur la santé et la sécurité du travail, and CNESST oversight, with readily available equipment and trained workforce, but incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63 regulations and unresolved federal-provincial jurisdictional boundaries post the 2023 IAA Supreme Court ruling introduce procedural uncertainty for commissioning-stage regulatory approvals, particularly for projects in James Bay and Northern Quebec Agreement territories.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026, meaning the precise regulatory requirements governing commissioning-phase environmental and safety approvals for new mine projects are not yet fully defined; for projects in James Bay/Northern Quebec Agreement territories, intersection with Bill 5 and Title II EQA obligations may require additional authorisations before commissioning can proceed.
CA.workaround: Engage CNESST and MRNF proactively to confirm applicable regulatory regime for commissioning phase under the transitional arrangements; build regulatory confirmation milestones into the commissioning schedule to avoid commencing energisation before all required authorisations are confirmed; monitor Bill 63 regulation finalisation and update the commissioning safety plan accordingly; for northern projects, conduct early consultation with Cree or Inuit authorities as required under JBNQA.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement commissioning safety procedures at a basic level but faces material gaps in specialist commissioning competency availability, weak and procedurally incomplete EIA and permit frameworks that create uncertainty around whether commissioning-phase regulatory approvals will be issued on schedule, limited local availability of calibrated safety instrumentation, and persistent enforcement unpredictability exacerbated by election-cycle-related approval stalls.
MN.specific_barrier: No Mongolia-specific commissioning safety regulatory standard exists; UNDP-identified absence of operational EIA procedures and national criteria means commissioning cannot rely on a predictable regulatory clearance pathway; specialist commissioning engineers, process safety competency assessors, and calibrated safety-critical instruments (e.g., gas detection, pressure relief valve test rigs, LOTO hardware) have limited in-country commercial availability; legislative instability (100+ amendments to Minerals Law) risks procedural requirements changing between commissioning planning and execution.
MN.workaround: Mobilise expatriate commissioning safety team with full equipment kits from regional hubs (e.g., Beijing or Ulaanbaatar-based international service providers); develop a commissioning safety management plan aligned to international standards (e.g., AS 61511, IEC standards) that can demonstrate compliance independent of domestic regulatory gaps; obtain advance written regulatory clearance from MRAM and relevant environmental authority before commencing pre-commissioning activities; avoid scheduling commissioning windows around Mongolian election periods to reduce approval stall risk.

US.statement: The United States can comply with commissioning safety requirements under MSHA Title 30 CFR Part 56/57 and applicable OSHA standards, with readily available specialist personnel and equipment, but the pre-development status of the Resolution Copper project (Oak Flat), unresolved MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule requiring new exposure control infrastructure during commissioning activities, and significant social licence and Indigenous rights risks create compliance complexity that prevents a straightforward Green rating.
US.specific_barrier: MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing facilities is not yet formally demarcated for this project, creating a compliance gap risk during commissioning of surface plant; the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) requires validated engineering controls and air monitoring protocols to be in place from first commissioning activity involving silica-generating processes; ongoing San Carlos Apache Tribe opposition and unresolved FPIC obligations introduce risk of injunctive legal action that could halt commissioning activities.
US.workaround: Obtain advance MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional determination for surface facilities before commissioning planning is finalised; integrate 2024 Silica Rule compliance controls (wet drilling, LEV, real-time air monitoring) into commissioning pre-mobilisation checklist; establish a legal monitoring protocol to track injunctive action risk from tribal and environmental litigants and build contingency schedule buffers; engage San Carlos Apache Tribe in a structured consultation process ahead of commissioning to reduce injunction risk and demonstrate good-faith FPIC effort.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a SIMOPS procedure under company-imposed standards, but the absence of any dedicated ESG or operational safety legislation means there is no regulatory framework specifically governing simultaneous operations, and selective enforcement means compliance value is unpredictable; the procedure must be entirely self-contained and internally enforced.
GN.specific_barrier: No regulatory instrument in Guinea specifically governs SIMOPS or simultaneous hazardous activity controls — the generic Mining Code and Environmental Code contain no provisions for operational interface risk management, meaning there is no external compliance anchor for the procedure; additionally, very limited equipment availability constrains the physical controls (exclusion zone barriers, communication systems, interlocks) that SIMOPS typically requires.
GN.workaround: The procedure must be written as a fully self-sufficient company standard without reliance on regulatory reference; physical SIMOPS controls (barriers, communication equipment, permit-to-work infrastructure) must be procured and imported rather than sourced locally; a dedicated internal SIMOPS coordinator role should be established given the absence of external regulatory oversight; all hazard interfaces must be pre-mapped and approved at the company level with documented sign-off chains that substitute for absent regulatory approval mechanisms.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading SIMOPS requirements under the Mines Safety and Inspection Act 1994, Work Health and Safety (Mines) Regulations 2022, and associated guidance, with equipment and trained personnel readily available; the shift to non-prescriptive WHS Regulations requires robust internal SIMOPS risk assessment capability but does not prevent compliance.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading SIMOPS requirements under the Mining and Quarrying Safety and Health Act 1999, its Regulations, and RSHQ guidance; the mature multi-agency framework and available workforce support full implementation, though the concurrent Weipa new development and East Weipa rehabilitation activities create a live SIMOPS interface that requires careful documented hazard management.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a SIMOPS procedure but faces material gaps: provincial primacy means SIMOPS regulatory expectations vary across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca with no uniform national operational safety standard for simultaneous activities, and the extreme high-altitude remote operational environment (approximately 4,000m ASL) severely limits the availability of specialist SIMOPS support personnel and emergency response resources needed to backstop simultaneous high-risk activities.
AR.specific_barrier: No unified national or provincial regulatory standard specifically governing SIMOPS or simultaneous hazardous operations in Argentine mining — each province applies its own industrial safety and environmental requirements inconsistently; specialist SIMOPS coordinators and emergency response teams capable of operating at 4,000m ASL with appropriate acclimatisation and high-altitude medical support are not locally available in Puna provinces and must be contracted from Buenos Aires or internationally.
AR.workaround: Procedure must be designed to meet the most stringent of the three applicable provincial frameworks and applied uniformly across all sites; specialist SIMOPS coordination personnel must be contracted from major Argentine industrial centres or internationally and rotated on FIFO arrangements with mandatory high-altitude acclimatisation protocols embedded in the SIMOPS mobilisation requirements; emergency response coverage for simultaneous operations must be pre-contracted given the absence of local emergency services capable of responding to multi-hazard simultaneous incidents at altitude.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with most SIMOPS requirements under the Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST), the Regulation Respecting Occupational Health and Safety in Mines, and CNESST enforcement, with equipment and trained personnel readily available; however, Bill 63's incomplete implementing regulations and unresolved federal-provincial assessment interaction create uncertainty around whether project-level approvals governing simultaneous major construction and operational activities will be stable, and James Bay Agreement obligations introduce procedural complexity for operations in affected northern territories.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations remain in draft consultation as of March 2026, meaning the reformed environmental assessment regime that would govern major simultaneous construction-and-operation phases is not yet fully operationalised; this creates uncertainty about whether simultaneous major project activities could trigger new BAPE review obligations mid-execution.
CA.workaround: SIMOPS procedure should be drafted conservatively to anticipate Bill 63 final regulations and include a trigger for procedural review upon regulation finalisation; legal advice specific to James Bay Agreement territory obligations should be obtained before authorising simultaneous operations in those areas; engagement with CNESST at the project planning stage to confirm regulatory expectations for the specific simultaneous activity interfaces is recommended.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a SIMOPS procedure under company standards, but the EIA framework's lack of operational procedures and clear agency mandates means there is no regulatory standard specifically addressing simultaneous operations risk management, enforcement of any operational safety interface controls is persistently weak, and limited local availability of specialist SIMOPS equipment and personnel requires external resourcing.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA and operational safety framework lacks national criteria and operational procedures for managing simultaneous hazardous activities per the UNDP assessment — no regulatory body has a clearly defined mandate to review or enforce SIMOPS-specific controls; additionally, specialist permit-to-work system administrators, SIMOPS coordinators with formal certification, and the communication and exclusion-zone equipment infrastructure required for industry-leading SIMOPS are not locally available and must be imported or contracted internationally.
MN.workaround: The SIMOPS procedure must function as a fully self-contained company standard with no reliance on regulatory prescription; specialist SIMOPS coordination capability must be resourced through international or regional contractors; a robust internal approval hierarchy for simultaneous activity authorisation must substitute for absent regulatory oversight; the procedure should include explicit contingency for regulatory process stalls around election periods that could delay any required permit amendments needed to commence or modify simultaneous operations.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most industry-leading SIMOPS requirements under MSHA regulations (30 CFR Parts 46/47/48/56/57) and applicable OSHA standards, with equipment and trained personnel readily available; however, the project's pre-development status means site-specific SIMOPS plans have not been tested under regulatory scrutiny, the MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional interface at surface processing facilities requires careful delineation in the SIMOPS procedure to avoid enforcement gaps, and the unresolved Indigenous rights and social licence context at Oak Flat creates risk that community opposition could interrupt or halt simultaneous operations through legal or direct action.
US.specific_barrier: The MSHA/OSHA jurisdictional boundary at surface processing and crushing facilities is not self-defining and requires explicit procedural delineation — failure to correctly assign regulatory authority for simultaneous activities spanning underground, surface mine, and processing areas has historically generated enforcement citations and withdrawal orders at US hard-rock operations; additionally, the 2024 MSHA Silica Final Rule (50 µg/m³ PEL) imposes monitoring and control requirements that must be integrated into SIMOPS dust exposure management when drilling, blasting, and mucking occur simultaneously.
US.workaround: The SIMOPS procedure must include an explicit jurisdictional matrix mapping each simultaneous activity location to either MSHA or OSHA authority with documented legal basis; real-time silica monitoring protocols must be embedded in the SIMOPS activity interface controls; pre-operational engagement with the relevant MSHA district office to review the draft SIMOPS framework is strongly recommended before first operations; a social licence risk register entry should be maintained within the SIMOPS procedure's scope to flag potential community action as an operational interruption trigger requiring SIMOPS suspension protocols.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core community and social performance activities but faces structural gaps: no dedicated ESG or social performance legislation exists, enforcement is selectively applied, and the absence of transparent EIA processes undermines meaningful community engagement and grievance mechanisms.
GN.specific_barrier: Complete absence of dedicated ESG or social performance legislation means there is no regulatory framework against which to benchmark community engagement, FPIC, or grievance mechanism requirements; selective enforcement and weak institutional capacity further undermine the credibility and enforceability of any community commitments made.
GN.workaround: Adopt IFC Performance Standard 1 and 5 (community engagement and land acquisition/resettlement) as the internal compliance baseline in lieu of absent national standards; establish an independently managed community grievance mechanism with documented response timelines; engage an in-country social performance specialist with Guinea-specific experience; maintain detailed records of all community engagement activities to demonstrate good-faith compliance independent of regulatory validation.

WA.statement: Western Australia has a mature and well-defined social performance regulatory framework including the Aboriginal Heritage Act 2021, native title obligations under the Native Title Act 1993, and EPA social impact assessment requirements, and the jurisdiction context confirms training and equipment availability with no structural barriers to full compliance.

QLD.statement: Queensland provides a comprehensive social performance and community engagement framework through the Environmental Protection Act 1994, Land Access Code, and associated community engagement obligations under DETSI and NRMMRRD, and despite multi-agency coordination complexity the jurisdiction context confirms no structural barriers to compliance with industry-leading social performance standards.

AR.statement: Argentina can support community and social performance activities but faces material gaps including unresolved legal uncertainty around the Glacier Law and Wetlands Law affecting Indigenous and community land rights in the Puna, provincial primacy creating inconsistent social licence and consultation standards across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca, and the April 2026 Glacier Law amendment generating active community protests and legal challenges.
AR.specific_barrier: Legally unresolved interaction between the Wetlands Law and Puna salt flat operations, combined with the Glacier Law amendment transferring protected-area designation to financially conflicted provincial governments, creates an environment where community consultation outcomes and land-use commitments may be legally challenged or overturned — undermining the durability of any social performance agreements reached.
AR.workaround: Apply IFC Performance Standard 7 (Indigenous Peoples) and PS1 (community engagement) as the binding internal standard above the provincial minimum; engage independent legal counsel in each province to map current consultation obligations; document all community engagement with timestamped evidence to withstand legal challenge; establish a multi-stakeholder advisory panel including Indigenous community representatives to build legitimacy independent of shifting provincial regulatory positions.

CA.statement: Quebec has substantive community and social performance obligations including BAPE public review processes, obligations under the James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement, and the new ATI requirement for impact-causing exploration, but Bill 63's implementing regulations remain incomplete as of March 2026 meaning the full scope of future consultation and social impact assessment obligations is not yet finalised.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63's implementing regulations are incomplete and subject to ongoing public consultation as of March 2026, meaning the specific procedural requirements for community engagement and social impact assessment under the reformed Mining Act regime are not yet operationalised — creating uncertainty about the exact compliance baseline the procedure must satisfy.
CA.workaround: Implement community engagement and social performance activities to the current established standard (EQA Title II, BAPE, JBNQA obligations) while actively monitoring Bill 63 regulation finalisation; engage Quebec-specialist regulatory counsel to provide advance notice of regulatory changes; design the community engagement framework to be modular so additional requirements can be incorporated without structural redesign when implementing regulations are gazetted.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement community and social performance activities in practice but faces significant structural challenges including legislative instability with over 100 amendments to the Minerals Law, an EIA framework lacking operational procedures and national criteria, weak enforcement of environmental and social standards, and government prioritisation of extraction over rigorous community protection — all of which undermine the predictability and enforceability of social performance commitments.
MN.specific_barrier: Mongolia's EIA framework lacks operational procedures and national criteria per UNDP assessment, meaning there is no reliable state-backed process for determining the scope of community impact assessment or consultation obligations — leaving the operator without a defined regulatory baseline against which to structure a community and social performance procedure.
MN.workaround: Adopt IFC Performance Standards 1, 4, and 7 as the operative internal compliance framework; commission a standalone Social Impact Assessment using internationally recognised methodology independent of the deficient national EIA process; establish a documented community grievance mechanism with independent oversight; engage a social performance specialist with Mongolia-specific experience to navigate election-cycle regulatory disruptions and maintain community relationships during periods of government inaction.

US.statement: The United States has a well-defined legal and regulatory framework for community engagement including NEPA public participation requirements and tribal consultation obligations under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act, but the Oak Flat project faces sustained unresolved Indigenous opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe regarding a sacred site, FPIC obligations that have not been fully satisfied despite judicial outcomes, and active opposition from major national environmental organisations — creating material social licence risk that cannot be resolved through procedural compliance alone.
US.specific_barrier: Sustained and legally unresolved opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe regarding the sacred cultural significance of Oak Flat, combined with FPIC obligations that remain substantively unsatisfied despite judicial outcomes, means that industry-leading social performance standards — which require genuine free, prior and informed consent rather than mere procedural compliance — cannot currently be met at this specific site.
US.workaround: Engage an independent FPIC specialist and Indigenous rights legal counsel to conduct a structured re-engagement process with the San Carlos Apache Tribe and other affected Indigenous communities that goes beyond legal minimum consultation requirements; commission an independent human rights impact assessment aligned with the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights; develop a transparent public reporting mechanism for community engagement outcomes; consider engagement with the US Institute for Environmental Conflict Resolution to facilitate dialogue — and suspend community engagement procedure sign-off as fully compliant until FPIC concerns are substantively addressed rather than merely procedurally documented.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement a Leadership Development Programme but faces material constraints including very limited availability of accredited local facilitators, management development institutions, and coaching professionals, requiring significant reliance on expatriate or remotely delivered content, compounded by political instability that disrupts long-term programme continuity planning.
GN.specific_barrier: No accredited local leadership development providers, business schools, or executive coaching infrastructure of international standard commercially available in Guinea; programme delivery must be imported or conducted remotely.
GN.workaround: Deliver core leadership modules via qualified expatriate facilitators or internationally accredited online platforms (e.g., Coursera for Business, LinkedIn Learning); partner with regional institutions such as the Lagos Business School or ESCA Casablanca for in-person cohort modules; adapt programme scheduling to account for political and approval-related disruption risks.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with an industry-leading Leadership Development Programme, with ready access to accredited executive education providers, coaching professionals, and established mining-sector leadership frameworks through institutions such as the University of Western Australia, Curtin University, and the Chamber of Minerals and Energy.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading Leadership Development Programme requirements, supported by strong tertiary and vocational education infrastructure, access to Queensland Resources Council leadership initiatives, and established mining-sector management development pathways, notwithstanding the remote location of Weipa which may limit in-person delivery frequency.

AR.statement: Argentina can implement a Leadership Development Programme but the extreme remoteness and high altitude of Puna operations (approx. 4,000m ASL), combined with limited local availability of internationally accredited leadership facilitators in Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca provinces, creates practical delivery constraints requiring adapted logistics and blended-learning approaches.
AR.specific_barrier: No internationally accredited leadership development providers or executive coaching professionals are commercially available within reasonable proximity to high-altitude Puna project sites in Salta, Jujuy, or Catamarca.
AR.workaround: Deliver programme modules through a blended model combining periodic in-person cohorts in provincial capitals (Salta city, San Salvador de Jujuy) with accredited online content; engage Buenos Aires-based or Chilean (Santiago) executive education institutions for residential modules; ensure programme scheduling accounts for access constraints at altitude.

CA.statement: Quebec can substantially comply with a Leadership Development Programme, but the remote James Bay/northern Quebec project context limits local access to leadership development providers, and the incomplete operationalisation of Bill 63's regulations introduces some uncertainty around Indigenous engagement and community leadership components that may be embedded in the programme.
CA.specific_barrier: Remote northern Quebec project locations have limited access to in-person accredited facilitators and executive education institutions, and evolving obligations under James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement may affect how Indigenous leadership and community engagement components of the programme are designed and delivered.
CA.workaround: Leverage Montreal and Quebec City-based institutions (HEC Montréal, Université Laval) for residential cohort modules; use accredited bilingual online platforms to maintain French-language compliance requirements; engage Indigenous community representatives and JBNQA-experienced advisors in programme design to ensure cultural appropriateness and legal alignment.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement a Leadership Development Programme at a foundational level, but limited local availability of internationally accredited leadership facilitators and executive education institutions, combined with a volatile regulatory and political environment that disrupts long-term programme planning, creates meaningful delivery constraints.
MN.specific_barrier: No internationally accredited executive education or leadership development providers of industry-leading standard are commercially available in Mongolia; the National University of Mongolia and Mongolian University of Science and Technology offer limited management development programmes that do not meet international mining-sector leadership benchmarks.
MN.workaround: Deliver internationally accredited modules through Singapore, Australian, or online providers (e.g., AIM — Australian Institute of Management, INSEAD online); conduct periodic residential cohorts in Ulaanbaatar using visiting facilitators; build programme scheduling flexibility to account for election-period disruption and regulatory uncertainty.

US.statement: The United States can comply with most Leadership Development Programme requirements given excellent access to accredited executive education, coaching, and leadership development resources nationally, but the unresolved Indigenous rights and FPIC context at Oak Flat introduces a specific obligation to embed culturally competent and Indigenous engagement leadership content into the programme that goes beyond standard industry practice and requires specialist design.
US.specific_barrier: Sustained and unresolved opposition from the San Carlos Apache Tribe and other Indigenous communities creates a specific requirement for culturally competent Indigenous engagement and human rights leadership capability within the programme that standard commercially available leadership development curricula do not adequately address.
US.workaround: Supplement standard leadership programme content with specialist Indigenous engagement and human rights modules developed in consultation with San Carlos Apache representatives and Indigenous rights experts; engage providers with demonstrated FPIC and free, prior and informed consent expertise (e.g., Māori or First Nations leadership facilitators with US context); integrate social licence risk management as a core leadership competency module.

GN.statement: Guinea can implement core explosives management controls but faces significant practical barriers: no dedicated explosives-specific ESG regulation exists beyond the generic Mining Code, explosive materials supply chains are very limited domestically, and selective/discretionary enforcement creates unpredictability around import permits, magazine licensing, and shot-firer certification approvals.
GN.specific_barrier: No dedicated explosives regulatory framework beyond generic Mining Code provisions; very limited domestic availability of specialist explosives (ANFO, emulsions, detonators) requiring importation; no established national competency standard for shot-firers or blasting engineers; unpredictable approval timelines for explosives import permits and storage magazine licensing from relevant ministries.
GN.workaround: Engage specialist international explosives contractor (e.g., Orica, Dyno Nobel, AEL) to supply product, manage in-country logistics, and provide certified blasting personnel under a service contract; establish explosives magazine to international standard (ISEE/IME) independently of local regulatory prescription; build bilateral documentation trail with Guinea's Ministry of Mines to evidence compliance even where regulatory guidance is absent; maintain contingency stockpile to buffer against import permit delays; adapt procedure to explicitly document the absence of a local prescriptive standard and apply the more stringent international standard by default.

WA.statement: Western Australia can fully comply with industry-leading explosives management requirements: the Dangerous Goods Safety Act 2004 and associated Explosives Regulations 2013 provide a comprehensive, well-enforced regulatory framework; licensed shotfirers, explosives suppliers, and specialist blasting contractors are readily available in the Pilbara; and equipment and training infrastructure are mature and locally accessible.

QLD.statement: Queensland can comply with industry-leading explosives management requirements under the Explosives Act 1999, Explosives Regulation 2017, and MQSHA 1999, with licensed suppliers and certified shot-firers available in the Weipa/Cape York Peninsula region, though the remote location requires additional logistical planning for emergency response and magazine re-supply.
QLD.specific_barrier: Limited availability of explosives supply logistics and emergency response assets at Weipa/Cape York Peninsula compared to southeast Queensland, requiring longer re-supply lead times and reduced emergency contractor response speed; this is an operational complexity rather than a structural barrier.
QLD.workaround: Maintain elevated on-site explosives inventory buffer within licensed magazine capacity to offset re-supply lead times; establish a site-specific emergency response plan that accounts for extended emergency services response times; pre-qualify a Cairns or Townsville-based licensed explosives contractor as primary supplier with defined delivery schedules; ensure all relevant site personnel hold current Queensland shotfirer licences to avoid dependency on fly-in specialists for routine operations.

AR.statement: Argentina can comply with core explosives management requirements under RENAR (Registro Nacional de Armas) federal licensing and provincial mining authority oversight, but the extreme high-altitude operating environment (~4,000m ASL) creates specific practical barriers for blasting performance and personnel safety, and provincial regulatory inconsistency across Salta, Jujuy, and Catamarca means that magazine licensing, import approvals, and shot-firer certification processes will differ materially by province.
AR.specific_barrier: High-altitude operational environment (~4,000m ASL) degrades explosive performance characteristics (detonation velocity, fume generation) requiring product and design adjustments not typically addressed in standard procedures; limited availability of altitude-qualified blasting engineers and specialist explosives technical support in the Puna region; RENAR federal import/purchase authorisations interact with provincial approvals creating a dual-approval burden with inconsistent timelines across provinces.
AR.workaround: Engage explosives supplier with documented high-altitude product formulation capability (confirmed suppliers include Orica and AEL Mining Services with Andean operational experience); adapt blast design standards within the procedure to account for altitude effects on detonation performance and toxic fume dispersion at reduced atmospheric pressure; obtain RENAR authorisations at federal level in parallel with provincial magazine licensing to reduce sequencing delays; standardise procedure requirements to the most stringent provincial standard applicable across all three provinces to avoid re-versioning; include altitude acclimatisation requirements for all blasting personnel within the procedure's health controls.

CA.statement: Quebec can comply with core explosives management requirements under the federal Explosives Act (Canada) and provincial Act Respecting Explosives (RLRQ c E-22), with licensed suppliers and trained blasting personnel available, but Bill 63's incomplete implementing regulations introduce some uncertainty around future-state authorisation requirements, and the remote James Bay territory location of relevant projects adds logistical complexity for supply, licensed personnel mobilisation, and emergency response.
CA.specific_barrier: Bill 63 implementing regulations not yet finalised as of March 2026, creating uncertainty about whether new environmental assessment triggers will affect explosives-related permit or authorisation processes for project expansions; remote James Bay territory location limits commercially available explosives re-supply frequency and extends emergency response timelines; ATI requirement effective May 2024 may interact with blasting activities during exploration phases requiring additional authorisation steps.
CA.workaround: Engage proactively with Quebec's Direction des substances explosives (DSE) under MERN to confirm that existing authorisations remain valid through the Bill 63 transition and seek written confirmation of any new obligations as regulations are finalised; pre-position explosives inventory at site to buffer against remote re-supply constraints; ensure James Bay and Northern Québec Agreement obligations are reflected in community consultation steps within the procedure for any surface blasting that could affect land users; engage a certified explosives engineer under Quebec Order of Engineers to validate blast designs, providing regulatory defensibility during the transition period.

MN.statement: Mongolia can implement core explosives management controls but faces meaningful barriers: the explosives regulatory framework under the Law on Explosive Materials (2011, with subsequent amendments) exists in principle but enforcement is persistently weak and inconsistent, licensed specialist explosives contractors and certified blasting engineers with international standard capability are limited in-country, and unpredictable permitting delays — particularly around election periods — create supply continuity risk for magazine licensing and import authorisations.
MN.specific_barrier: Limited availability of internationally certified blasting engineers and specialist explosives technical personnel in Mongolia outside of Ulaanbaatar; no domestically manufactured emulsion or ANFO bulk product supply at international quality standard — reliance on importation from China or Russia creates supply chain fragility and import permit dependency; explosives import permit approvals subject to the same unpredictable bureaucratic stalling characterising the broader regulatory environment, particularly during electoral periods.
MN.workaround: Establish a long-term service contract with an international explosives company (Orica or MaxamCorp have documented Mongolia presence) to supply product, manage import logistics, and provide certified blasting supervisors; maintain a minimum 60-day on-site explosives inventory in licensed magazine to buffer against import permit delays; supplement local personnel training with internationally accredited blasting certification programmes (ISEE Blaster Certification or equivalent) delivered in-country or regionally to build local competency; document all compliance activities to international standard independently of local regulatory prescription to protect against enforcement unpredictability; adapt procedure to include explicit escalation steps when import permits are delayed beyond defined thresholds.

US.statement: The United States (Oak Flat/Arizona context) can comply with core explosives management requirements under the federal Explosives Industry Program (ATF licensing under 18 USC Chapter 40) and MSHA 30 CFR Part 56 blasting standards, with licensed suppliers and certified blasters commercially available in Arizona, but the project's pre-development status means site-specific ATF storage licence and MSHA blasting plan approvals have not yet been established, and the unresolved Indigenous opposition and FPIC concerns create social licence risk that could disrupt access to the site for explosives delivery and blasting operations.
US.specific_barrier: Project remains in pre-development phase — ATF magazine licence, MSHA-approved blasting plan, and site-specific emergency response coordination with Pinal County have not yet been established or tested; sustained San Carlos Apache Tribe opposition and potential direct action risks could disrupt safe access corridors for explosives transport and delivery, which is a named ATF and DOT compliance requirement; OSHA/MSHA jurisdictional interface at surface facilities requires explicit delineation in the procedure to avoid regulatory gaps in explosives handling at the surface processing boundary.
US.workaround: Initiate ATF Federal Explosives Licence application and MSHA blasting plan development concurrently with project permitting to avoid sequencing delays at construction start; engage Arizona-licensed explosives contractor (multiple providers available in the Phoenix/Tucson corridor) under a pre-qualification agreement; develop a site-specific explosives transport and access protocol that explicitly addresses potential access disruption scenarios and defines contingency routes in consultation with Pinal County Sheriff and relevant federal land managers; clearly delineate MSHA versus OSHA jurisdictional boundaries within the procedure for all explosives handling activities at surface facilities; engage with San Carlos Apache Tribe representatives specifically regarding explosives transport routes as part of broader FPIC dialogue to reduce social licence risk to logistics.
